subject: ACC
title: Introduction to Accounting I
description: Introduction to managerial and financial accounting and methods used to record and report managerial and financial information to decision makers internal and external to the firm. Part I.

subject: ACC
title: Introduction to Accounting II
description: Introduction to managerial and financial accounting and methods used to record and report managerial and financial information to decision makers internal and external to the firm. Part II.

subject: ACC
title: Intermediate Financial Accounting I
description: Accounting process and the application of GAAP to the recognition and measurement of cash, receivables, inventories, property plant and equipment, depreciation and depletion, intangibles, and current liabilities.
prerequisites: ACC 200 and ACC 210, or BUS 624 (with a minimum grade of C- or better).

subject: ACC
title: Intermediate Financial Accounting II
description: Term paper and meetings with faculty advisor required.

subject: ACC
title: Directed Reading and Research
description: Reading and research of a special area in major under direction of faculty member(s). Project must include statement of objectives, outline of activities planned, results expected, and how they are to be reported and evaluated. Must be approved in advance by the department chair and faculty advisor.

subject: ACC
title: Federal Individual Income Taxation
description: Examines federal income tax concepts, such as gross income, exclusions, deductions, exemptions, and tax credits, especially for sole proprietors. Introduces taxation of property transactions. Development of professional writing skills is integral to this course.
prerequisites: ACC 200 and ACC 210, or BUS 624 (with a minimum grade of C- or better).

subject: ACC
title: Taxation of Business Entities
description: A survey of the general concepts, rules, and practices involved in the taxation of sole-proprietorships, corporations, partnerships, and subchapter S corporations.

subject: ACC
title: Accounting Information Systems
description: Accounting systems analysis, control and design in manual and computerized environments. Knowledge and skills of information technology for auditing AIS systems. Hands-on experience with microcomputers and a computerized accounting system.
prerequisites: ACC 323 (with C- or better or concurrent) and BUS 311 (with C or better).

subject: ACC
title: Law for the Accountant
description: Intensive study of areas of law of importance to accountants. Particular attention is given to principles of law relating to contracts, sales, commercial paper, secured transactions, property, legal entities, agency, securities, and accountant's legal liability.

subject: ACC
title: Accounting Internship
description: On-the-job experience in the accounting community.

subject: ACC
title: Advanced Financial Accounting
description: Accounting topics relating to consolidation requirements and introduction to the fundamentals of fund accounting, including the general fund, restricted funds, debt service funds, enterprise funds, general long-term account group, general fixed assets accounting group, and accounting entries for encumbrances.

subject: ACC
title: Special Topics in Accounting
description: Addresses current issues impacting the accounting profession. Topics vary each semester.

subject: ACC
title: Auditing
description: Auditing concepts including standards, objectives and ethics for external auditors. Emphasis on reporting standards, internal control, evidence, statistical sampling, IT audits and assurance. Development of professional writing skills is an integral part of this course.
prerequisites: ACC 323 and ACC 409, both with C- or better.

subject: ACC
title: Forensic Accounting
description: Theoretical and real life aspects of forensic accounting. Includes discussions of the legal environment, types of forensic engagements such as, lost earnings, business valuations, fraud, and real world forensic cases.
repeatable: 1 time, but credit earned one time only.

subject: ACC
title: (Alpha) Accounting Capstone
description: Integration of numerous elements of the accounting program. Current accounting issues discussed. Lectures, discussions, case analysis. (B) managerial-taxethics; emphasis on instruction in writing; (C) financialauditing-accounting information systems. ACC 465B - Accounting Capstone: Managerial Accounting-Tax-Ethics - Credits: 2 Integration of numerous elements of the accounting program. Current accounting issues discussed. Lectures, discussions, case analysis. (B) managerial-taxethics; emphasis on instruction in writing; (C) financialauditing-accounting information systems.
prerequisites: ACC 401 (or concurrent) and ACC 418 (or concurrent) for (B); ACC 418 (or concurrent) for (C).

subject: ACC
title: CPA Review-Audit and Attestation
description: Auditing and Attestation (AUD) section covers the entire auditing process, including auditing procedures, generally accepted auditing standards, standards related to attest engagements, and the AICPA Code of Professional Conduct. governmental and other entities.

subject: ACC
title: CPA Review-The Regulation
description: The Regulation section focuses on federal taxation, especially taxation of business entities; individuals, and property taxation, but also tests on business law, business ethics, and professional and legal responsibilities.

subject: ACC
title: International Corporate Governance
description: Understanding of complex and critical issues of international corporate governance, financial reporting, and ethical conduct. Includes corporate governance in the U.S., major European markets, and Asia. Emphasis on internal and external stakeholders, regulators, and gatekeepers.

subject: ACC
title: Accounting Theory and Development
description: History and theoretical background of accounting standards. Including accounting theories, formulating and testing theories; scientific, pragmatic, syntactic and semantic theories; normative and positive theories. Literature supportive and critical of accounting theories and standards.
prerequisites: ACC 323 with C- or better, or consent.

subject: ACC
title: Information and Assurance and Analytics
description: Focus on auditing processes, standards, and guidance specific to IT risks. Exposure to advanced IT audit software and its practical application and real world IT audit issues.
prerequisites: ACC 418 with C- or better, or consent.

subject: ACC
title: Global Accounting
description: Theory and fundamental causes of international variations in accounting. Special emphasis on problems such variations create for financial reporting, control, and decision-making within multinational business enterprises.
prerequisites: ACC 323 with C- or better, or consent.

subject: ACC
title: Accounting and Tax Research
description: In-depth examination of tax and accounting research, IRC, and SEC procedures. Extensive practice in issue identification, reading and analyzing primary authority, and communicating results. Business Administration students only.
prerequisites: ACC 460E (or concurrent) with C- or better, or consent.

subject: ACC
title: Tax of Partners/Partnerships
description: Examines advanced topics in federal taxation of partners and partnerships regarding the contribution, operation, and distribution from partnerships and transfers of partnership interests.
prerequisites: ACC 407 with C- or better, or consent.

subject: ACC
title: Advanced Public Sector Accounting
description: Provides the tools necessary for understanding the principles of fiscal accountability and reporting in governmental and not-for-profit organizations.
prerequisites: ACC 415 with C- or better, or consent.

subject: ACC
title: Estate and Gift Taxation and Planning
description: Examines estate and gift tax provisions and basic estate planning techniques to save taxes and avoid probate. Overviews generation-skipping transfer taxes and income taxes on estates and trusts.
prerequisites: ACC 401 with C- or better, or consent.

subject: ACC
title: Multijurisdictional Taxation
description: Examines international, state, and local tax issues. Topics include U.S. International taxation of in- and outbound transactions, sourcing of income and deductions and nexus.

subject: ACC
title: Financial Statements Analysis
description: Analyses of a firm's profitability, liquidity, and solvency using ratios and common size financial statements. Students are expected to find strengths and weaknesses of the firm based on their analysis.

subject: ACC
title: Data Analytics for Accountants
description: Introduction to visual analytics. Tableau and Power BI for Excel. Introduction to data processing and blending, data visualization and other visualization techniques. Introduction to PowerQuery, PowerPivot, PowerView, and PowerMap.

subject: ACC
title: Analysis and Decision-Making
description: Integrates learning through analysis and communication of comprehensive business problems. Stresses research, critical thinking, and analytical and communication skills applied to contemporary accounting and tax issues.
prerequisites: ACC 625 with C- or better (or concurrent), no waiver.

subject: ACC
title: Current Topics in Accounting
description: Concentration on current issues impacting the accounting profession. Topics vary each semester.

subject: ACC
title: Accounting Internship
description: On-the-job experience in the accounting community. Necessary evaluation reports and meetings with faculty advisor required.

subject: ACC
title: Thesis Research

prerequisites: MAcc student and School of Accountancy Director approval.

subject: ACC

subject: ACC

subject: ACC

Students with prior experience in ASL are required to take a placement test (email: linguist@hawaii.edu). The placement test is required even if you have had previous ASL courses approved for transfer credits from another institution. The placement exam is strongly recommended if you are interested in taking advanced courses (300-level) and there has been a gap since your last language class, or if you would like a firmer sense of your proper placement. The 101 course is intended for students with no prior experience in the language. If your language proficiency is judged to be beyond the 101-level on the first day of class, you will be placed in a higher-level course. Students who are placed 102 or a higher level may complete the language requirement faster and also earn back-credits (up to 12 credits, which can be counted toward graduation. Restrictions apply: see the Manoa Back Credit Policy at https://manoa.hawaii.edu/ovpae/academic-policies/5403/). Students starting from 101 will receive no backcredits. A placement adjustment made in the first week of class does not guarantee a seat in the course appropriate for the student.

subject: AMST

subject: AMST

subject: AMST

subject: AMST

subject: AMST

subject: AMST

subject: AMST

subject: AMST

subject: AMST

description: Overview of museum education including museum learning theories, informal learning programs, audience research, national and international policies and reports, and community projects.

subject: AMST

subject: ANSC

subject: ANSC

subject: ANSC

Readings include academic writing and also literary nonfiction and journalism.

grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: ASAN

Includes analysis of: Orientalizing artistic traditions throughout history, history and concept of Orient, post-colonial critique of Orientalism. description: Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

Dynamics of planets, satellite systems, asteroids and comets; planetary atmospheres and internal structure; thermal balance; the Sun as a star. Introduces numerical computing. Principles and techniques of optical and near-infrared astronomical observation. Astronomical coordinate systems. Telescopes, cameras, spectrographs, and detectors. Astrometry, photometry, and spectroscopy of astronomical objects.
prerequisites: ASTR 210 or ASTR 242; PHYS 152 or PHYS 274; MATH 216, MATH 242, or MATH 252A.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: BOT

subject: BOT

prerequisites: BOT 456 or MICR 485; and BIOL 375; or consent.

prerequisites: one of BOT 456, NREM 680, PEPS 671, ZOOL 620, ZOOL 623; or consent.

Recommended: students have working knowledge of calculus. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: fall

subject: BOT

subject: BOT

description: Verbal categories, aspects, focus devices, resultative and directional compounds, coverbial constructions. Interaction between syntax and semantics. preparing and presenting lessons with materials based on comparative linguistic analysis. Materials, teaching aids, test construction.

subject: CHN
title: Practicum: Teaching Chinese Language
description: For graduate students pursuing teaching Chinese language. Students gain practical skills and hands-on experiences in creating instructional and assessment materials and teaching an actual Chinese language class using the self-developed materials effectively.
prerequisites: CHN 643 or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: fall

subject: CHN
title: (Alpha) Topics in Chinese Language
description: Extensive studies of selected topics (B) teaching and testing: specific problems in teaching Chinese including characters and cultural elements; proficiency and communicative ability; (C) cognitive grammar. CHN 643 - Methods in Teaching Chinese as Second Language - Credits: 3
grade_option: A-F only for (C). Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year.

subject: CHN
title: Current Topics in Chinese Grammar
description: issues and debates, focusing on the papers published by leading Chinese linguists employing these approaches. CHN 750D - Research Seminar in Chinese Language: classical grammar - Credits: 3
prerequisites: CHN 452, CHN 455, or CHN 456; or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: CHN
title: Second Semester Classical Chinese
description: Builds on the foundation laid in CHN 461; introduces complex syntactic patterns, advanced vocabulary; teaches sophisticated reading strategies and cultural literary contexts; exposes students to a wide range of intermediate level texts.
repeatable: 2 times Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: CHN
title: Advanced Classical Chinese
description: (B) teaching methods; (C) structure; (D) classical grammar; (E) sociolinguistics. CHN 750 - (Alpha) Research Seminar in Chinese Language - Credits: 3 CHN 753M - Research Seminar in Chinese Literature: modern - Credits: 3 (B) teaching methods; (C) structure; (D) classical grammar; (E) sociolinguistics. Study of authors, a genre, a period, or a problem. (M) modern; (T) traditional.
prerequisites: EALL 611, WGSS 613, WGSS 615, or WGSS 650; or consent for (M); CHN 612, or consent for (T).

subject: CHN
title: Directed Research
description: CHN 750B - Research Seminar in Chinese Language: teaching methods - Credits: 3 (B) teaching methods; (C) structure; (D) classical grammar; (E) sociolinguistics. CHN 753T - Research Seminar in Chinese Literature: traditional - Credits: 3 Study of authors, a genre, a period, or a problem. (M) modern; (T) traditional. School of Cinematic Arts A grade of C or better in the prerequisite courses is required for continuation, unless otherwise specified. CINE 210 - Introduction to Cinematic Digital Production - Credits: 3 Introduction to the basic techniques of cinematic digital production and allows students to explore their personal voice in this process.
prerequisites: EALL 611, WGSS 613, WGSS 615, or WGSS 650; or consent for (M); CHN 612, or consent for (T).

subject: CINE
title: Introduction to 3D Animation
description: A basic overview of the 3D animation production process, including modeling, texturing, rigging, animation, lighting, and rendering.

subject: CINE
title: Introduction to 2D Animation
description: Introduction to traditional styles and methods of hand drawn 2D, digital, and stop motion animation through theory and practice.

subject: CINE
title: Introduction to Cinema and Digital Media
description: Introduction to the study of cinema: history, aesthetics, and cultural impact.

subject: CINE
title: Cinematic Narrative Production
description: Production-intensive course with collaborative as well as individual projects. Theories and application of basic digital cinema productions, including camera, lighting, sound, and editing.

subject: CINE
title: Cinematography
description: Comprehensive course in visual styles supporting screen narratives through a study of principles of camera elements, operations, lighting, color and composition. Professional role and responsibilities of cinematographer. Project-oriented.

subject: CINE
title: Experimental Art and Animation
description: Provides students an opportunity to experiment with new mediums while collaborating with artists from different backgrounds, such as art, theatre, dance, film, and animation.
prerequisites: CINE 216 (or concurrent) or ART 113 or CINE 353/DNCE 353/THEA 353 (or concurrent) or CINE 356/DNCE 356/THEA 356 (or concurrent).
major_restrictions: SCA, ART, THEA, DNCE, THDN majors only.

subject: CINE
title: 3D Modeling
description: Essential principles of 3D modeling in order to create both organic character models and hard surface models. Students will create high-quality, animation-ready 3D models with textures and poses.

subject: CINE
title: 3D Animation
description: Creating the illusion of life through the principles of 3D animation.

subject: CINE
title: 3D Lighting, Camera, and Rendering
description: and color within a virtual 3D space. Rendering and cinematography for 3D animation.

subject: CINE
title: Storyboarding and Animatics
description: Exposes students to the history, application, format, styles, and methods of creating storyboards and animatics. Visual storytelling will be analyzed by examining the foundational components of the visual language of a film.

subject: CINE
title: Visual Development for Animation
description: Visual design and development for animated characters and environments. Students will create concept art, character and prop designs, movement styles, backgrounds, and layouts for animation pre-production.

subject: CINE
title: Movement Study for Animation
description: Creating the illusion of life and expressive movement through the principles of 2D animation: timing, spacing, physics, staging, anatomy, attitude, and acting.

subject: CINE
title: Motion Graphics
description: A project-based introduction to motion graphics using Adobe After Effects.

subject: CINE
title: Independent Producing
description: proposal writing, script breakdowns, budgeting, scheduling, legal issues, festival strategy, and distribution.
prerequisites: CINE 255; and one of CINE 215, CINE 216, or CINE 310.

subject: CINE
title: Screenwriting
description: Introduction to the basics of writing a short narrative screenplay for film or animation. Students learn the fundamentals and format of screenwriting as well as basic elements of storytelling and character development.

subject: CINE
title: Screening Asian Americans
description: Survey of Asian and Asian American representations in American film and television from the silent era to the present, with an emphasis on Orientalism and multiculturalism, as well as performance and spectatorship.

subject: CINE
title: Scenic I: Beginning Scenic Design
description: Workshop introducing the basic principles and approaches of scenic design for theatre and dance, with emphasis on the creative process.
prerequisites: a course in THEA or DNCE, production experience, or consent. (Consent required for production experience option).

subject: CINE
title: Oral Tradition to Screenplay
description: Adapting the stories, styles, and cultural values of oral tradition storytelling to cinematic narratives.

subject: CINE
title: Costumes I: Beginning Costume Design
description: Basic principles and approaches to costume design for theatre and dance. Visual communication methods, creative process, historical research, and organizational practices.

subject: CINE
title: Directing the Camera for the Screen

subject: CINE
title: Indigenous Aesthetics
description: Aesthetic theories and practices of indigenous cultures of the Pacific and their adaptation to the screen in cinematic storytelling.

subject: CINE
title: Directing the Actor on Screen
description: Introduction of the screen-director to the craft of acting for the camera. Students will develop communication skills and learn practical techniques to elicit spontaneous and relaxed performances with actors from the Theatre program.
prerequisites: CINE 215, CINE 216, or CINE 310; and CINE 350 (or concurrent).

subject: CINE
title: Editing for Cinema
description: Advanced course examining the theory, techniques, and practices of motion picture editing; use of non-linear digital editing systems; and practical experience in digital editing projects.

subject: CINE
title: Post-Production Sound
description: Practical course on the theory, art, and techniques of sound recording, editing, and design for cinema. Students work on projects involving dialogue and sound effects in post-production. Detailed analysis of cinematic grammar, placement, movement, focus, and effects of the camera to create the mise-en-scene. Practical exercises to apply theory to individual creative work and in collaboration with actors from the Theatre program.
repeatable: Repeatable one time on different topics.

subject: CINE
title: Documentary Studies
description: In-depth study of the nature, history, and impact of documentary filmmaking traditions around the world, focusing on the interplay between filmmaker, subject, and audience.

subject: CINE
title: Authors in Cinematic Arts
description: In-depth study of the auteur theory and specific application to the authors in cinematic arts, such as film directors, animators, or screenwriters.
repeatable: Repeatable one time on different topics.

subject: CINE
title: Study Abroad
description: Intensive study of selected topics, genres, filmmakers, or digital media production in the host country in a UH Mānoa-approved study abroad location.

subject: CINE
title: Topics in Cinematic Arts
description: CINE 374. Topics of study and critical inquiry in a variety of areas in the cinematic arts. Taught by regular and visiting faculty.

subject: CINE
title: Advanced Cinematic Production
description: Production of a major cinematic/digital narrative project. Working in groups, each student takes on creative and technical role and responsibilities of a principal crew position. Emphasis on artistic form in narrative development; timely execution from pre- to postproduction.

subject: CINE
title: Techniques in Cinematic Arts
description: Specialized techniques in the creation of digital media: taught by regular and visiting faculty.
prerequisites: CINE 310, CINE 350, and one of CINE 312, CINE 330, CINE 370, CINE 372, or CINE 374.

subject: CINE
title: Advanced Cinematography
description: Applies the basic foundations, techniques, and theory of cinematography (covered in CINE 312) to a more informed and crafted practice with Camera and Lighting Scene study workshops, and research exercises and film projects.

subject: CINE
title: Animation Production I
description: Participation in a group research or creative project under supervision of a faculty member. Only six credits of CINE 399/CINE 499 in any combination can be applied to meet requirements for the major. Students work independently to produce a short, animated film. Emphasis on visual storytelling and character animation.

subject: CINE
title: Workshop in Cinematic Arts
prerequisites: CINE 350 and consent; and one of the following: CINE 310, CINE 315, CINE 316, CINE 317, CINE 321, CINE 322, CINE 323, or CINE 330.

subject: CINE
title: Documentary Production
description: Analysis and practical knowledge of the documentary process including, but not limited to, research, organization and story structure, shooting, camera coverage, and editing.
prerequisites: any CINE 215 or ICS 110 (Alpha) or ICS 111.
gen_ed: DA CINE 419 - Virtual and Augmented Reality Programming - Credits: 3 Students will learn to develop virtual reality and augmented reality applications with turnkey tools as well as through programming. Prior programming experience is not required for this course.

subject: CINE
title: Screenwriting Capstone
description: Culmination of beginning and advanced screenwriting classes. Students will write a treatment and first act for a feature-length project or pilot script and series bible for a television project.
prerequisites: CINE 317 or (CINE 321, CINE 322, and CINE 323); and CINE 350.

subject: CINE
title: Advanced Screenwriting
description: Application of narrative principles of character development, story structure, and thematic spine to screenplays for live action and animated short films, television, or transmedia projects. Emphasis on writing instruction.

subject: CINE
title: Indigenous Filmmaking
description: Theories and studies of indigenous films and creation of a cinematic project based in indigenous cultural and value systems.
prerequisites: CINE 310, CINE 350, and one of the following; CINE 312, CINE 330, CINE 370, CINE 372, or CINE 374.

subject: CINE
title: Ethics and Film
description: Ethical theory and dilemmas as reflected in film and filmmaking. Social responsibility for filmmakers.

subject: CINE
title: Directing the Motion Picture
description: Students direct a narrative live-action short film from pre- through post-production, learning how to develop a directorial vision and how to implement it through storyboarding, scheduling, and collaborative skill sets.

subject: CINE
title: Oceanic Media and Culture
description: Involves close textual analysis of film, TV and multimedia content. The course includes cinematic and television screenings. Junior standing or higher.

subject: CINE
title: Data Visualization
description: Introduction to data visualization through practical techniques for turning data into images to produce insight. Topics include: information visualization, geospatial visualization, scientific visualization, social network visualization, and medical visualization. Junior standing or higher.

subject: CINE
title: Seminar in Cinematic Arts
description: Intellectual issues in cinematic arts. Conducted by regular and visiting faculty with extensive student participation and scholarly presentation.
prerequisites: CINE 255 and junior standing, or consent.
repeatable: Repeatable one time on different topics.

subject: CINE
title: Capstone Cinematic Production
description: Emphasis on advanced production skills in creating a capstone project in live-action fiction film, animation, documentary, or new media. Screenwriting students may also develop a feature screenplay or television pilot and series bible.
prerequisites: CINE 405 or CINE 410 or CINE 420 or CINE 455 or CINE 475.

subject: CINE
title: Video Game Design and Development
description: Students will team design, build, and demonstrate video games or related interactive entertainment environments and applications. Topics will include emerging computer science techniques relevant to the development of these types of environments. Junior standing or higher.
prerequisites: any CINE 215 or ICS 110(Alpha) or ICS 111.

subject: CINE
title: Global Media
description: Close textual analysis and strategic analysis of global media dynamics with an emphasis on transnational media corporations.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only.

subject: CINE
title: Cinematic Arts Internship
description: Internship in professional cinematic, television, animation and/or digital media production company under professional and faculty supervision.
prerequisites: CINE 350 and consent; and one of the following: CINE 310, CINE 315, CINE 316, CINE 317, CINE 321, CINE 322, CINE 323, or CINE 330.

subject: CINE
title: Directed Study
description: Independent research or creative project under the supervision of a faculty member. Only six credits of CINE 399/499 in any combination can be applied to meet requirements for the major. Civil, Environmental and Construction Engineering Preference in registration is given to declared engineering majors. Minimum grade for prerequisite courses is D- except PHYS 170, CEE 270, and CEE 370, which require a C or better grade, unless otherwise specified.
prerequisites: CINE 350 and consent, and one of CINE 310, CINE 315, CINE 316, CINE 317, CINE 321, CINE 322, CINE 323, CINE 330.

subject: CEE
title: Surveying/Geodesy/Geomatics
description: Plane surveying for cadastral, engineering, and construction applications including taping, leveling, traverses, bearings and azimuths, areas and volumes, EDM. Geodesy, map projections, satellite geodesy (GPS), geodetic surveying and photogrammetry. Field lab in notes and procedures.

subject: CEE
title: CAD/BIM/3D Visualization
description: Computer-aided drafting (CAD) tools using AutoCAD and Revit. AutoCAD interface, titleblocks, model/paper space, viewports, texts and annotation, dimensioning. Basics of BIM, toolbox, modeling techniques, annotating, dimensioning. CAD drafting to real-world engineering applications.

subject: CEE
title: Applied Mechanics I
description: Forces, resultants, and equilibrium; analysis of trusses, frames, and machines; centroids, moments of inertia; friction.
prerequisites: grade of C or better in all of the following: PHYS 170; MATH 242 or MATH 252A; and either MATH 243 (or concurrent) or MATH 253A (or concurrent).

subject: CEE
title: Applied Mechanics II
description: Dynamics of particles and rigid bodies; force, acceleration, impulse-momentum, work-energy.
prerequisites: C or better in CEE 270; MATH 244 (or concurrent) or MATH 253A (or concurrent).
major_restrictions: CE, CNST, ENGS, ME majors only.

subject: CEE
title: Applied Probability and Statistics
description: Description of sample data; correlation and regression; probability and statistical distributions; estimations of population parameters; fitting distributions to histograms; hypothesis testing.

subject: CEE
title: Fluid Mechanics Fundamentals
description: (3 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Compressible and incompressible fluid properties; fluid statics; kinematics, energy and momentum considerations in steady flows; application of steady flow concepts to various fluid processes; with an emphasis on instruction in writing lab reports.

subject: CEE
title: Environmental Engineering
description: (3 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Parameters and indices of environmental quality; materials balances; chemical kinetics; ideal reactor models; water and air pollution control; solid and hazardous waste management; emphasis on instruction in writing lab reports.

subject: CEE
title: Mechanics of Solids
description: Introduction to analysis and design of deformable bodies subject to loading. Stress, strain, constitutive relation, axially loading, torsion, statically indeterminate systems, Mohr's circle, failure criteria, buckling, defection due to axial, shear, torsional, and flexural loading.
prerequisites: C or better in all of the following: CEE 270; and MATH 244 (or concurrent) or MATH 253A (or concurrent).

subject: CEE
title: Mechanics of Solids Lab
description: Tension, compression, and torsion of bars, and bending of beams. Emphasis on writing lab reports.

subject: CEE
title: Construction Materials
description: (2 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Concrete properties, role of aggregates, concrete mix design, supplementary cementitious materials, chemical admixtures, behavior of concrete, concrete testing, field practices. Emphasis on writing instruction. (3 Lec, 1 3-hr Lab) Introduction to geotechnical engineering: soil characterization, index properties, seepage and flow in soil, stresses in soils, compressibility, consolidation, shear strength. Substantial emphasis on writing lab reports.
prerequisites: CEE 305 and CEE 370 with a minimum grade of C or better.

subject: CEE
title: Structural Analysis
prerequisites: CEE 370 with a minimum grade of C or better.

subject: CEE
title: Engineering Economics
description: Economic analysis in engineering and management decision-making, interest, depreciation, income tax, cost classification, break-even analysis, economic comparisons of alternatives, benefit-cost analysis. Analysis of statically determinate plane and space trusses and frames; deflections; introduction to matrix methods; computer applications.
prerequisites: ECON 120 (with a minimum grade of C-) or ECON 130 (with a minimum grade of C-), and senior standing.
major_restrictions: BENG, CE, CEM, CNST, EE, and ME majors only.

subject: CEE
title: Computer Methods in Engineering Systems
description: Numerical solutions of engineering problems using digital computers. Regression analysis; numerical differentiation and integration; solutions of algebraic, transcendental, and differential equations; and analysis of large structural systems.

subject: CEE
title: Engineering Hydraulics
description: Hydraulics of closed conduits and open channels with emphasis on engineering applications. Topics also include pump hydraulics, bridge hydraulics, urban drainage engineering, and flood plain management.
prerequisites: CEE 320 with a minimum grade of C or better.

subject: CEE
title: Environmental Fluid Mechanics
description: Introduction to basic concepts of pollutant transport phenomenon through theoretical modeling, lab and field experiments and observations. Specific topics include mass balance, jets and plumes, mixing and transport in rivers, reservoirs, groundwater and estuaries; non point course pollution.
prerequisites: CEE 320, and MATH 244 or MATH 253A (or equivalent).
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: CEE
title: Applied Hydrology
description: Surface water hydrology topics include hydrologic cycle, hydrographs, regression methods, urban rain-runoff process, flood frequency analysis, flood routing and hydrology for detention basin design. Groundwater hydrology topics include seawater intrusion, theory of groundwater flow and solutions to steady and unsteady aquifer flows.
prerequisites: CEE 305 and CEE 320 with a minimum grade of C or better.

subject: CEE
title: Water and Wastewater Engineering
description: Hydrologic fundamentals of water demand and supply; water and wastewater distribution; collection systems; quality characterization; analytical methods for water quality management.
gen_ed: DP CEE 440 - Vulnerability & Adaptation on Coastal Infrastructure - Credits: 3 Assessing vulnerability of coastal communities to climate change stressors and providing technical engineering solutions for adaptation. Senior standing or higher. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: CEE
title: Principles of Sustainability Analysis
description: Key principles of sustainability and its analysis. Quantification of environmental impact/assessment using target plots, mass/energy balances, and life cycle analyses (cradle to gate/grave) applied to products, processes, or systems. Use of SimaPro. Junior standing or higher.
grade_option: A-F only. Senior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: CEE
title: Geotechnical Engineering II
description: Continuation of CEE 355 field exploration, lateral earth pressures, bearing capacity theory, slope stability, use of geosynthetics.

subject: CEE
title: Pavement Engineering
description: (2 Lec, 1 3-hr Lab) Design principles of flexible and rigid pavements; HMA mixture design, equipment and construction; and application of life cycle cost analysis (LCCA) in pavement engineering. Includes laboratory sessions for aggregate testing and Superpave mix design.
prerequisites: CEE 355 (or concurrent with a minimum grade of D-), CEE 375 (or concurrent with a minimum grade of D-), and CEE 361 (with a minimum grade of C or better).

subject: CEE
title: Traffic Engineering
description: Design/analysis of signalized, unsignalized intersections, urban networks. Traffic impact studies; analysis steps and applications. Design/redesign options. Parking studies: demand, alternative designs (lot layouts).
prerequisites: CEE 361 with a minimum grade of C or better.

subject: CEE
title: Urban and Regional Transportation Planning
description: Application of travel demand forecasting models to transportation planning. Evaluation and decisionmaking. Term projects.
prerequisites: CEE 361 with a minimum grade of C or better.

subject: CEE
title: Construction Methods
description: Methods of construction, primarily buildings. Construction types: light and heavy wood; steel; plain, reinforced, and prestressed concrete; masonry. Foundations; associated details of frames, walls, roofs, floors, openings, finishes. Disasters, failures, and their causes. Industrialization of the building process.
major_restrictions: CE, CEM, CNST, EE, ME majors only.

subject: CEE
title: Construction Project Management
description: methods, safety, and labor.
major_restrictions: CE, CEM, CNST, EE, and ME majors only.

subject: CEE
title: Construction Equipment and Methods
description: Methods and equipment used on horizontal/ heavy engineering projects. Available equipment, their production, and how they are used to excavate, move, process, and place the earth.

subject: CEE
title: Construction Estimating and Bidding
description: Estimating science; techniques of estimating quantities and pricing of work for construction contracting; classification of costs, analysis of plans and specifications for estimating; computerized estimating; cash flow, bidding strategy, preparation and submission.
major_restrictions: CE, CNST, EE, ME, OE majors only.

subject: CEE
title: Construction Planning and Scheduling
description: To teach the theory and the practice of planning, scheduling, and reporting a construction project through the use of bar chart and CPM. Format to include lectures, text, outside speakers, site visits, discussions, case study, and computers.

subject: CEE
title: Construction Safety
description: Safety and health concepts, laws and requirements for civilian and contractor personnel in construction, including applications in project management and construction activities.

subject: CEE
title: Construction Law
description: litigation in construction projects.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: CEE
title: Undergraduate Structural Research
description: Individual research project for undergraduate students in the structures track. Topic to be determined by consultation with structural faculty advisor.

subject: CEE
title: Structural Loads
description: Introduction to the minimum load requirements for buildings and other structures used in current structural design codes. Derive and apply dead, live, rain, soil, snow, wind, earthquake, flood, inundation, and other loads to structural systems.
prerequisites: CEE 305 and CEE 381 with a minimum grade of C or better.

subject: CEE
title: Senior Design Project & Professional Ethics
description: Design problem involving several areas of civil engineering and requiring a team approach for a solution. Oral communication skills are assessed through multiple presentations to an industry. Engineering ethics, ethical decision making and deliberation.
prerequisites: CEE 220 (with a minimum grade of D-), CEE 250 (with a minimum grade of D-), and CEE 305 (with a minimum grade of C).
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: CEE
title: Senior Design Project
description: Design problem involving several areas of civil engineering and requiring a team approach for a solution. Oral communication skills will be assessed through multiple presentations to an industry panel. (3 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Behavior and design of reinforced concrete beams, one-way slabs and columns. Laboratory section includes design and hands-on manufacturing and testing of reinforced concrete members. Individual investigation in civil and environmental engineering topics as approved by instructor.
class_standing: junior or senior standing, and consent.

subject: CEE
title: Structural Steel Design
description: Basic properties of steel; behavior and design of steel beams, columns, and connections; introduction to rigid frames.

subject: CEE
title: Special Problems
major_restrictions: senior standing, and minimum cumulative GPA of 2.7 or minimum GPA of 3.0 in engineering.

subject: CEE
title: Operations Management
description: analysis; applications; case studies; managerial reporting; LINDO software.
prerequisites: CEE 476 (or equivalent) (with a minimum grade of B-), and consent.

subject: CEE
title: Cost Engineering and Quality Control
description: Study and applications of cost/schedule control systems criteria, earned value analysis, probabilistic cost estimating, construction risk management, construction quality control, and operations research in construction.
prerequisites: consent; knowledge of Python Programming desirable. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: Fall

subject: CEE
title: Risk Analysis and Decision Making
description: Risk analysis and decision making are fundamental areas of construction and project management. Students will benefit from analyzing risks and learning various decision making techniques for real-time applications.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: fall CEE 614 - Negotiation and Alternative Dispute Resolution - Credits: V Lawyers negotiate settlements in almost all their cases. This class presents a "hands-on," skill-building approach to the newest ideas, as well as centuries-old techniques, about the skill lawyers will use most often in their private practice-negotiation. The class also examines the rapidly developing field of alternative dispute resolution (ADR), including mediation, facilitation, arbitration, and courtannexed ADR.

subject: CEE
title: Parallel Computing for Engineers
description: Concepts and techniques in high performance parallel computing. Topics include parallel language and algorithms, parallelizing pre-existing serial codes, statistical analysis, and techniques up to increase computational speed and accuracy for problems requiring large memory size.

subject: CEE
title: Organizations: Theory and Change
description: Explores characteristics and structural, human resources, political, and cultural frames of organizational theory. Focus on organizational change strategies and theories. Discusses how to use these frames and theories in everyday management of public service organizations.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only
major_restrictions: BE, CEE, ECE, ME majors only.

subject: CEE
title: Coastal Flood Mitigation
description: Design and solutions to coastal flood mitigation problems. Topics include climate adaptation; engineering solutions and best practices to mitigate coastal risk under different ocean hazard scenarios; and ecological approaches to mitigate coastal risk.
prerequisites: consent; knowledge of AutoCAD and ORE 661 desirable.

subject: CEE
title: Fluid Mechanics
description: Theory of fluid dynamics in differential form, covering equation of motion, vortex generation, flow in rotating frame, potential theory, laminar flow, and introduction to turbulence.

subject: CEE
title: Groundwater Modeling
description: Introduction to the finite-difference method; steadystate and transient groundwater flow in saturated and unsaturated media; applications to groundwater recharge and aquifer evaluation.

subject: CEE
title: Biological Treatment
description: Fundamentals of applied microbiology and biochemical reactor engineering, quantitative description of microbial growth, operational theory and design basis of aerobic, anoxic and anaerobic treatment processes. Applications for water, wastewater, air, solid wastes, and soil.

subject: CEE
title: Hydrologic Processes in Soils
description: Hydrologic properties in soils and the processes involved in water infiltration drainage and solute transport. Emphasis on key parameters required for modeling.
prerequisites: Recommended: CEE 424 or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: CEE
title: Surface Water Hydrology
description: Deterministic and probabilistic methods include reliability of empirical distributions, multiple regression analysis, extreme value analysis and domain of attraction. Short-memory models for stochastic simulation of streamflows include autoregressive, Markov chain and moving average models. Time series analysis of hydrologic data is discussed.

subject: CEE
title: Groundwater Hydrology
description: Groundwater occurrence, movement, quality, and resource evaluation, development, and management. Emphasis on saltwater encroachment, well evaluation, aquifer protection, recharge with wastewater, and Hawaiʻi type hydrology.

subject: CEE
title: Physical and Chemical Treatment
description: Introduction to physical and chemical processes for water and wastewater treatment: Review of momentum and mass transfer, chemical reactions, colloidal chemistry, coagulation and flocculation, granular filtration, sedimentation, carbon adsorption, gas transfer, disinfection and oxidation.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: spring

subject: CEE
title: Environmental Chemistry
description: Basic concepts of chemistry as related to the environment, with more emphasis on water. Topics include chemical kinetics, equilibrium, acid-base, precipitation and dissolution redox reactions, sorption, organic chemicals in the environment.

subject: CEE
title: Environmental Microbiology
description: Combined lecture-discussion on major topics in environmental microbiology, microbial ecology, and a broad understanding of microbial processes in natural and engineered environments.
major_restrictions: Graduate standing in CEE or consent. CEE majors only.

subject: CEE
title: Applied Mathematics for Engineers
description: Application of advanced mathematics to engineering phenomena; linear algebra, Eigen systems; partial differentiation and multiple integrals; vector/tensor analysis; Fourier series/transforms and Laplace transform; ordinary/partial differential equations; probability and statistics.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: Fall
major_restrictions: CE, ME, ORE, and ECE majors only.

subject: CEE
title: Hazardous Waste Remediation
description: technology, in-situ bioremediation, soil vapor extraction, air sparging, electrokinetics, hydraulic fracturing, reactive walls, and phytoremediation.

subject: CEE
title: Water Quality Modeling
description: Mathematical formulation of pollutant transport and mixing in the water environment. Kinetics formulation and parameter identification, model calibration and verification. Design projects.
prerequisites: Recommended: CEE 422 and CEE 431, or consent.

subject: CEE
title: Membrane Separations
description: Applications of membrane separations to desalination, power generation, and ultrapure water systems. Discussion of reverse osmosis, osmosis-driven processes, ultrafiltration, microfiltration, electrodialysis and ion exchange technologies. Membrane fouling and concentration polarization from practical/theoretical standpoints.

subject: CEE
title: Seepage, Drainage, and Dewatering
description: Theory of seepage, field and laboratory methods of measurement; graphical and numerical methods; design of drainage structures; construction dewatering.
prerequisites: CEE 355 (with a minimum grade of D).

subject: CEE
title: Deep Foundations
description: Analysis and design of deep foundations; driven piles and drilled shafts. CEE 455 (with a minimum grade of C), or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years
prerequisites: CEE 355 (with a minimum grade of D) and CEE 455 (with a minimum grade of D).

subject: COMG

subject: COMG
