subject: ES
title: Introduction to Ethnic Studies
description: Concepts and theories analyzing ethnic group experiences in relation to colonization, immigration, racism, and social class. Emphasis on cultural perspectives and values rooted in the experiences of peoples indigenous to Hawaiʻi, the Pacific, and Asia.

subject: ES
title: Introduction to Ethnic Studies
description: Concepts and theories analyzing ethnic group experiences in relation to colonization, immigration, racism, and social class. Emphasis on cultural perspectives and values rooted in the experiences of peoples indigenous to Hawaiʻi, the Pacific, and Asia. Further development of skills and strategies for dealing with the high demands of academic reading by focusing on becoming efficient and autonomous readers.

subject: ES
title: Race, Class, Gender in Popular Culture
description: Contemporary issues of race, class, and gender in popular culture (film, television, music, social media, sports, etc.). Introduction to critical media analysis and social science theories and methods. Emphasis on writing instruction.

subject: ES
title: African American Experience II *DS
description: ES 305 - African American Experience I *

subject: ES
title: Introduction to Race and Ethnic Relations
description: Race and ethnic relations in world perspective; social, economic, and political problems associated with perception, existence, and accommodation of these groups within the wider society.

subject: ES
title: Hawaiians
description: The social system, land stewardship, and governance of Native Hawaiians in a Pacific context. Transformation of the Hawaiian system by a capitalist economy. Native Hawaiian regeneration and quest for sovereign governance as an Oceanic society.

subject: ES
title: Filipinos in Diaspora
description: Introduction to Filipinos in diaspora. Topics include: race, empire, migration, representation, cultural production, identity formation, and decolonization.

subject: ES
title: Ethnic Identity
description: Individual and group problems of identity, identity conflict, culture conflict, inter-ethnic relations. Critical review of available material on Hawaiʻi.

subject: ES
title: African American Experience I
description: Introduction to environmental justice, explores the premise that all people have a right to a life-affirming environment. Will examine environmental racism, and the geographical dimensions of race and indigeneity.
prerequisites: one DS or DH course, or consent.

subject: ES
title: Ethnicity and Community: Hawaiʻi
description: Site visits to museums, social welfare units, etc., as well as guest lecturers from the community including police, health, education.
prerequisites: one DS or DH course. Frequency of Course (when offered): Summer only

subject: ES
title: Asian America
description: History of selected Asian immigrant groups from the 19th century to the present. Topics include: immigration and labor history, Asian American movements, literature and cultural productions, community adaptations and identity formation. Emphasis on ethics.

subject: ES
title: Hawaiʻi and the Pacific
description: Hawaiʻi as part of the Pacific community: selected historical and contemporary problems of Pacific areas; cultural and economic imperialism, land alienation, and the impact of development on Pacific peoples.

subject: ES
title: Land Tenure and Use in Hawaiʻi
description: Dynamics of change: indigenous Hawaiian land tenure; Great Mahele and Kuleana Act; ethnic succession of land ownership; concentration of ownership today; effects of land development on ethnic communities.

subject: ES
title: Power and Place: Chinatowns
description: Historical and contemporary experiences of Chinese in Hawaiʻi and the U.S. continent with a focus on Chinatowns; urbanization; economic development, community organizing; comparative racialization.

subject: ES
title: Filipinos in Hawaiʻi
description: Historical and contemporary experiences; immigration; traditional culture and values; plantation experience; labor organizing; development of Filipino community; racism; discrimination; and ethnic identity. This course develops oral communication skills.

subject: ES
title: American Indian Experience
description: Provides a comprehensive look at the indigenous foundation of life and society in the Americas and elaborates on historical and contemporary importance of American Indian rights issues. Emphasis on teaching ethical issues and writing.

subject: ES
title: South Asian Migrants: Culture and Politics
description: Historical and contemporary experiences of South Asian migrants in North America, Pacific, Caribbean, and/or African diasporas; causes and patterns of migration, inter-ethnic relations policies; role of race, gender, culture in community, identity formation.
prerequisites: one ES or WGSS course in the 100, 200 or 300 level; or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: ES
title: The Sounds of Race and Inequality
description: Explore inequality as manifested and contested in silence, music, dialect, nature, voice, and acoustic space by listening through a matrix of race, ethnicity, gender, class, and sexualities. Combination of hands-on work and current scholarship.

subject: ES
title: Economic Change and Hawaiʻi's People
description: Hawaiʻi's economic transformation from sustainable communal subsistence through mercantile capitalism, plantation capitalism, and global finance capital and impact on its people. Alternative sustainable enterprises for a self-sufficient island economy.

subject: ES
title: Immigration to Hawaiʻi and U.S.
description: Historical overview: "push and pull factors"; effect of changing economy; experiences of various ethnic groups; problems of recent immigrants; immigration policies in the U.S. and Hawaiʻi.

subject: ES
title: Pacific/Asian Women in Hawaiʻi
description: Adaptive strategies of Hawaiian, Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Filipino, Samoan, and Southeast Asian women in Hawaiʻi; feminist anthropological and historical analysis.
prerequisites: one ANTH, SOC, or WGSS course.

subject: ES
title: Literatures of Hawaiʻi
description: intersectional theories of social change to evaluate strategies to create just and sustainable futures.

subject: ES
title: Asian American Literature
description: Basic concepts and representative texts for the study of Asian American literature by writers from a variety of backgrounds.

subject: ES
title: Gender and Race in U.S. Society
description: Historical and sociological studies of race and gender in U.S. society; grassroots feminist and racial/justice activism on the continent and in Hawaiʻi. The course pays special attention to labor migration, cultural production and community politics. Oceania-centric perspective. Analysis of imperialism, colonialism, gender, sexuality, race, ethnicity, and queer(ed) relations and identities in Hawaiʻi and the Pacific.
prerequisites: one DS or DH course, or consent.

subject: ES
title: Issues of Diversity in Higher Education
description: Examines issues of diversity within higher education. Examines different dimensions of diversity including ethnicity, gender, national origin, age, and sexual orientation. Will utilize national and local case studies.

subject: ES
title: Fieldwork in Ethnic Studies
description: Supervision of individual student research projects pertinent to ethnic studies, including service learning, civic engagement, internship, oral history, or supervised practicum experience in teaching select ethnic studies courses.

subject: ES
title: Social Movements in Hawaiʻi
description: Examines, through writing and collaboration, interethnic movement building among Native Hawaiian, other Pacific Islander, and Asian peoples of Hawai'i through

subject: ES
title: Change in the Pacific-Polynesia
description: Impact of cultural and physical change and their interrelationship.

subject: ES
title: Multiethnic Popular Culture: Hip Hop
description: Historical, social, cultural, and political aspects of the formation and development of Hip Hop in Hawaiʻi and elsewhere in Oceania with special attention to cultural interactions and connections. This course develops oral communication skills.
prerequisites: one DS or DH course or consent.

subject: ES
title: Ethnic Studies in the Digital World
description: ethnicity-and vice-versa. We will challenge racism and discrimination manifested in social media, changing notions of identity and group belonging, ewaste, gaming, big data, and more. ES 440 - Contemporary Diasporas in Comparative Perspective - Credits: 3
prerequisites: one DH or DS course, or consent.

subject: ES
title: Race, Class, and the Law
description: Historical context and implications of landmark court decisions and legal issues affecting social change in ethnic communities in Hawaiʻi and the continental U.S.
prerequisites: one DS or DH course or consent.

subject: ES
title: Women and Work
description: Gender and race of paid and unpaid work in the formal and informal sectors of the economy; carework in households; unemployment; ethics of revaluing feminized work; labor rights: scope: local and global.
prerequisites: one 300-level ES or WGSS course, or SOC 300; or consent.

subject: ES
title: American Ethnic and Race Relations
description: Surveys ethnic and race relations in the U.S. Focus on historical conflicts and critical issues such as racism, immigration, affirmative action, economy, government, and systemic change. Develops written communication skills.
prerequisites: one DH or DS course, or consent.

subject: ES
title: Filming Social Change
description: Compares the circumstances under which contemporary Asian, Pacific Islander, or African migrants form diasporas across the globe; focus on a particular ethnic group to examine its site-specific experiences.

subject: ES
title: Filipino Americans: Research Topics
description: A research seminar on the study of Filipino Americans. Special themes in film/video/media, the performing arts, or literature may be offered.

subject: ES
title: Food, Culture, and Empire in U.S. and Hawaiʻi
description: Examines the cultural, historical, and political processes that have informed our understandings and practices involving food. We will analyze food and foodways in the U.S. and Hawaiʻi.
prerequisites: at least one course in WGSS or ES; or consent by instructor.

subject: ES
title: (Alpha) Topics in Comparative Ethnic Conflict
description: Causes and dynamics of ethnic conflicts with attention to problem resolution; (B) Middle East; (C) Hawaiian sovereignty in Pacific context. sovereignty in Pacific context.
prerequisites: one DS or DH course, or consent for (C).

subject: ES
title: Racism and Ethnicity in Hawaiʻi
description: Historical and contemporary social processes involved in race, racism, and inter-ethnic relations in Hawaiʻi. Focus on intersection of Asian and/or Pacific Island cultures with Native Hawaiian culture.
prerequisites: one of WGSS 151, WGSS 175, WGSS 176, or WGSS 202; or consent.

subject: ES
title: Global Ethnic Conflict
description: Ethnic conflicts cause most wars on our globe today. Examines causes of ethnic conflict, including climate change. Will evaluate approaches to building peaceful relations between groups and developing sustainable relationships with the environment.
prerequisites: one DS or DH course or consent.

subject: ES
title: Latinx Experience in Hawaiʻi
description: Latinx presence; relations among Latinx, other immigrant, and Indigenous communities; causes and patterns of immigration; racism and discrimination; ethnicity and identity issues; struggles for justice.
prerequisites: one upper division ES or SOCS course or consent.

subject: ES
title: Peoples of Hawaiʻi
description: Critically examines the historical and contemporary experiences of various people of Hawaiʻi using anthropological and ethnic studies approaches. Emphasis on cultural perspectives and values of peoples indigenous to Hawaiʻi, the Pacific, and Asia. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: ES
title: Politics of Multiculturalism
description: The development of ethnic relations and political approaches to multiculturalism in two multiethnic nations: Canada and the U.S.
prerequisites: SOC 300 or one 300 level ES course, or consent.

subject: ES
title: Oral History: Theory and Practice
description: Literature, methodology, ethics and project design of oral history research. Students develop and execute an oral history project.

subject: ES
title: Hawaiʻi's Labor History
description: Conditions of work under varying political, social, and economic transformations in Hawaiʻi; anthropological, sociological, and historic data.
prerequisites: one DS or DH course or consent.

subject: ES
title: Special Topics in Ethnic Studies
description: Selected themes in ethnic studies exploring current issues and new topics; taught by regular and visiting faculty. Teacher candidates will examine the impact of culture, family, school, classroom, and individual characteristics on learning, and orally present, in varied formats, acquired knowledge and competencies.

subject: ESEE
title: Assessment II: Formal Assessment
description: Understand techniques in the assessment, planning, and instructional process appropriate for students with mild/moderate disabilities. Inclusion of students with disabilities in the general education environment is stressed. Significant instruction on writing individualized education programs.

subject: ESEE
title: Planning and Instruction: Science
description: Examination of content, processes, strategies, and issues related to the teaching and learning of science in elementary settings, K-6. Candidates learn to differentiate instruction based on learner needs, using evidence-based practices.

subject: ESEE
title: Planning and Instruction: STEM
description: Examination of content, processes, strategies, and issues related to teaching and learning of Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) content for inclusive elementary classrooms, K-6. Teacher candidates focus on planning, teaching, and assessing STEM. ESEE 334 - Planning and Instruction: Integrating Social Studies and the Performing Arts - Credits: 3

subject: ESEE
title: Field Rounds
description: Social Studies and Performing Arts integrated content in diverse K-6 classrooms. Teacher candidates engage with social issues through collaborative, expressive, aesthetic, creative, and critical pedagogy. Candidates perform, present, and teach to develop oral communication skills. Field-based course engages teacher candidates in a variety of purposeful observation and reflection activities aligned with course work. Field settings are appropriate to concurrent classes; supervision provided by participating site personnel and college supervisor.

subject: ESEE
title: Seminar for Student Teaching
description: Seminar to support student teaching experiences and prepare teacher candidates to transition into a full time teacher, examine ethical issues in the field, prepare for employment, and demonstrate ability to meet professional standards. title: Block Pattern Design
description: (2 Lec, 1 3-hr Lab) Principles of pattern making for women's apparel through manipulation of pattern blocks.

subject: FDM
title: Textiles I
description: Fashion Design and Merchandising The minimum required grade for prerequisites is a grade of D or better, unless otherwise specified.

subject: FDM
title: Introduction to the Fashion Industry
description: Introduction to the fields of apparel design and merchandising including theories of fashion change, apparel industry operations, current industry issues, literature of the field, professional competencies, careers in apparel and related businesses.

subject: FDM
title: Culture, Gender, and Appearance
description: Social construction of gender within culture and its visual expression through appearance. Analysis of role, identity, conformity, and deviance in human appearance. Development of ethnic dress of China, Japan, and Korea as a visual manifestation of culture. Students will be trained in written communication skills to describe and report important designs of these ethnic dresses related to culture.

subject: FDM
title: Fashion Forecasting/Marketing
description: Principles and practices in fashion trend forecasting, including environment analysis, consumer research, competitive analysis, color and textile forecasting, style directions, and digital marketing trends. Adapting fashion trend forecasts to apparel lines.
prerequisites: one FW or FG course, or consent.

subject: FDM
title: Draping
description: Principles of pattern making through draping muslin models on standard garment forms. case study and application of the concepts of brand management.
prerequisites: FDM 205 and FDM 215. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: FDM
title: Group Collection Development
description: Advanced study in the specialty market design, pattern making, and construction for fashion design majors. Different specialty designs, such as swimwear, menswear, or others, will be offered each semester.
prerequisites: FDM 215, FDM 221, and FDM 301. Recommended: FDM 315, FDM 330.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only
repeatable: 5 times Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: FDM
title: Advanced Construction
description: Principles of advanced techniques for garment construction with emphasis on new, difficult-to-handle fabrics.

subject: FDM
title: 2D/3D Computer Aided Design
description: Exploration of CAD applications from the design to the pattern-making process. Use of Adobe Illustrator and Photoshop for fashion illustration. Use of OptiTex PDS pattern design software featuring 2D and 3D CAD.

subject: FDM
title: 3D Retail Store Design
description: Visual merchandising concepts and planning; use of a virtual 3D store modeling software package. Visual Retailing/Mockshop, to design store interiors, fixtures, lighting, signage, window displays, and planograms.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: FDM
title: Retail Buying and Merchandising
description: Theories and procedures in selecting, buying and selling apparel and textiles. Types of merchandising organizations, analysis of consumer demand, brick-andclick opportunities and challenges, development of an image, operation location, store and floor layout.

subject: FDM
title: Merchandise Planning and Control
description: Theories, problems, and procedures of financial and assortment planning and control of merchandise inventories.
major_restrictions: FDM majors and merchandising minors only.

subject: FDM
title: Product Lifecycle Management
description: Application of principles of apparel production management, including methods engineering (detail construction for ordering), story boards and color tables, production measurements, costing, and PLM computer applications.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: FDM
title: Apparel Manufacturing
description: Application of principles and procedures related to the promotion of fashion apparel. Preparation and presentation of fashion information through shows, displays, media, and written communications.
repeatable: Repeatable one time. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

Geography and Environment Sophomore standing or higher or consent is required for all 300-level courses, unless otherwise specified. Junior standing or higher or consent is required for all 400-level courses, unless otherwise specified.

subject: GEO
title: The Natural Environment
description: Introduction to physical geography including weather, climate, vegetation, soils, geology, and landforms. Environmental issues and natural hazards.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: GEO
title: General Geomorphology
description: Introduction to geomorphological concepts, process mechanics, and relationships between forms and processes. Emphasis on various subdisciplines of geomorphology: coastal hillslopes, fluvial, aeolean, and glacial.
prerequisites: ERTH 101 and ERTH 101L, or ERTH 101 and ERTH 101L.

subject: GEO
title: World Regional Geography *FGB
description: GEO 325 - Geography, Environment, and Society *DS GEO 380 - Statistical Methods in Geography

subject: GEO
title: Water and Society
description: World's major cultural regions; geographic aspects of contemporary economic, social, political conditions. Interaction of people with water at household, community, regional, national, and international scales, from cultural, political, economic, and biophysical perspectives.

subject: GEO
title: Digital Earth
description: Cartographic representation and meaning in a digital age. Earth models, map projections, coordinate systems, scale, distance, and direction. Data types and transformations in graphic and digital representation. Manual, automated, and web-based map making and analysis.

subject: GEO
title: Geography and Contemporary Society
description: Elements of economic geography and resource management, population and urban geography; application to current problems of developed and underdeveloped worlds.
class_standing: sophomore standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Introduction to Biogeography
description: Introduction to ecosystem concept; environmental adaptations for energy and nutrient transfer; characteristics, dynamics, productivity, and distribution of principal vegetation communities. Human dominance.
class_standing: sophomore standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Introduction to Climatology
description: Elements and controls of climate. World patterns of insolation, temperature, evaporation, precipitation, atmospheric circulation. Climatic classifications.
prerequisites: GEO 101 or ATMO 101 or ATMO 200, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Global Environmental Issues
description: Use and abuse of natural resources and humanity's progress toward developing a sustainable relationship

subject: GEO
title: Economic Geography
description: Examines how factors of production like land, labor and capital; economic activities like consumption, trade, production, and investments; and institutions like state, markets, and corporations alter economic space.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: GEO
title: Globalization and Environment
description: Debates on globalization and development, population and resources; root causes of environmental degradation; impacts of globalization on environmentalism and environmental change; social approaches to managing environmental change. Junior standing or higher.

subject: GEO
title: Geography of Global Tourism
description: Tourist landscape in relation to resources, spatial patterns of supply and demand, impacts of tourism development, and models of tourist space. Flows between major world regions. Emphasis on Writing.

subject: GEO
title: Geography, Environment, and Society
description: Examines the geography of resources and environmental change with a holistic and multi-scale perspective. Social approaches to resolving environmental problems.

subject: GEO
title: Politics, Nations, and States
description: Examines the political organization of space in the sovereign state system. Contemporary and historical analyses of boundaries, geopolitics, homelands, nations, nationalism, and territory. Emphasis on written instruction.
class_standing: Sophomore standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Geography of North America
description: Overview of the physical and cultural geography. Regions and characters. Patterns of population, natural resources, industry, agriculture, and transportation/communication networks.
prerequisites: GEO 101 or GEO 102 or GEO 151, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Intersectional Feminist Geographies
description: Explores space and place from intersectional feminist perspectives including Indigenous, Black, island, postcolonial and queer geographies. Focuses on contemporary case studies in gender and sexuality.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only.

subject: GEO
title: Geography of Japan
description: Regional synthesis of physical and cultural features; economic, social, political geography; origins and development of cities.

subject: GEO
title: Islands and Archipelagos
description: Applies an island studies perspective to critically evaluate the commonalities and differences across islands and archipelagos in several world regions. Examines how island geographies influence social identities and movements and are impacted by environmental conditions. Sophomore standing or higher.

subject: GEO
title: Geography of China
description: Topics: environmental parameters and resource base, ecological control and resource management, institutional and technological transformation of agriculture, industrial potential and industrial location, settlement patterns and rural urban symbiosis.

subject: GEO
title: Geography of South Asia
description: An interdisciplinary course on South Asia covering contemporary debates and social movements pertaining to caste, gender, class, religion, region, labor, law, and development. Includes an emphasis on instruction and feedback in oral communication.

subject: GEO
title: Geography of Southeast Asia
description: An investigation of the development context of Southeast Asia including socioeconomic, cultural, and environmental resources. Problems and prospects for change.

subject: GEO
title: Geography of the Pacific
description: Physical character of the Pacific; cultural, political, economic geography of Melanesia, Micronesia, Polynesia (except Hawaiʻi).

subject: GEO
title: Geography of Honolulu
description: Development of Honolulu and Oʻahu from 1778. Evolution of function, land use, and social patterns. Contemporary planning and environmental issues arising from urban growth.

subject: GEO
title: Geography of Hawaiʻi
description: Regional, physical, cultural geography. Detailed study of people and resources.

subject: GEO
title: UAV and Aerial Photography
description: (2 Lec, 1 3-hr Lab) Introduction to UAV (unmanned aerial vehicle systems) and the measurement, interpretation, analysis, and use of photography acquired by UAV and other aerial systems.

subject: GEO
title: Introduction to Cartography and Air Photo
description: (2 Lec, 1 3-hr Lab) Principles of cartography: compilation and measurement from aerial photographs, alternate forms of data presentation, symbolism, design, and map projection.

subject: GEO
title: Map Design and Production
description: (3 2-hr Lab) Compilation, design and production of maps for presentations, research, and illustration using artists and mapping software.
class_standing: junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Statistical Methods in Geography
description: Quantitative statistical methods will be explored for describing and interpreting geographic/environmental phenomena. Topics will include data display, measurement, sampling, spatial statistics, dimensional analysis, nonparametric and parametric models.
prerequisites: (GEO 101 or GEO 102 or GEO 151) or concurrent or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Introduction to GIS
description: Design, implementation, and use. Database construction and documentation. Techniques for spatial data manipulation and display. Evaluation of existing systems. Student research projects.

subject: GEO
title: Directed Reading
description: Limited to senior majors with a minimum cumulative GPA of 2.7 or a minimum GPA of 3.0 in geography.

subject: GEO
title: Vegetation and the Climate System
description: Role of vegetation in the climate system; links to hydrology and biogeochemical cycling; vegetation and climate history; evolution of terrestrial ecosystems; effects of global warming.
prerequisites: GEO 101 or GEO 300 or GEO 401 or GEO 402 or GEO 405 or ATMO 101 or ATMO 200 or ATMO 302 or ATMO 303 or ATMO 310, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Climate Change
description: Approaches to the study of past and future climate change.
prerequisites: GEO 101 or GEO 300 or GEO 401 or GEO 402 or GEO 405 or ATMO 101 or ATMO 200 or ATMO 302 or ATMO 303 or ATMO 310, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Agricultural Climatology
description: Analyzing climatic data; relation to photosynthesis, phenological development, and crop yields. Cropweather models as guides to improved land-use planning and agronomic practices.
prerequisites: GEO 101 or GEO 300 or GEO 400 or GEO 401 or GEO 405 or ATMO 101 or ATMO 200 or ATMO 302 or ATMO 303 or ATMO 310, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Fluvial Geomorphology
description: Introduction to the single most important geomorphic agent shaping the terrestrial environment. Focus on fluvial process, fluvial dynamics, fluvial landforms, and sediment transport.

subject: GEO
title: Climate, Air Quality, & Policy
description: Examination of air quality problems from scientific and policy perspectives. Includes case studies that explore economic, political, technical, and legal aspects of pollution control.
class_standing: junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Water in the Environment
description: Water fluxes in the environment. Occurrence and movement of water; methods of quantification. Water balance of soil-plant system: precipitation, interception, infiltration, runoff, soil moisture, evapotranspiration, and groundwater recharge. consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years
prerequisites: GEO 101 or GEO 300 or GEO 400 or GEO 401 or GEO 402 or ATMO 101 or ATMO 200 or ATMO 302 or ATMO 303 or ATMO 310, or consent.
gen_ed: DP GEO 408 - Conservation and Evolutionary Biogeography - Credits: 3 Theories and techniques for the analysis of spatial microevolutionary patterns, taught from an interdisciplinary perspective. Examples and readings emphasize Hawaiʻi and the Pacific region.

subject: GEO
title: Cultural Biogeography
description: Coevolution of human societies and plants over the last 10,000 years. Foraging, farming and urban societies economies; spread and modification of selected plants; issues of preservation of genetic resources and traditional plant knowledge. The form and function of gardens.
class_standing: junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Human Role in Environmental Change
description: Human impacts through time on vegetation, animals, landforms, soils, climate, and atmosphere. Special reference to Asian/Pacific region. Implications of longterm environmental change for human habitability.
prerequisites: with a minimum grade of B, one of GEO 101, BIOL 101, BIOL 123 and either GEO 322 or BIOL 310; or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Past Global Change and the Human Era
description: Study of past environments to understand present and future global change. Focus on terrestrial Quaternary environments and global processes.
class_standing: junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Environmental Impact Assessment
description: Introduction to analytical methods for identifying, measuring, and quantifying the impacts of changes or interventions in resource, human-environment, and other geographic systems. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years
prerequisites: either GEO 309, BIOL 265 or BIOL 487, or
class_standing: junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO

description: Challenges associated with the management of land, water resources, fisheries, forests and agriculture in modern Southeast Asia. Case studies are used to illustrate current problems and evaluate potential management solutions.
gen_ed: DS GEO 414 - Environmental Hazards and Community Resilience - Credits: 3 Investigation of the forces behind natural and technological hazards, and human actions that reduce or increase vulnerability to natural disasters. Junior standing or higher.

subject: GEO
title: Nature-Based Tourism Management
description: Principles of nature-based tourism, including a survey of impacts, objectives, planning, and management systems. Junior standing or higher.

subject: GEO
title: Urban Geography

prerequisites: GEO 102 or GEO 151 or GEO 330, or consent.

subject: GEO

class_standing: junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO

class_standing: junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO

class_standing: junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Geographies of Popular Culture
description: Examines contemporary geographical debates related to concepts of discourse, identity, space/place, power, representation, and popular culture. Considers various landscapes of popular culture and how popular culture mediate a sense of place, geopolitics, and identity formation.
class_standing: junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO
title: Environment, Resources and Society
description: Human interaction with the environment. How market, property institution, and technological change affect the environment. Epistemological basis of environmental policies. Debates on controversial environmental issues.

subject: GEO

subject: GEO

description: Explores ethics of the present and historic expansions of the fashion industry and its environmental impact globally. Examines how gender/race/class shape garment production and consumption and fashion activism in world regions. Emphasis on oral/written communication.
grade_option: A-F only. Junior standing or higher.

subject: GEO
title: Political Geography of Oceans
description: higher.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

class_standing: junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO

class_standing: Junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO

description: Geographical factors underlying conflict in the world.

subject: GEO
title: GIS and Spatial Analysis for Social Sciences
class_standing: sophomore standing or higher, or consent.

subject: GEO

subject: GEO
title: 3D Mapping and Analysis
description: Introduction to spacial analysis and GIS for social science studies, using open-source GIS tools to collect, visualize, and analyze social data; public health, socioeconomic and social media data. Requires basic knowledge about GIS.

subject: GEO

description: (2 Lec, 1 2-hr lab) Applications of GIS technologies to solve real-world problems in natural and environmental sciences. Research project, lab.

subject: GEO

subject: GEO

description: Will critically examine what constitutes progress, advancement, or betterment in this highly uneven world, where inter-regional, inter-class, inter-group, and intergender differences in development are expanding. Graduate standing only.

subject: GEO

subject: GEO

subject: GEO

description: Role and potential of systems science in analysis of human environment interaction, especially resource management. Framework and methodology for problem structuring; overview of techniques. Concepts and theories of community, resource access, and governance. Practical challenges to CBNRM in contemporary political economy.

subject: GEO
title: Seminar in Geography of Southeast Asia

subject: GEO

description: Elements of research design, practical field experience, exposure to research and ideologies, broad exposure to heritage and ethos of the discipline.

subject: GEO

description: Investigation of geographic problems of Melanesia, Micronesia, Polynesia.
repeatable: With consent of instructor. State-space modeling also will be included.

subject: GEO

Introduction to research methods in human geography. Explores and provides guided practical experience with research methods and analysis.

subject: GEO

description: Concepts, theory, models. Geographic approaches to spatial and environmental problems. Required of entering graduate students unless waived by department.

subject: GEO

Content will examine the historical development of and contemporary scholarship in political ecology from a human and environmental geography perspective. Advanced seminar on social production of space. Topics include spatial metaphor in social theory; western spatiality from the renaissance through the enlightenment, modernity and post modernity; and geography of the body, home, landscape, and nation.

subject: GEO
title: Seminar: Resource Management in AsiaPacific
description: Examination of resource management problems in Asia and the Pacific. Problems of resource use-agriculture, forestry, energy, minerals, ocean, air quality.

subject: GEO

repeatable: 1 time Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: GEO
title: Seminar: Geographies of Migration
description: Topics vary; may include immigration, borders, refugees, asylum, climate change, and displacement.

major_restrictions: ECE, CENG, ICS, CM, CSCI, THEA, DNCE, THDN majors only.

subject: ICS

subject: ICS

description: Introduction to emerging technologies for construction of World Wide Web (WWW)-based software. Covers programming and scripting languages used for the creation of WWW sites and client-server programming. Students will complete a medium-sized software project that uses languages and concepts discussed in class.
prerequisites: (ICS 311 or ECE 367) and ICS 314, or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year.

subject: ICS
title: Database Systems II

subject: ICS
title: Computer Architecture

subject: IS
title: The Humboldt Brothers' Legacy
description: Life and thought of the two Humboldt brothers. Alexander (1769-1859) helped to lay the foundations of modern science, including geology, geography, botany, zoology, physiology and anthropology. Older, less wellknown Wilhelm (1767—1835) was an influential statesman, classical scholar, man of letters, humanist, political philosopher, anthropologist and linguist, and founded the University of Berlin. Review of the two polymaths' impact on modern science in the Americas, the Pacific and Asia. For Study Abroad Program students participating in the UH Mānoa Semester in London. Emphasis is placed on understanding within historical contexts important issues for contemporary British society-particularly those of class, power, gender, and race.

title: Golf: Advanced
description: dances taught will be more complex, including work with instruments. Individual choreography will be emphasized.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Human Physiology and Anatomy
description: Introduction to human physiology and anatomy designed to serve the needs of those interested in pursuing healthrelated careers. Structure and function of major systems examined (gross anatomy, micro-anatomy, physiology, and pathology).

subject: KRS
title: Social Dance: Ballroom
description: Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Social Dance: Latin
description: Social dances of a Latin flavor including rhumba, chacha, tango, samba, and others. Basic steps, rhythm pattern, styling, and variations are taught. Social etiquette is stressed.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Badminton
description: Rules, etiquette, grip, forehand and backhand strokes, serving, smash, drive, net play, offensive and defensive strategy; singles and doubles play.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Folk and National Dances
description: Popular dances of various national groups, including square dances.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Dances of Hawaiʻi
description: Background and fundamentals of hula. Selected dances with and without instruments.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Tennis: Beginning
description: Rules, etiquette, grip, forehand and backhand strokes, serving, volleying; singles and doubles play.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Tennis: Intermediate
description: Corrective work in three basic strokes and in net play; the lob, drop shot, overhead smash, and half-volley; applying spin in basic strokes; basic strategy in singles and doubles play.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Tennis: Advanced
description: Improving upon the strokes introduced in KRS 130 and KRS 131; advanced competitive strategy; problems in rules; officiating; elements of tournament play.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Dances of Hawaiʻi: Advanced
description: Advanced techniques in hula movements. Selected
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Volleyball: Beginning
description: Combined lecture-lab-physical activity with emphasis on beginner skills and knowledge relative to serving, passing, spiking, digging, blocking, and offensive and defensive strategy.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Tumbling and Rebound Tumbling
description: Single and combination stunts on tumbling mats and trampoline, balancing stunts; techniques of spotting; safety procedures.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Volleyball: Intermediate
description: Combined lecture-lab-physical activity with emphasis on improving skills and knowledge through practice and cognitive activities relative to serving, passing, spiking, digging, blocking, and offensive and defensive strategy.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Heavy Apparatus
description: Single and combination stunts on side horse, horizontal bar, parallel bars, still rings; techniques of spotting; safety procedures.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Judo
description: Rules, etiquette, method of falling and breaking the fall, simple throws and their counters, simple holds and breaking of such holds, randori. (Student to provide own gi.)
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Adapted and Prescribed Exercises
description: Small group and individual guidance and instruction for students recommended by student health service.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Aikido
description: Rules, etiquette, basic rolls, simple holds and the breaking of such holds, specific physical conditioning exercises. (Student to provide own gi.)
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: T'ai Chi Ch'uan
description: Classic forms of t'ai chi ch'uan.
repeatable: Repeatable unlimited times, but credit earned one time only.

subject: KRS
title: Olympic and Power Lifting
description: Exercise technique and prescription with an emphasis on anaerobic exercise. Explores advanced periodization models and their utilization, mastery of Olympic lifts, and plyometric programs.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Karate: Beginning
description: Rules, etiquette, basic stances, blocks, thrusts, kicks, ippon kumite, and selected kata. (Student to provide own gi.)
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Karate: Intermediate
description: Emphasis on improving the basic techniques (stances, punches, kicks, forms, and sparring); introduction to combination techniques. (Student to provide own gi.)
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Wrestling: Beginning
description: Rules: fundamental defensive and offensive maneuvers and competitive strategy, i.e., takedowns, reversals, escapes, and pinning combinations; conditioning exercises.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Yoga: Beginning
description: Experiencing yoga and its effects on body, mind and consciousness of individual. Encompasses exercise, breathing techniques, deep relaxation, meditation, practice in concentration, and yogic postures.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned one time only.

subject: KRS
title: Yoga: Intermediate
description: Corrective work and improvement of basic techniques. Exercise sets and combinations designed to develop endurance, flexibility, muscle and nerve strength, meditation, and deep relaxation technique.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Water Polo
description: Fundamentals of basic water polo skills, namely, ball handling, passing, shooting, dribbling plus a brief introduction into beginning techniques of individual offense and defense.
prerequisites: KRS 104 or consent. (Student to provide own swimming attire approved by instructor.)
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Sport Proficiency I: Invasion
description: Knowledge, skill development, and strategy of various invasion type games and sports. Sports like basketball, soccer, ultimate frisbee and/or others are introduced

subject: KRS
title: Sport Proficiency II: Net, Field, and Target
description: Knowledge, skill development, position, and strategy of net, field and target sports. Sports like volleyball, tennis, softball, and golf are introduced within a tactical framework.

subject: KRS
title: Floor Exercise Gymnastics
description: An entry-level course designed to develop students' knowledge of men's and women's gymnastics floor exercise routines, including injury prevention, skill progression, spotting techniques, and routine choreography. Primarily for KRS majors; others admitted on space-available basis.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Track and Field for Physical Education
description: Knowledge, skills, and rules of various track and field events. Organization of track meets and strategy in competition. Primarily for KRS majors; others admitted on space-available basis.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: Softball
description: Combined lecture-lab-physical activity with emphasis on basic skills, rules, etiquette, offensive and defensive strategies, position play, and game situations.
repeatable: Unlimited times, but credit earned 1 time only.

subject: KRS
title: School Health Problems: Elementary
description: Responsibilities of elementary school teacher in recognizing and meeting pupils' needs, emphasizing teacher's role in health instruction, health services, school health policies.

subject: KRS
title: School Health Problems: Secondary
description: Responsibilities of secondary school teacher in recognizing and meeting pupils' needs, emphasizing health instruction, health services, healthful school living, school health policies.

subject: KRS
title: Introduction to Kinesiology
description: Kinesiology as a professional field. Overview of history, philosophy, and current trends in kinesiology. Survey of psychological and sociological foundations of kinesiology, exercise, fitness and sport.
major_restrictions: KRS majors or approval. KRS 208 - Recreation Services in Contemporary Society - Credits: 3 Theories and philosophies in recreation: history; contemporary issues; roles in modern society; relationship to health, physical education, and exercise science.

subject: KRS
title: Human Development
description: Developmental processes across the life span. Life span perspective on psychological, social, and physical development. Human growth and development from conception to death with major theories and related research on maintaining healthy lifestyles.

subject: KRS
title: Safety and Risk Management
description: Understanding the fundamental principles and techniques of safety and accident-prevention programming emphasizing school, home, public places, on the job, and motor vehicle situations.

subject: KRS
title: Outdoor Recreation Management
description: Objectives of school health program, emphasizing scope and sequence of health instruction; critical examination of health curriculum guides from various states.

subject: KRS
title: Programming and Leadership
description: Factors in planning and leading recreation programs; characteristics and responsibilities of leadership in relation to nature, scope, and resources of a variety of programs.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only KRS 271 - Performance Based Assessment in Health and Physical Education - Credits: 3 Assessment and evaluative techniques applied to health and physical education, including test construction, performance-based assessment, measurement criteria and instruments, interpretation of data and content, as well as program evaluation.

subject: KRS
title: Health Promotion and Disease Prevention
description: Understanding standards-based planning, teaching, and assessment in health with a focus on advocacy, as well as the incidence, prevalence, and prevention of disease within sub-topics of health (mental, social, emotional, environmental, financial, etc.).

subject: KRS
title: Principles of Sports Medicine
description: Examines with some depth, principles of risk management and pathology of athletic injuries, major musculoskeletal structures, and concepts of evaluative skills, treatment, and rehabilitation for common athletic injuries.

subject: KRS
title: Music and Rhythm in Physical Education
description: Use of music in physical education programming (K-12), emphasizing selection of appropriate music for specific activities as expressive or creative movement, movement exploration, rhythmic gymnastics, and dancing. A variety of strategies for teaching dance will be shared. Theory and strategy of offensive and defensive football coaching. Emphasis on coaching philosophy, selecting and developing an offense and defense, organizing practices, special situations, scouting, and training and conditioning.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: KRS
title: Coaching of Basketball
description: Theory and strategy of offensive and defensive basketball coaching. Emphasis on coaching philosophy, selecting and developing an offense and defense, organizing practices, special situations, scouting, and training and conditioning.

subject: KRS
title: Coaching of Baseball
description: Theory and strategy of offensive and defensive baseball coaching. Emphasis on coaching philosophy, selecting and developing an offense and defense, organizing practices, special situations, scouting, and training and conditioning. Administrative framework and procedures pertinent to the operation of agencies providing recreational services to the various publics.

subject: KRS
title: Water Safety Training
description: Theory and methods of advanced lifesaving and water safety leading to American Red Cross Water Safety Instructor (WSI) certification.
prerequisites: KRS 104 or consent. (Student to provide own swim attire approved by the instructor.)

subject: KRS

description: Practicum in training of persons to become qualified in emergency care knowledge, basic life support, and first aid skills. First Aid and CPR certificates may be earned.

subject: KRS

repeatable: 1 time, but credit earned one time only.

subject: KRS
title: Coaching of Track and Field

subject: KRS
title: Coaching of Swimming
description: Theory and methods of coaching competitive swimming. Technical, organizational, and administrative aspects. Emphasis on stroke mechanics and training methods.

subject: KRS
title: Fieldwork in Recreation I

subject: KRS
title: Special Recreation

subject: KRS

major_restrictions: Kinesiology and Rehabilitation Science, Health/Exercise Science and Lifestyle Management, and Physical Education majors only.

subject: KRS

major_restrictions: Kinesiology and Rehabilitation Science, Health/Exercise Science and Lifestyle Management, and Physical Education majors only.

subject: KRS
title: Teaching Techniques I: Elementary

grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: KRS
title: Teaching Techniques II: Secondary

major_restrictions: Primarily for Health & Physical Education majors.

subject: KRS
title: Drugs and Society

subject: KRS

description: Supervised practicum in strength training and conditioning. Students will be exposed to the theory and practice of designing and implementing both strength/ power, and general conditioning programs.

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

repeatable: 1 time, but credit earned one time only.

subject: KRS

prerequisites: KRS 402E (with a minimum grade of B-), KRS 402S (with a minimum grade of B-), and KRS 404 (with a minimum grade of B-); admittance to licensure track, pass Praxis II Core Academic Skills tests.

subject: KRS

major_restrictions: Kinesiology & Rehabilitation Science, Physical Education, and Secondary Education-Physical Education majors only.

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

subject: KRS
title: Teaching Residency: secondary
description: Full-time supervised experience in elementary and secondary school level. School level corresponds to level of licensure desired. (E) elementary; (S) secondary. Each alpha can be taken one time.

subject: KRS

KRS 419 - Administration in Athletic Training, Exercise Science, Allied Health - Credits: 3 consent. An examination of organization and administration in Athletic Training, Allied Health and Exercise Science. Content includes leadership and motivation models, legal liability, ethical considerations and management strategies for all aspects of Health Care Administration.
major_restrictions: Post-Master's Certificate in Nursing majors only.

subject: KRS
title: Lower Extremity Assessment

subject: KRS

major_restrictions: Kinesiology and Rehabilitation Science, Health/Exercise Science and Lifestyle Management, and Physical Education majors only.

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: KRS
title: Learning Communities
description: settings.
repeatable: 1 time Frequency of Course (when offered): Summer only

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

description: Basic consideration is kinesthesis, motor ability, fatigue, developmental factors, practice, motivation in relation to motor learning and human performance.

subject: KRS
title: Motor Development and Learning

subject: KRS
title: Adapted Physical Activity in Early Childhood
description: young children with and without disabilities are covered.

subject: KRS

prerequisites: (FSHN 185, and KRS 113 or PHYL 103 or (PHYL 141/PHYL 141L and PHYL 142/PHYL 142L) or PHYL 301) with a minimum grade of C.

subject: KRS

description: Research methods in the study of physical activity, types of research, statistical concepts and techniques, and reporting research results.

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

subject: KRS
title: Sexual Health Education
description: Program planning, curriculum development and classroom teaching strategies in the areas of human sexuality, reproductive health, family planning, and family health. Focus on research-based curriculum and strategies to promote personal and social responsibility for health.

subject: KRS

subject: KRS

Emphasis will be on experimental learning, writing and self-directed research related to students' internship experiences.

major_restrictions: Kinesiology & Rehabilitation Science, Health/Exercise Science and Lifestyle Management, and Physical Education majors only.

subject: KRS
title: Introduction to Athletic Training Clinic

subject: KRS
title: Lower Extremity, Thorax, and Abdomen
grade_option: A-F only. ATEP Program students only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: KRS
title: AT Practicum I
description: Introductory-level supervised experiences within the profession of athletic training. This practicum is required for the KRS entry-level Graduate Athletic Training Education Program.

subject: KRS

description: Introductory-level supervised experiences within the profession of athletic training. This practicum is required for the KRS entry-level Graduate Athletic Training Education Program.

subject: KRS
title: Athletic Training Practicum III
description: Advanced-level supervised experiences within the profession of athletic training. This practicum is a requirement for entry to the KRS Master's Athletic Training Program.

subject: KRS
title: Athletic Training Practicum IV
description: (5 2-hr Practicum) Advanced-level supervised experiences within the profession of athletic training. This practicum is a requirement for entry to the KRS Master's Athletic Training Program.

subject: KRS

major_restrictions: Enrolled in DRB or KRS graduate programs (including Biomed Sci-Anat/RepoBiol & Phys majors) only or consent.

subject: KRS
title: Upper Extremity, Head, Neck, and Spine
description: Human gross anatomy dissection of the upper extremity, head, neck, and spine. Emphasis is placed on muscles, function, innervation, and vascular supply.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only
major_restrictions: Enrolled in DRB or KRS graduate programs (including Biomed Sci-Anat/RepoBiol & Phys majors) only or consent.

subject: KRS

description: Life span perspective on psychological, social, and physical development. Major theories and related research on human development; applications in policy planning and service delivery in counseling.

subject: KRS
title: Athletic Training, Clinical Experience IV

subject: KRS
title: Muscular Strength and Power Development
description: Interpretation of scientific principles of muscular strength and power development. Examination of muscular adaptations to increased and decreased use.

subject: KRS
title: Athletic Training, Clinical Experience I
description: (1 Sem, 5 3-hr Practicum) Practicum requiring performance of duties and responsibilities of athletic training students. Must be completed during first semester post admission into program.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: KRS
title: Athletic Training, Clinical Experience II
description: (1 Sem, 5 3-hr Practicum) Practicum includes duties and responsibilities of athletic training students. Practicum must be completed during second semester post admission to the program.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: KRS
title: Athletic Training, Clinical Experience III
description: (1 Sem, 5 3-hr Practicum) Culminating practicum requiring performance of duties and responsibilities of second year graduate athletic training students. A practical field experience in athletic training is required.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: KRS
title: Athletic Training Clinical Practicum
description: (1 Sem, 5 4-hr Practicum) Advanced clinical practicum in which the BOC certified or certification-eligible graduate student experiences an enhancement of athletic training knowledge and refinement of athletic training skills.

subject: KRS

description: (1 Sem, 5 4-hr Practicum) Advanced research practicum in which the BOC certified or certification-eligible graduate student deepens their understanding and enhances their critical thinking abilities in order to contribute to the advancement of the discipline and the athletic training profession.

title: Seminar in Athletic Training

subject: KRS
title: Athletic Training Capstone Experience

subject: KRS
title: Administration in Kinesiology
description: non-school settings.

subject: KRS
title: Introduction to Practicum

Unusual real and fictional bodies from fairground history, art, anatomy, literature, natural history and ethnology. Discussion of the moral, medical, philosophical and aesthetic dilemmas of spectacular difference.

subject: LLEA
title: (Alpha) Fantasy and the Fantastic
description: Cross-cultural study of fantasy and the fantastic in short stories, fairy tales, films and novels from Europe and the Americas in English translation. Discussion of illusion, identity, time, the future, the bizarre and major concepts in fantasy literature. (B) fairies, devils and fantasy; (C) the fantastic, the strange and science fiction. LLEA 471C - Fantasy and the Fantastic: the fantastic, the strange and science fiction - Credits: 3 Cross-cultural study of fantasy and the fantastic in short stories, fairy tales, films and novels from Europe and the Americas in English translation. Discussion of illusion, identity, time, the future, the bizarre and major concepts in fantasy literature. (B) fairies, devils and fantasy; (C) the fantastic, the strange and science fiction.

title: Directed Reading and Research
description: Independent study of approved reading and research with faculty supervision.

subject: LLEA
title: Seminar in Research Methods
description: Study of basic research methods and tools, including technology. Print and electronic source materials. Information literacy.

title: Roman Philosophy

prerequisites: any two 300-level LATN courses, or consent.

subject: LAIS
title: Islands/Islas/Ilhas and Global Exchange
description: Cross-cultural study of global issues of navigation, commerce, and the environment, as expressed by the arts, traditions, and narratives from 1492 to the present. Connections with Europe, Africa, Americas, Asia, Oceania.
gen_ed: FGB LAIS 320 - Vaqueros, Gauchos, Paniolo: Cowboy Legacies of the Americas and Hawaiʻi - Credits: 3

subject: LATN
title: Roman Drama
description: Selections from Plautus, Terence, and Seneca. Selections from Lucretius, Cicero, and Seneca. LAIS 340 - Chocolate Tales & Culinary Cultures: Latin America to Hawaiʻi - Credits: 3 Traces evolution of uses, meanings, and cultural representations of chocolate and other food native to Latin America. Emphasis on how narratives of food and food practices engage with national, ethnic, social, and gender identity constructions. Sophomore standing or higher, or consent.

subject: LAIS
title: (Alpha) Studies in Culture
description: Politics of culture and representation. Will consider issues, traditions, movements, texts, and cultural icons for their significance for national and regional identity formation, intercultural relations and global flows of images, people, and capital. (B) Latin America; (C) Iberian Peninsula.

subject: LAIS
title: Spanish Film
description: A chronological survey of films from Spain, from the Silent Era to the present. Conceptually, a cultural history of Spain in the 20th century, as seen through films.

subject: LAIS
title: Latin American Film
description: A survey of films from Latin America, the Silent Era to the present, and their representation of the cultural history of the region. Develops written communication skills.

subject: LAIS
title: Studies in Culture: Latin America
description: Study of cross-cultural patterns in household and community level organizations in Latin America and elsewhere. Topics may include gender relations, kinship structures, political economy, impacts of colonialism, modernization, and globalization on households. Sophomore standing or higher.

subject: LAIS
title: Studies in Culture: Iberian Peninsula
description: Survey of the history and culture of the indigenous peoples of Latin America through a study of their literature, texts and practices. (B) Mesoamerica; (C) Andean South America.

subject: LAIS
title: U.S. Latino Culture and Literature
description: The culture and history of U.S. Latinos through an analysis of their literature and arts and their sociopolitical relationship to the U.S. mainstream culture.

subject: LAIS
title: Studies in Culture: Portugal and Brazil
description: Surveys the cultures of the Portuguese-speaking world from pre-Lusitanian times, including connections with Africa, Asia, the U.S., and Hawaiʻi.

subject: LAIS
title: Colonial Latin American History
description: Pre-Columbian civilizations: Spanish and Portuguese colonization; political, economic, social and religious evolution to 1810; independence.

subject: LAIS
title: New World Rituals and Ideologies
description: etc. Sophomore standing or higher. Minimum C- required grade for prerequisites.
prerequisites: LAIS 360 or LAIS 363 or LAIS 368 or LAIS 372 or LAIS 380, and SPAN 351, and SPAN 352; or consent.
gen_ed: DH LAIS 495 - (Alpha) Topics in Latin American and Iberian Studies - Credits: 3 Combined lecture-discussion on political, social, cultural themes of Iberia and Latin America; (B) Aztlán, Quetzalcóatl, Pachamama; (C) Luso-Hispanic pop culture.
repeatable: 1 time for different alphas. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: LAIS
title: Hispanic Cultural Studies
description: Critical overview of contemporary theories on Hispanic culture. Issues of identity such as mestizaje, hybridity, and pluralism will be discussed from a hemispheric perspective. Law

subject: LAW
title: Exchange Student Program
description: Designed for students accepted to participate in an exchange program while enrolled at William S. Richardson School of Law. Must obtain prior approval for the transfer credits.

subject: LAW
title: Organizing for Social Change
description: Examines conditions that lead people to become active, self-governing agents. Covered are strategies and tactics of organizers, history of social change movements, antisubordination theories of justice and organizing case studies. enforcement, contract interpretation, breach, and remedies. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only
repeatable: Up to 4 credits. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: LAW
title: Employment Discrimination
description: A study of the law of employment discrimination. LAW 508 - Negotiation and Alternative Dispute Resolution - Credits: V Lawyers negotiate settlements in almost all their cases. This class presents a "hands-on," skill-building approach to the newest ideas, as well as centuries-old techniques, about the skill lawyers will use most often in their private practice- negotiation. The class also examines the rapidly developing field of alternative dispute resolution (ADR), including mediation, facilitation, arbitration, and court-annexed ADR.

subject: LAW
title: Contracts I
description: Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year Law of private agreements. Explores the evolution and application of common law doctrines, and, where applicable, relevant provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code. Examines the bases of promissory liability, contract formation, mutual assent, defenses to enforcement, excuses, remedies and damages, and the rights and interests of third parties. Attention will be paid throughout the course to the role of contracts in a market society and the conflicting interests of certainty, freedom of contract and fairness.

subject: LAW
title: Contracts II
description: Comprehensive program that teaches students the analytical and presentational skills necessary for excellent legal writing. Introduces students to legal problem solving and writing through the types of documents lawyers prepare in practice. Continuation of LAW 509. Topics include defenses to enforcement, performance and breach, and remedies.

subject: LAW
title: Historic Preservation Law
description: Introduction to the protection of cultural, archaeological, and historical resources with emphasis on key federal and state laws.

subject: LAW
title: Lawyering Fundamentals II
description: A comprehensive program that teaches students the analytical and presentational skills necessary for excellent legal writing. Introduces skills and strategies for preparing written legal arguments and oral advocacy.

subject: LAW
title: Contracts
description: Law of private agreements. Focuses on common law doctrines with some attention to key Uniform Commercial Code provisions. Examines the bases of promissory liability, contract formation, defenses to
prerequisites: LAW 509 with a minimum grade of C-.

subject: LAW
title: Professional Responsibility
description: Introductory consideration of selected topics relating to functions, structure, and responsibilities of the legal profession and its future role in society.

subject: LAW
title: Reparations and Reconciliation
description: Seminar addresses a legally and socially important contemporary issue-healing present-day wounds of historic injustice. Considers how to repair the continuing social damage of injustice. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: LAW
title: Criminal Law
description: Examination of substantive rules, enforcement procedures, and rationales of criminal law in the U.S.

subject: LAW
title: Children, Parents and the Law
description: Exploration of fundamental concepts of law relating to children, ethical issues, and the role of lawyers in assisting children, and how the child's rights and obligations are balanced with those of parents and state. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: LAW
title: Business Reorganization in Bankruptcy
description: Examination of the rights and remedies available to a failing business and its creditors when the business seeks to reorganize its business and financial affairs under chapter 11 of the federal bankruptcy code. The course is structured as a "practicum," which tracks a single business through restructuring and emphasizes the practical and strategic aspects of lawyering. Recommended: LAW 562.

subject: LAW
title: Civil Procedure I
description: Study of pre-trial, trial, and appellate procedures in the federal and Hawaiʻi courts.

subject: LAW
title: Civil Procedure II
description: Building upon the principles of LAW 516, continues the study of pre-trial, trial, and appellate procedures in the federal and Hawaiʻi courts.
prerequisites: LAW 516 with a minimum grade of C-.

subject: LAW
title: Real Property I
description: Basic course in property ownership, development, regulation. Emphasis on theory.

subject: LAW
title: Real Property II
description: Contract of sale, equitable conversion, deed.
prerequisites: LAW 518 with a minimum grade of C-.

subject: LAW
title: Law and Psychology
description: Approaches psychology as a problem solving tool that can facilitate legal analysis. Covers a variety of areas including jury decision-making, research methodology, social cognition, culture, and behavioral economics, among others. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: LAW
title: Advanced Torts and Insurance Law
description: Advanced study of several areas of tort law and an introduction to insurance law and policy. This course is of considerable importance to students interested in civil litigation and personal injury law. Recent important developments in Hawaiʻi tort and insurance law will be included.
prerequisites: LAW 522 with a minimum grade of C-.

subject: LAW
title: Legislation and Statutory Interpretation
description: The interrelationship between the legislative and judicial branches of government is explored through a review of Federal and Hawaiʻi law-making processes, direct democracy, legislative drafting, and theories of the legislative process and statutory interpretation.
repeatable: Alphas B-Q repeatable three times, up to 12 credits; alphas R-Z repeatable three times, up to 16 credits.

subject: LAW
title: Group Directed Study
description: Designed for maximum flexibility, this course allows a professor to work with a small number of students on a reading/ discussion project of mutual interest.

subject: LAW
title: Federal Indian Law
description: Examines Federal Indian Law, including fundamental concepts and the historical evolution of legal doctrines. Considers the implications of Native Hawaiian sovereignty within the framework of Federal Indian Law. Introduction to basic legal issues at the intersection of law, aging and medicine. Addresses various issues confronting elderly; issues confronting the general population including health care financing, decisionmaking, and bioethics. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: LAW
title: Second-Year Seminar
description: Torts cover the statutory and common law of negligence, causation, defenses, damages, strict liability, intentional torts and tort policy and reform, with emphasis on national and Hawaiʻi law. Seminar required for spring semester of all second-year law students. Substantial paper required. Topics announced in previous fall semester. Placement by lottery.

subject: LAW
title: Business Associations
description: After a brief survey of agency, partnerships, and other forms of business organization, the course will cover the fundamentals of corporations, and securities regulation, including disregarding the corporate entity, management and control of closely held corporations, merger, liability under the federal securities laws, takeovers, public registration, exemptions, and derivative suits.

subject: LAW
title: Health Law
description: Introduction to medical jurisprudence, medical malpractice, informed consent, health care decisions, medical ethics, the health care industry, managed care, financing health care, and the role of government in health care.
major_restrictions: LAW majors only; NURS DNP majors only

subject: LAW
title: Constitutional Law I
description: Introduction to judicial function in constitutional cases, jurisdiction of the U.S. Supreme Court, and discretionary barriers to judicial review.

subject: LAW
title: Constitutional Law II
description: Advanced course in constitutional law with special emphasis on rights secured by the First, Fifth, and Fourteenth Amendments to the Constitution of the U.S.
prerequisites: LAW 533 with a minimum grade of C-.

subject: LAW
title: Intellectual Property
description: A study of the law relating to property rights resulting from intellectual effort, including patents, copyright, trademarks, and trade secrets.

subject: LAW
title: Conflict of Laws
description: Problems respecting the law applicable in transactions or to relationships with elements in more than one state.

subject: LAW
title: Remedies
description: Examines both practice aspects and theoretical underpinnings of equitable remedies. Frequently, compensatory damages cannot adequately protect clients or provide them with the relief they need. Topics include temporary restraining orders, preliminary and permanent injunctions, restitution and unjust enrichment, specific performance, and equitable defenses such as unclean hands, laches, and estoppel. Practice issues concerning appeal, jury trials, and the relationship of equity to law are also explored.

subject: LAW
title: Contract Drafting
description: Hands-on workshop class in drafting contracts, agreement, and similar documents for commercial/business purposes.
prerequisites: LAW 506, or LAW 509 and LAW 510, with a minimum grade of C-.

subject: LAW
title: Criminal Procedure
description: Issues of free press and fair trial, illegal search and seizure, arrest and confession, speedy trial, double jeopardy are covered through student interactions as defense or prosecution attorneys and as judges.

subject: LAW
title: Advanced Civil Procedure
description: Addresses various aspects of complex litigation and recent criticism of the civil litigation system itself. Theoretical in emphasis.
prerequisites: LAW 516 and LAW 517 with a minimum grade of C-.

subject: LAW
title: Evidence
description: "Objection, your Honor!" This course examines the rules of evidence that govern trials in both federal and Hawaiʻi courts and will focus on such topics as hearsay, witness examination, impeachment, physical and demonstrative evidence, expert testimony, writings, relevance, judicial notice, and presumptions.

subject: LAW
title: Race, Culture, and Law
description: U.S. cases and legal theory emphasizing law in the social construction of racial categories, shifts in race-based anti-discrimination law, and the interaction of culture and law in judicial decision-making.

subject: LAW
title: Licensing Intellectual Property
description: Theory and practice of the law relating to the transfer of rights in information and other intangibles are examined together with end user license agreements and the structure and negotiation of upstream licensing mechanisms.
prerequisites: LAW 535 or departmental approval. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

title: Secured Transactions
description: Introduction to Uniform Commercial Code, particularly Article 9- reducing risk of nonpayment by obtaining an interest in borrowers' property.
