subject: LAW
title: (Alpha) Externship
description: Legal work for judges and attorney supervisors in public agencies, private law firms, and the legislature. (H) Hawaiʻi; (P) outside Hawaiʻi.

subject: LAW
title: Externship: Hawaiʻi
description: Introduction to U.S. Immigration and Nationality Law: a brief overview of historical development of immigration law; analysis of exclusion and deportation grounds and remedies; the study of both immigrant and nonimmigrant visa applications and petitions. Current law on asylum and refugee applications and U.S. citizenship and naturalization requirements. Legal work for judges and attorney supervisors in public agencies, private law firms, and the legislature. (H) Hawaiʻi; (P) outside Hawaiʻi.

subject: LAW
title: Externship: outside Hawaiʻi
description: Introduction to U.S. maritime law and admiralty jurisdiction emphasizing development of rules of maritime law and rights of seamen and maritime workers. Legal work for judges and attorney supervisors in public agencies, private law firms, and the legislature. (H) Hawaiʻi; (P) outside Hawaiʻi.

subject: LAW
title: Corporate and Partnership Taxation
description: Examines tax aspects of formation, operation, reorganization, and liquidation of partnerships and corporations.

subject: LAW
title: Trusts and Estates
description: Deals primarily with the disposition of family wealth including: the making of wills; the creation, enforcement, administration, and termination of trusts; and intestate succession, including probate.

subject: LAW
title: E-Discover
description: Surveys the relevant law, theory, and practice governing the review and production of electronically stored information, focusing on the rules of civil procedure, the

subject: LAW
title: Corporate Finance
description: Provides an understanding of the basic financial concepts and tools for lawyers with transactional practices, preferred stock, common stock and convertible securities.

subject: LAW
title: Labor Law I
description: Regulation of union-management relations under state and federal laws.

subject: LAW
title: High Growth Entrepreneurship
description: An interdisciplinary (JD-MBA) course examining legal, business, and technology issues related to building high growth companies. Student teams develop company feasibility reports and skills necessary to advise or build high growth businesses. Recommended: LAW 531. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year.
major_restrictions: Law, MBA, and ME students only.

subject: LAW
title: Administrative Law
description: Procedure and remedies for resolving controversies between citizens and government officials exercising administrative power.

subject: LAW
title: Debtors' and Creditors' Rights
description: Bankruptcy laws and rules, laws of liens, receiverships.

subject: LAW
title: Trial Practice
description: Examination of sequential stages of pre-trial and trial practice in a problem setting. Topics include investigation, pleadings, motions, discovery, voir dire examination, opening statements, direct and cross examination, closing argument, selected evidentiary problems, post-trial motions, and appellate practice. Students engage in simulated exercises, and their work is critiqued.

subject: LAW
title: Pre-Trial Litigation
description: Theory and practice of civil pre-trial litigation with focus on pleading, discovery, and pre-trial motions.

subject: LAW
title: Non-Profit Organizations
description: Examines the meaning, scope, and role of non-profit organizations in contemporary society, and focuses on selected non-tax laws and primary tax issues relevant to non-profits. Recommended: LAW 531 and LAW 567. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: LAW
title: Federal Income Taxation
description: Surveys the entire federal income tax system, with emphasis on those areas of greatest importance to nontax lawyers. Students are expected to develop proficiency in the use of the Internal Revenue Code and Treasury Regulations.

subject: LAW
title: Family Law
description: Legal forms of-and responses to-formation, maintenance, and dissolution of the family. Marriage, annulment, divorce, alimony, separation agreements, child custody and adoption, parentage.

subject: LAW
title: Federal Courts
description: An examination of the jurisdiction and law-making powers of the federal courts, standing issues, appellate jurisdiction of the Supreme Court, federal-question and diversity-of-citizenship jurisdiction of the federal district courts, immunities from suit in the federal courts possessed by governmental entities and officers, intervention by federal courts in state proceedings, and choice of law in the federal courts. Particular emphasis on relevant Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.
prerequisites: LAW 533 (or concurrent) with a minimum grade of C-.

subject: LAW
title: International Protection of Human Rights
description: The growing body of international human rights laws, including procedural law and role of nongovernmental organizations.

subject: LAW
title: Jurisprudence
description: Relationships between the concepts of law and morality with views of legal and moral philosophers. LAW 574 - State and Local Government Law and Finance - Credits: V City, town, county, district governments: administrative organization; regulatory powers; police power; local governmental taxation; relationship between local, state, and federal government.

subject: LAW
title: Advanced Legal Analysis
description: Designed to help students enhance their legal writing and analysis skills as law students, bar examinees, and legal professionals.

subject: LAW
title: Artificial Intelligence and Social Justice
description: Introduces you to and engages you directly in the study of artificial intelligence, legislation and regulation, and social justice in a variety of application contexts.

subject: LAW
title: Extended Bar Review I
description: Provides students with critical skills for success on the bar exam, specifically, MBE and MEE question practice and review of highly tested topics in Civil Procedure, Criminal Law and Procedure, and Real Property.

subject: LAW
title: Introduction to Space Law & Policy
description: Acquaints students with the fundamental principles of space law and policy, including the pre- and postSputnik history of space exploration. Provides students with critical skills for success on the bar exam, specifically, MBE and MEE question practice and review of highly tested topics in Contracts, Evidence, and Torts.

subject: LAW
title: Land Use Management and Control
description: Survey course of public land use management.

subject: LAW
title: Advanced Immigration Law & Policy
description: Current immigration policy developments as a window into the way law, both legislated and case-law driven, interacts with policy objectives to produce desirable as well as unanticipated outcomes. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: LAW
title: Real Estate Development and Financing
description: Federal and state laws in the practice of real estate development and financing law. Condominium, securities, subdivision, consumer protection, and mortgage areas.

subject: LAW
title: Civil Rights
description: Focuses on the civil rights of Americans and introduces alternative remedies and procedures for securing these rights.

subject: LAW
title: Refugees, Asylum & Forced Migration
description: Based on international agreements, implemented through domestic federal statutory law, and administered by Executive Branch agencies who are subject to judicial review, creating a rich context for legal learning. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: LAW
title: Labor and Employment Law
description: Employment law, statutory rights affecting the employment relation, and alternative contract provisions to secure the parties' intentions. Focus on the practical application of labor and employment law. Materials relating to the unionized employment relationship. Emphasis on the labor arbitration process and possibly, to issues regarding internal union affairs.

description: Introduces logic as a way of understanding the meanings of everyday words and sentences, as well as the inferences that humans draw from them. Topics include propositional logic, first-order logic, elementary set theory, and relations.

subject: LING
title: (Alpha) Language in Hawaiʻi and the Pacific
description: Introduction to the study of language and languagerelated issues, with a focus on Hawaiʻi and the Pacific; (B) unit mastery; (C) lecture discussion with focus on writing as a grading criterion.

subject: LING
title: Bad Words
description: An examination of the link between language and society through the use and perception of taboo words.

subject: LING
title: General Linguistics
description: Introduction to the formal analysis of language, focusing on phonetics, phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, historical linguistics, language acquisition, and related topics.

subject: LING
title: Computer Applications
description: Introduction to the study of language and languagerelated issues, with a focus on Hawaiʻi and the Pacific;

subject: LING
title: Languages of the World
description: Survey of major language families; typological classification and language universals; writing systems, "contact" languages. Variety of grammatical structures illustrated by selected languages.

subject: LING
title: The Philippine Language Family
description: Introduction; phonological and grammatical systems; historical developments; emphasis on Filipino, Cebuano, and Ilokano.
prerequisites: ANTH 152. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: LING
title: Language and Gender
description: The role of language in the construction of gender and in the maintenance of the gender order. Field projects explore hypotheses about the interaction of language and gender. No previous knowledge of linguistics required. LING 394 - Philippine Sociolinguistics: Language Use, Ideologies, and Identities - Credits: 3
prerequisites: LING 102 or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: LING
title: Pidgin and Creole Languages
description: Examines the intersection between language and society, specifically Philippine languages in various communities. Explores how language policies, discourses, and ideologies shape people's use of language. Emphasis on developing oral communication and written skills.

subject: LING
title: Articulatory Phonetics
description: Intensive training in recognition, reproduction, and recording of human speech sounds; preparation for fieldwork with unrecorded languages and for clinical work in speech pathology.

subject: LING
title: Psycholinguistics
description: How does language serve as a proxy for larger social questions? Focuses on four main themes: language revitalization, discrimination on the basis of accent, gender miscommunication and the English Only Movement.

subject: LING
title: Language Endangerment and Revitalization
description: An overview of language endangerment, especially in the Pacific and Asia, and a critical examination of the strategies that are being developed to combat it.
prerequisites: one of LING 102, LING 150B, LING 150C, LING 105, LING 320, SLS 150, SLS 301, or consent.

subject: LING
title: Raciolinguistics
description: attitudes, and language in the public sphere.

subject: LING
title: Morphology
description: Theory of word structure; analysis of a variety of morphological types.

subject: LING
title: Introduction to Phonological Analysis
description: Phonological analysis and theory.

subject: LING
title: Introduction to Grammatical Analysis
description: Syntactic analysis and grammatical theory.
prerequisites: LING 320 (with a minimum grade of B) or consent.

subject: LING
title: Cognitive Linguistics
description: Conceptual systems and language from a cognitive science perspective. Linguistic evidence on conceptual structure, reasoning, categorization, and understanding.
prerequisites: LING 102, LING 320, ICS 111, or PSY 100; or consent.

subject: LING
title: Animal Communication
description: Investigates animal communication from the perspective of modern linguistics. Dispels common misconceptions about "talking animals" and shows how the cognitive, biological, and environmental needs and opportunities of animals determine what and how they communicate.

subject: LING
title: Computational Modeling
description: Hands-on introduction to modeling language. Focuses on connectionism, relations between language perception, and motor control. Requires no programming experience.
prerequisites: LING 102, LING 320, ICS 111, or PSY 100; or consent.

subject: LING
title: Topics in Linguistics
description: Variable topics in special areas of Linguistics, depending on instructor interest.

subject: LING
title: Meaning
description: Theories of how literal and figurative language encode meaning and processes of meaning encoding and decoding.
prerequisites: LING 102, LING 320, ICS 111, or PSY 100; or consent.

subject: LING
title: Polynesian Language Family
description: Introduction to the language family of Hawaiian, Samoan, Tahitian, Tongan, etc.; models of migration and settlement and linguistic evidence; subgrouping and reconstruction of Proto-Polynesian; linguistic characteristics of present-day languages; language endangerment and conservation in Polynesia.
prerequisites: LING 320 with a grade of B or better, or consent.

subject: LING
title: Induction of Linguistic Structure
description: Phonological and grammatical structures of a previously uncodified language are determined by linguistic analysis of data obtained from speakers of the language.

subject: LING
title: Children's Speech
description: Child language acquisition, language milestones, multilingualism. Relation of cognitive development to language development. Emphasis on developing research skills, with an emphasis on written communication.

subject: LING
title: Semantics and Pragmatics
description: Ways in which the interpretation of sentences in natural language depends upon the literal meaning of propositions and their logical (semantic) and conversational (pragmatic) inferences.

subject: LING
title: Acoustic and Auditory Phonetics
description: Principles of acoustics and audition as they relate to speech sounds, use of computer-based analysis tools to investigate acoustic properties of speech.

subject: LING
title: Discourse and Grammar
description: Language as a communication system, current theories of grammar, meaning, sociolinguistics, linguistic change and comparison. This is not a course about how to become an entrepreneur or how to start or manage a business. It is most appropriate for those students who are part of a family that owns and manages a business.

title: Marketing Research and Data Analytics
description: Use of marketing research and marketing management and data analytics to support marketing management. Topics include: the research process; survey design; sampling; measurement; primary data analysis; customer data analytics; and digital media analytics.

subject: MKT
title: Marketing Communications
description: In-depth coverage of the major communication tools used in marketing such as advertising, sales promotion, public relations and the internet. Emphasis on integrated marketing communications.

subject: MKT
title: Integrated Communication Campaigns
description: Management of integrated marketing communications campaigns. Includes: conducting target market and competitor research, developing creative content and media strategies, production of communication materials and completion of major real world project.

subject: MKT
title: Retailing Management
description: Analysis of opportunities and challenges in physical and digital retailing management: physical layout and digital design; merchandise planning; organizational forms; expense analysis; integration of physical/digital programs.

subject: MKT
title: Sales and Sales Management
description: Delves into selling and the sales management process. Through a variety of activities, students experience applying selling techniques, sales planning, recruiting and training salespeople, methods of motivating and compensating, territory management and sales team communications.

subject: MKT
title: Professional Selling
description: Emphasis on selling technique, social-psychological principles of persuasion, and interpersonal communication. Lecture, discussion, and application of relevant principles and techniques. provide opportunities for application.

subject: MKT
title: Customer Relationship Management
description: Focuses on the evolving field of customer relationship management. Students learn how to manage marketing information and decision-making systems that maximize customer retention and build long-term relationships.

subject: MKT
title: Sustainable Marketing
description: Provides environmental knowledge and managerial tools that help for profit and not-for-profit organizations address pressing issues like global warming, plastic pollution, and fair trade while earning surplus revenue and remaining socially accountable.

subject: MKT
title: Marketing for New Ventures
description: Examines role of marketing in the formation and launch of entrepreneurial ventures within and outside of ongoing businesses. Emphasis on new product development and low cost, high impact marketing activities.

subject: MKT
title: International Marketing
description: Principles and topics related to international marketing, with emphasis on strategic planning and applications.

subject: MKT
title: Marketing Strategies
description: Decision-making by the marketing executive; integration of all elements of the marketing program based on actual business situations.
prerequisites: MKT 311, MKT 321, and BUS 312; or consent.

subject: MKT
title: Directed Reading and Research
description: department chair and the faculty advisor.

subject: MKT
title: Marketing Analytics
description: Introduces current quantitative techniques and analytical approaches to better marketing decisionmaking. Students learn how to solve problems in a datadriven way using software tools.

subject: MKT
title: Japanese Marketing Systems
description: Specialized study of Japanese marketing systems, considers both global and domestic marketing activities in the context of the Japanese economy.

subject: MKT
title: New Product Innovation and Development
description: Innovation/development processes critical to successful new products. Emphasis on existing larger businesses. Students gain experience in bringing new products from creative idea generation to market launch.

subject: MKT
title: Consumer Behavior for Managers
description: Designed to help students better understand how consumers process information, form attitudes, and make decisions. Draws on theories from social sciences to help students manage consumer needs and satisfaction.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: MKT
title: Sales Leadership
description: Sales managers participate in diverse activities including hiring, designing and implementing training programs, infield coaching and development, motivating and compensating employees, and team building. Helps students develop knowledge and skills in these areas.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: MKT
title: Advanced Marketing Management
description: A case course in the application of advanced marketing methods.

subject: MKT
title: Strategic Brand Management
description: The study of branding and brand management. Explores how managers can formulate and implement integrated marketing strategies to build brand equity. Includes relevant theory and real world applications.

subject: MKT
title: Marketing Research Management
description: Examines use of marketing research/data analytics to support marketing management. Topics include: research process; survey design; sampling; measurement; primary data analysis; customer data analytics; digital media analytics. Real world project reinforce learning.

subject: MKT
title: Creativity in Marketing
description: Thought processes that relate to creativity. Through a series of exercises, students experience enriched creativity and enhanced communication skills.

subject: MKT
title: Services Marketing
description: Conceptual understanding of distinctive aspects of services, services management, and services research.

subject: MKT
title: Digital Marketing Management
description: Examines digital marketing's role in new product development; direct sales; marketing communications via the internet, social media, and mobile devices; and digital tools such as online tracking and analysis. Real activities provide opportunities for application.
major_restrictions: PhD student status in business administration or consent.

subject: MKT
title: Advanced Seminar in Marketing
description: Significant topics, problems in marketing.

subject: MKT
title: Seminar in Marketing Theory
description: Focuses on the building blocks of theory, their use, and evolution within a marketing context. Explores marketing theories, theory construction, and the creation of marketing knowledge.
major_restrictions: PhD student status in business administration or consent.

subject: MKT
title: Seminar in Consumer Behavior
description: Focuses on consumer behavior theory. Provides indepth review of important published work in traditional and cross-cultural consumer behavior fields.
major_restrictions: PhD student status in business administration or consent.

subject: MKT
title: Seminar in Selected Marketing Topics
description: Selected topics in any aspect of marketing to increase exposure to the range of issues researchers commonly confront.
major_restrictions: PhD student status in business administration or consent.

subject: MKT
title: Directed Research
description: Reading and research in an area of marketing under the direction of faculty member(s). Mathematics The minimum required grade for prerequisites is a grade of C (not C-) or better, unless otherwise specified. All 600-level courses' prerequisites are graduate standing or consent.
major_restrictions: PhD student status in international management or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Survey of Mathematics
description: Selected topics designed to acquaint non-specialists with examples of mathematical reasoning. May not be taken for credit after MATH 215 or higher.

subject: MATH
title: Math for Elementary Teachers I
description: Understanding, communicating, and representing mathematical ideas, problem solving, and reasoning. Number systems, place value, fractions, and properties of operations. Prospective elementary education majors only.

subject: MATH
title: Math for Elementary Teachers II
description: mathematical modeling.

subject: MATH
title: Precalculus: Elementary Functions
description: Algebra review, functions with special attention to polynomial, rational exponential and logarithmic functions, composed and inverse functions, techniques of graphing.
prerequisites: two years of high school algebra, one year of plane geometry.

subject: MATH
title: Precalculus
description: Algebra review. Polynomial, rational, exponential, and logarithmic functions, composed and inverse functions, techniques of graphing. Trigonometric function, analytic geometry, polar coordinates, vectors, and related topics.
prerequisites: MATH 134 or MATH 161 or assessment exam.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Programming
prerequisites: MATH 134 or MATH 161, or placement exam.

subject: MATH
title: Applied Calculus I
description: Basic concepts; differentiation, differential equations and integration with applications directed primarily to the life sciences.

subject: MATH
title: Applied Calculus II
description: Differential calculus for functions in several variables and curves, systems of ordinary differential equations, series approximation of functions, continuous probability, exposure to use of calculus in the literature.

subject: MATH
title: Calculus I
description: Basic concepts; differentiation with applications; integration.
prerequisites: MATH 140X or MATH 215 or placement exam.

subject: MATH
title: Calculus II
description: Integration techniques and applications, series and approximations, differential equations,
prerequisites: MATH 241 or MATH 251A or a grade of B or better in MATH 215; or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Calculus III
description: (3 hr) Introduction to numerical algorithms and structured programming using Fortran, MATLAB, or other appropriate language. Multiple integrals; line integrals and Green's Theorem; surface integrals, Stokes's and Gauss's Theorems.
prerequisites: one semester of calculus (MATH 203, MATH 215, MATH 241, MATH 242, MATH 243, MATH 244, MATH 251A, MATH 252A, or MATH 253A) (or concurrent), or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Calculus for Business and Social Sciences

subject: MATH
title: Accelerated Calculus I
description: Basic concepts; differentiation with applications; integration. Compared to 241, topics are discussed in greater depth. equations.
prerequisites: assessment and consent, or a grade of A in MATH 140X and consent.

subject: MATH
title: Accelerated Calculus II
description: Integration techniques and applications, series and approximations, differential equations, introduction to vectors.
prerequisites: MATH 251A (with a minimum grade of B) or MATH 241 (with a minimum grade of A and consent). Honors Program restriction: Yes

subject: MATH
title: Accelerated Calculus III
description: Vector calculus; maxima and minima in several variables; multiple integrals; line integrals, surface integrals and their applications.
prerequisites: MATH 252A with a grade of B or better, or consent. Honors Program restriction: Yes

subject: MATH
title: History of Mathematics
description: The historical development of mathematical thought.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Discrete Mathematics
description: Symbolic logic, sets and relations, algorithms, trees and other graphs. Additional topics chosen from algebraic systems, networks, automata.
prerequisites: one semester of calculus from mathematics department; or consent. Recommended: one semester programming.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Differential Equations I
description: First order ordinary differential equations, constant coefficient linear equations, oscillations, Laplace transform, convolution, Green's function.
prerequisites: MATH 216 or MATH 243 (or concurrent) or MATH 253A (or concurrent), or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Differential Equations II
description: Constant coefficient linear systems, variable coefficient ordinary differential equations, series solutions and special functions, Fourier series, partial differential Deterministic mathematical modeling emphasizing models and tools used in the biological sciences. Topics include difference equations, qualitative behavior solutions of ODEs and reaction-diffusion equations. A computer lab may be taken concurrently.

subject: MATH
title: Linear Algebra and Differential Equations
description: Introduction to linear algebra, application of eigenvalue techniques to the solution of differential equations. Students may receive credit for only one of MATH 307 or MATH 311.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Linear Algebra
description: may receive credit for only one of MATH 307 or MATH 311.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Advanced Mathematics
description: Formal introduction to the concepts of logic, finite and infinite sets, functions, methods of proof and axiomatic systems. Learning mathematical expressions in writing is an integral part of the course.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Real Analysis
description: A rigorous axiomatic development of one variable calculus. Completeness, topology of the line, limits, continuity, differentiation, integration. Emphasis on teaching mathematical writing.
prerequisites: MATH 242 or MATH 252A, and MATH 321; or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Foundation of Euclidean Geometry
description: Axiomatic Euclidean geometry and introduction to the axiomatic method.
prerequisites: MATH 243 or MATH 253A, and MATH 321 (or concurrent); or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Non-Euclidean Geometries
description: Hyperbolic, other non-Euclidean geometries.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Euclidean and NonEuclidean Geometries
description: Axiomatic geometry and introduction to the axiomatic method; Euclidean geometry; hyperbolic geometry, and other non-Euclidean geometries.
prerequisites: MATH 243 or MATH 253A, and MATH 321 (or concurrent); or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: MATH
title: Financial Mathematics
description: Interest rate measurement, valuation of annuities, loan repayment, bond valuation, rate of return. Term structure of interest rates, cashflow duration, and immunization. Financial mathematics actuarial exam topics.
prerequisites: MATH 216 or MATH 242 or MATH 252A or (MATH 203 and BUS 250). Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: fall

subject: MATH
title: Elementary Probability Theory
description: Sets, discrete sample spaces, problems in combinatorial probability, random variables, mathematical expectations, classical distributions, applications.
prerequisites: MATH 216, MATH 242, or MATH 252A; or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Elementary Probability and Statistics
description: Problem-oriented introduction to the basic concepts of probability and statistics, providing a foundation for applications and further study.
prerequisites: MATH 216 or MATH 242 or MATH 252A or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Elementary Statistics
description: Estimation, tests of significance, the concept of power.

subject: MATH
title: Partial Differential Equations I
description: Integral surfaces and characteristics of first and second order partial differential equations. Applications to the equations of mathematical physics.
prerequisites: MATH 243 or MATH 253A, or consent. Recommended: MATH 244 and MATH 302.

subject: MATH
title: Partial Differential Equations II
description: Laplace's equation, Fourier transform methods for PDEs, higher dimensional PDEs, spherical harmonics, Laplace series, special functions and applications.

subject: MATH
title: Ordinary Differential Equations
description: Systems of linear ordinary differential equations, autonomous systems, and stability theory applications. Optional topics include series solutions, Sturm theory, numerical methods.

subject: MATH
title: Numerical Analysis
description: Numerical solution of equations, interpolation, leastsquares approximation, quadrature, eigenvalue problems, numerical solution of ordinary and partial differential equations. (These topics are covered in the year sequence MATH 407-MATH 408.)
prerequisites: MATH 243 or MATH 253A, and MATH 307 or MATH 311; or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to the Theory of Numbers
description: Congruences, quadratic residues, arithmetic functions, distribution of primes. Emphasis is on teaching theory and writing, not on computation.

subject: MATH
title: Topology
description: General topology, including compactness and connectedness; the Jordan Curve Theorem and the classification of surfaces; first homotopy or homology groups.

subject: MATH
title: Linear Algebra
description: Vector spaces over arbitrary fields, minimal polynomials, invariant subspaces, canonical forms of matrices; unitary and Hermitian matrices, quadratic forms.
prerequisites: MATH 307 or MATH 311, and MATH 321; or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Abstract Algebra
description: Introduction to basic algebraic structures. Groups, finite groups, abelian groups, rings, integral domains, fields, factorization, polynomial rings, field extensions, quotient fields. Emphasis on writing instruction. (These topics are covered in the year sequence MATH 412-MATH 413 .)

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Abstract Algebra
description: Continuation of MATH 412. This is the second course of a year sequence and should be taken in the same academic year as 412. Emphasis on writing instruction.

subject: MATH
title: Principles of Analysis I
description: Topology of Rn, metric spaces, continuous functions, Riemann integration, sequences and series, uniform convergence, implicit function theorems, differentials and Jacobians. Emphasis on teaching mathematical writing. (These topics are covered in the year sequence 431-432.)
prerequisites: MATH 311, MATH 321, and MATH 331; or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Principles of Analysis II
description: Continuation of MATH 431. This is the second course of a year sequence and should be taken in the same academic year as MATH 431. Emphasis on writing instruction continues.

subject: MATH
title: Vector Analysis
description: Vector operations, wedge product, differential forms, and smooth mappings. Theorems of Green, Stokes, and Gauss, both classically and in terms of forms. Applications to electromagnetism and mechanics.

subject: MATH
title: Optimization: Theory & Methods
description: Introduction to theory and methods for optimization. Topics may include least square analysis, search methods, conjugate direction methods, linear programming, integer programming, and constrained optimization.
prerequisites: MATH 244 or MATH 253A, and MATH 307 or MATH 311, or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Differential Geometry
description: Properties and fundamental geometric invariants of curves and surfaces in space; applications to the physical sciences.
prerequisites: MATH 244 or MATH 253A, and MATH 311; or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Complex Analysis
description: Analytic functions, complex integration, introduction to conformal mapping.
prerequisites: MATH 244 or MATH 253A; recommended MATH 331; or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Topics in Undergraduate Mathematics
description: Advanced topics from various areas: algebra, number theory, analysis, and geometry.

subject: MATH
title: Axiomatic Set Theory
description: Sets, relations, ordinal arithmetic, cardinal arithmetic, axiomatic set theory, axiom of choice and the continuum hypothesis.
prerequisites: MATH 321 or graduate standing in a related field or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Mathematical Logic
description: A system of first order logic. Formal notions of wellformed formula, proof, and derivability. Semantic notions of model, truth, and validity. Completeness theorem.
prerequisites: MATH 321 or graduate standing in a related field or consent. Recommended: 454.

subject: MATH
title: Combinatorial Mathematics
description: Finite configurations. Topics may include counting methods, generating functions, graph theory, map coloring, block design, network flows, analysis of discrete algorithms.

subject: MATH
title: Senior Seminar
description: Seminar for senior mathematics majors, including an introduction to methods of research. Significant portion of class time is dedicated to the instruction and critique of oral presentations. All students must give the equivalent of three presentations.

subject: MATH
title: Mathematical Biology Seminar
description: Reports on research in mathematical biology, reviews of literature, and research presentation. Required for Certificate in Mathematical Biology.
class_standing: junior standing or higher and consent.

subject: MATH
title: Directed Reading
description: Limited to advanced students who must arrange with an instructor before enrolling.

subject: MATH
title: Problem Solving for Teachers
description: Practicing teachers develop and improve their problemsolving skills by working on challenging mathematical tasks. Students improve their mathematics content knowledge by working on problems and learning to design challenge problems for their own classes. Practicing teachers in grades K-12 only.
prerequisites: MATH 244 (or concurrent) or MATH 253A (or concurrent), or consent. Recommended: MATH 305 or MATH 371 or MATH 372; or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Statistical Inference
description: Sampling and parameter estimation, tests of hypotheses, correlation, regression, analysis of variance, sequential analysis, rank order statistics.
grade_option: CR/NC only. MATH 600 - (Alpha) Career Skills for Graduate Students in Mathematics - Credits: 1 Seminar addresses issues important in the career of a mathematician, beginning from their time in graduate school, through navigating the job market and on to their eventual work in industry or academia. (B) teaching.
repeatable: Unlimited times, Only one time for (B).

subject: MATH
title: Applied Dynamical Systems
description: Continuous and discrete dynamical systems; bifurcation theory; chaotic maps. Additional topics from PDEs and linear algebra.

subject: MATH
title: Modern Algebra
description: Modules, Sylow theorems, Jordan-Holder theorem, unique factorization domains, Galois theory, algebraic closures, transcendence bases. (These topics are covered in the year sequence MATH 611-MATH 612.)

subject: MATH
title: Modern Algebra
description: Continuation of MATH 611. This is the second course of a year sequence and should be taken in the same academic year as MATH 611.

subject: MATH
title: Group Theory
description: Sylow theorems, solvable groups, nilpotent groups, extension theory, representation theory, additional topics.

subject: MATH
title: Ring Theory
description: Ideal theory in Noetherian rings, localization, Dedekind domains, the Jacobson radical, the Wedderburn-Artin theorem, additional topics.

subject: MATH
title: Lattice Theory
description: Linear algebra, ordinary differential equations, and partial differential equations. Topics include spectral theory, matrix exponentiation, initial-value problems, dynamical systems, bifurcation theory, first-order PDEs, characteristics, Sturm-Liouville theory, boundary-value problems, Green's functions. Introduction with applications to general algebra. Partially ordered sets, decomposition theory, representations of lattices, varieties and free lattices, coordinatization of modular lattices.

subject: MATH
title: Partial Differential Equations
description: Introduction to basic techniques, including subalgebras, congruences, automorphisms and endomorphisms, varieties of algebras, Mal'cev conditions. Classical existence and uniqueness theory for PDEs, qualitative properties, classification, boundary value and initial value problems, fundamental solutions, other topics.

subject: MATH
title: Numerical Analysis
description: Numerical linear algebra including iterative methods, SVD, and other matrix factorizations, locating eigenvalues, discrete approximation to partial differential equations. Recommended: MATH 407, MATH 411, or consent.

subject: MATH
title: Key Elements of Linear Algebra
description: Key concepts of linear algebra for graduate students in mathematics. Specific topics include vector spaces, linear transformations, multilinear forms, and Jordan decomposition. May not receive credit for both MATH 411 and MATH 610. MATH majors only.

subject: MATH
title: Key Elements of Topology
description: Key concepts of Topology for graduate students in mathematics; topological spaces; separation axioms, compactness, connectedness; continuity. MATH majors only.

subject: MATH
title: Topology
description: Properties of topological spaces; separation axioms, compactness, connectedness; metrizability; convergence and continuity. Additional topics from general and algebraic topology. (These topics are covered in the year sequence MATH 621-MATH 622.)

subject: MATH
title: Topology
description: Continuation of MATH 621. This is the second course of a year sequence and should be taken in the same academic year as MATH 621.

subject: MATH
title: Geometric Group Theory
description: Geometric, topological, and dynamical methods in the study of finitely generated infinite groups.
prerequisites: MATH 621 (with a minimum grade of B-).

subject: MATH
title: Differentiable Manifolds I
description: Differentiable structures on manifolds, tensor fields, Frobenius theorem, exterior algebra, integration of forms, Poincare Lemma, Stoke's theorem.

subject: MATH
title: Theory of Functions of a Real Variable
description: Lebesgue measure and integral, convergence of integrals, functions of bounded variation, LebesgueStieltjes integral and more general theory of measure and integration. (These topics are covered in the year sequence 631-632.)

subject: MATH
title: Theory of Functions of a Real Variable
description: Continuation of MATH 631. This is the second course of a year sequence and should be taken in the same academic year as MATH 631.

subject: MATH
title: Functional Analysis
description: Linear topological spaces, normed spaces, Hilbert spaces, function algebras, operator theory.

subject: MATH
title: Calculus of Variations
description: Simple variational problems, first and second variation formulas. Euler-Lagrange equation, direct methods, optimal control.

subject: MATH
title: Analytic Function Theory
description: Conformal mapping, residue theory, series and product developments, analytic continuation, special functions. (These topics are covered in the year sequence MATH 644-MATH 645.)

subject: MATH
title: Analytic Function Theory
description: Continuation of MATH 644. This is the second course of a year sequence and should be taken in the same academic year as MATH 644.

description: and computably enumerable sets; axioms of set theory; ordinals and cardinals.

subject: MATH
title: Directed Reading and Research
description: Axiomatic development, ordinal and cardinal numbers, recursion theorems, axiom of choice, continuum hypothesis, consistency and independence results. Maximum of 3 credit hours.

subject: MATH
title: Thesis Research
description: Recursive, r.e., Ptime, and Logspace classes. Nondeterminism, parallelism, alternation, and Boolean circuits. Reducibility and completeness.

subject: MATH
title: Introduction to Algebraic Number Theory
description: Number fields and rings of integers; primes, factorization, and ramification theory; finiteness of the class group; Dirichlet's Unit Theorem; valuations, completions, and local fields. Further topics.
prerequisites: MATH 611 (with a minimum grade of B-).

subject: MATH
title: Advanced Probability
description: Independence and conditioning, martingales, ergodic theory, Markov chains, central limit theorem.
prerequisites: MATH 631 (with a minimum grade of B) or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: MATH
title: Stochastic Processes
description: Stationary, Gaussian, and Markov processes.
prerequisites: MATH 671 (with a minimum grade of B) or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: MATH
title: Graph Theory
description: Connected graphs and digraphs. Graph embeddings. Connectivity and networks. Factors and factorizations. Coverings. Coloring. Applications. MATH 695 - Directed Reading and Research for Plan B Masters Students - Credits: V Maximum of 3 credit hours. Research for master's thesis.

subject: MATH
title: Apprenticeship in Teaching
description: An experience-based introduction to college-level teaching; students serve as student teachers to professors; responsibilities include supervised teaching and participation in planning and evaluation. Open to graduate students in mathematics only.
major_restrictions: graduate standing in mathematics and consent.

subject: MATH
title: Dissertation Research
description: Research for doctoral dissertation. Mechanical Engineering Preference in registration is given to declared engineering majors.

subject: ME
title: Introduction to Engineering Design I
description: (1 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Introductory experience in analysis, synthesis, and design. Teamwork and project required.

subject: ME
title: Space Exploration
description: Introduction to science and engineering of Solar System exploration with laboratory exercises. Covers planetary science, model-based systems engineering, mission planning, constraints on payload and spacecraft design, human spaceflight technology. Research projects emphasize written communication.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: ME
title: Introduction to Engineering Design
description: (1 Lec, 2 2-hr Lab) Introductory experience in communication, presentation, professional ethics, social responsibility, engineering economics, quality control, and computer-aided drafting. Teamwork and project required.

subject: ME
title: Applied Mechanics II
description: Dynamics of particles and rigid bodies; force, acceleration, impulse-momentum, work-energy.
prerequisites: C or better in CEE 270; MATH 244 (or concurrent) or MATH 253A (or concurrent).
major_restrictions: CE, CNST, ENGS, ME majors only.

subject: ME
title: Space Science and Instrumentation
description: Essential techniques for remote compositional analysis of planets; understanding spectroscopy, mineralogy, and geochemistry of planetary surfaces and their measurement. Design of space flight instrumentation. Significant oral and written communication is required.
prerequisites: EPET 201, or ERTH 101 and ERTH 101L and ERTH 105, or ERTH 101 and ERTH 107; and CHEM 161 and PHYS 272.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: ME
title: Thermodynamics
description: (3 Lec, 1 Discussion) Basic laws, closed and open systems. Work, heat, concept of entropy. Properties of pure simple substances. Ideal gases. Introduction to power and refrigeration cycles.
prerequisites: grade of C or better in all of CHEM 162 (or CHEM 171 or CHEM 181A), PHYS 170 and MATH 244 (or MATH 253A).

subject: ME
title: Mechanics of Fluids and Lab
description: ideal fluids, effects of viscosity. Similitude, boundary layer flow. Measurement techniques in thermodynamics and fluid mechanics. Hands-on experience with instrumentation. Open-ended design of thermos-fluid systems. Emphasis on writing instruction.
prerequisites: grade of C or better in all of ME 311 and CEE 271 (or ME 271).

subject: ME
title: Materials Science and Engineering
description: Electronic, atomic, and crystalline structure of materials and their effect on the mechanical, electrical, optical, and magnetic properties of engineering metals, ceramics, polymers, and composites.
prerequisites: grade of C or better in all of CHEM 162 (or CHEM 171 or CHEM 181A), MATH 242 (or MATH 252A), and PHYS 170.

subject: ME
title: Manufacturing Processes and Lab
description: (3 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Energy requirements for manufacturing methods. Manufacturing methods to obtain components with desired size/shape/properties. Conduct manufacturing experiments. Emphasis on writing instruction.

subject: ME
title: Manufacturing Processes Lab
description: (1 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Manufacturing laboratory: tension/ compression tests, cold rolling, welding, casting, statistical process control, programming and milling using a CNC machine.

subject: ME
title: Computer Methods in Engineering
description: Numerical solutions for algebraic and transcendental equations, simultaneous linear algebraic equations, integration and differentiation; integration of ordinary differential equations. Engineering applications. (3 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Incompressible and compressible
prerequisites: grade of C or better in all of ECE 160 (or ECE 110 or ICS 111), MATH 244 (or MATH 253A), and MATH 302 (or MATH 307).

subject: ME
title: Mechanics of Solids
description: Introduction to analysis and design of deformable bodies subject to loading. Stress, strain, constitutive relation, axially loading, torsion, statically indeterminate systems, Mohr's circle, failure criteria, buckling, defection due to axial, shear, torsional, and flexural loading.
prerequisites: C or better in all of the following: CEE 270; and MATH 244 (or concurrent) or MATH 253A (or concurrent).

subject: ME
title: Component Design
description: Design, analysis, and selection of machine components: shafts, screws, fasteners, welds, rolling contact bearings, journal bearings, gears, clutches, brakes, belts, and roller chains.
prerequisites: ME 213, and either ME 371 or CEE 370.

subject: ME
title: Kinematics/Dynamics Machinery
description: Velocity and acceleration analysis of planar mechanisms; kinematic synthesis of linkages, cams, and gears; static and dynamic force analysis of mechanisms; balancing of machinery.
prerequisites: CEE 271 or ME 271 (C or better), MATH 244 (or MATH 253A)and either MATH 302 or MATH 307.

subject: ME
title: Dynamics of Machines and Systems and Lab
description: (3 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Lumped-parameter modeling of dynamic systems. Methods of analysis, including transform techniques. Time and frequency response. Feedback control. Engineering instrumentation. Data acquisition. Dynamic measurements. Design and testing. Lab report writing.
prerequisites: grade of C or better in all of CEE 271 (or ME 271) and MATH 302 (or MATH 307).

subject: ME
title: Space Mission Design
description: Covers all aspects of spacecraft design, subsystems, science payload, systems engineering, project management, budgets, and ethical issues that are important to consider in producing a fully successful mission.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: ME
title: Dynamics Systems Laboratory
description: (1 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Analysis, design, fabrication, testing and characterization of engineering instrumentation. Computer-based data acquisition methods. Techniques and procedures associated with carrying out dynamic measurements within the constraints of cost, time and accuracy.
prerequisites: one of ME 360, MATH 407, or PHYS 305 (or concurrent for any); and ME 375 (or concurrent).

subject: ME
title: Advanced Mathematics for Engineers
description: Applications of ordinary differential equations, Laplace transform, vector field theory, matrices, line integrals.

subject: ME
title: Computational Fluid Dynamics
description: Basic computational fluid dynamics; four important partial differential equations; introduction to finite element method: Interpolation and Galerkin method; finite element method for transport phenomena; some algorithms for parallel computing.
prerequisites: ME 422 (or concurrent ), and either ME 360, MATH 407 or PHYS 305; or consent.

subject: ME
title: Orbital Mechanics
description: Basic theory of orbits of space objects, including spacecraft, small satellites, planets and small planets, and other fundamentals of astrodynamics and applications to aerospace engineering.
prerequisites: grade of C or better in all of the following: ME 271; ME 375; MATH 244 or MATH 253A; MATH 302 or MATH 307; ECE 160 or ICS 111.

subject: ME
title: Optimal Control for Aerospace Applications
description: Fundamentals of optimal control theory and calculus of variations. Application of this theory to aerospace engineering problems, including mission design problems and a wide range of space maneuvers.

subject: ME
title: Heat Transfer and Lab
description: (3 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Steady and unsteady conduction; steady convection and radiation; heat exchangers. Emphasis on writing instruction.

subject: ME
title: Applied Thermodynamics
description: Gas mixtures, generalized thermodynamic relationships, combustion and thermochemistry, chemical equilibrium, power and refrigeration cycles.
prerequisites: grade of C or better in ME 311.

subject: ME
title: Applied Thermal Engineering
description: Principles, design and analysis of practical thermal systems. Engineering applications. Valve, compressor, condenser and evaporator technologies. System integration and control. Thermal loads and thermal comfort.
prerequisites: ME 422 (or concurrent). Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: ME
title: Power and Propulsion
description: Principles, performance, and design of gas turbine power plants and propulsion systems.

subject: ME
title: Astronautics
description: One-dimensional compressible flow involving change of area, friction, heat transfer. Normal and oblique shocks. Prandtl-Meyer flow. Application to nozzles, diffusers, airfoils.

subject: ME
title: Thermal Management of Electronic Systems
description: To introduce concepts in the thermal management of electronics, and to develop sound technical tools to approach modern electronic packaging and cooling applications.

subject: ME
title: Scaling Methods in Engineering
description: Scaling methods and optimization under global constraints; multi-scale optimal design of mechanical, thermal, and natural systems; effectiveness of heat, fluid, and convective trees; theoretical design optimization of manmade and natural power systems; analysis of time dependent structures.

subject: ME
title: Solar Thermal Engineering
description: Fundamentals of solar energy, solar engineering material characteristics, and solar thermal and photovoltaic systems. Will learn solar thermal system components, and be able to perform simple system design.
grade_option: A-F only. Senior standing or higher.

subject: ME
title: Failures in Materials
description: Analysis of component failures due to imperfections, fatigue, brittle fracture, wear, corrosion, bending, impact, and overload. Fracture mechanics. Case studies.

subject: ME
title: Materials Selection for Design
description: Methodology for the selection of materials for mechanical applications to prevent mechanical failure and environmental degradation. Design considerations associated with the use of metals, ceramics, polymers, and composites.

subject: ME
title: Experimental Methods in Materials Research
description: (1 Lec, 2 2-hr Lab) Common experimental techniques in materials testing and research: x-ray diffraction, optical and electron microscopy, thermal and mechanical properties, electrochemical methods-theory and handson experience.

subject: ME
title: Corrosion Engineering
description: Basics of corrosion processes and emphasis on corrosion control. Thermodynamics and kinetics of corrosion, metal alloys and their behavior, corrosion control techniques (cathodic protection, anodic protection, coatings, and inhibitors).

subject: ME
title: Mechatronics
description: processes and systems; electrical circuits and mechanical components; programming and control; hands on technology; application case studies. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only.
major_restrictions: ME, EE, CE, ICS majors only.

subject: ME
title: Advanced Materials Manufacturing
description: (2 Lec, 1 2-hr Lab) Introduction to anisotropic materials, advanced manufacturing techniques for composite and intelligent materials, joining of composites, thin film processing and stereolithography, computer aided manufacturing and rapid prototyping, manufacturing process optimization, open-ended manufacturing projects.
prerequisites: ME 341, ME 342, and senior standing; or consent.

subject: ME
title: Introduction to Nanotechnology
description: (3 Lec) Tools and techniques of micro- and nanotechnology in design, modeling, simulations, analysis, fabrication, testing and characterization; nanomaterials, nanostructures, nano-composites, nanocoating, nano-optics, nano-electronics and nanobiotechnology.

subject: ME
title: Nanosystem and Microsystem Design
description: Fabrication, design, and analysis of physical systems, sensors, and actuators at the nanoscale and microscale. Microfabrication/nanofabrication, fabrication process design, electrostatistic and electromagnetic interactions, signal transduction, measurements. Course work will focus on process and system design.
prerequisites: ME 331 (with a minimum grade of C-), ME 375 (with a minimum grade of C-), or consent.

subject: ME
title: Research Methods in Engineering
description: Introduces the basic skills of preparing and publishing scientific research. Students will learn how to prepare, write, publish, and present a scientific paper, while working on a research project related to thermofluids.
grade_option: A-F only. Senior standing or higher.

subject: ME
title: Feedback-Control Systems
description: Analysis/ design of feedback systems. Compensator design via root locus and Bode analysis. Routh/Nyquist stability. State space representation and introduction to MIMO formulation. Controllability/observability. Application to physical dynamic systems such as industrial robots.

subject: ME
title: Robotics
description: Principles and design methods for autonomous systems.

subject: ME
title: Energy Conversion Systems
description: Energy conversion and its impact on the environment. Conventional, hydroelectric, nuclear fission and fusion, solar, wind, ocean, geothermal, and biomass power; energy storage, transmission and conservation. Electrochemistry, batteries, fuel cells, flow batteries, electrochemical capacitors, electric vehicles, electrochemical grid storage.
prerequisites: ME 322, ME 411, and ME 422 (or concurrent); or consent.
major_restrictions: EE and ME majors only. ME 466 - Electrochemical Power Sources: Principles, Applications, and Sustainability - Credits: 3

subject: ME
title: Introduction to Nuclear Energy & Engineering
description: Covers the fundamentals of nuclear energy and engineering principles as applied to the operation of nuclear reactors and the design of reactors for electrical power generation.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only.

subject: ME
title: Nuclear Power Engineering
description: Nuclear reactor principles. Reactor heat transfer, heat generation and removal. Design and analysis of reactor power systems and plants.

subject: ME
title: Experimental Stress Analysis
description: (1 3-hr Lab) Techniques of experimental stress analysis: strain and deflection measurement of beams and shafts, strain to stress conversion, principal and maximum shearing stresses, failure in biaxial stress states, stress concentrations, residual stresses, buckling, creep, electrical resistance strain gages, brittle coatings, photoelastic methods, transducers.

subject: ME
title: Mechanical Vibrations
description: Response of machines and systems to transient and periodic excitation. Vibration isolation and transmissibility. Modal analysis of multi-degree-offreedom systems. Applications to design.

subject: ME
title: Fundamentals of Acoustics
description: Plane and spherical acoustic waves. Transmission, reflection, radiation, and absorption. Near and far fields, radiation patterns. Applications to noise control. Instruments.

subject: ME
title: Thermofluid Measurements and Design
description: Measurement techniques in thermodynamics, fluid mechanics, and heat transfer. Hands-on experience with instrumentation. Open-ended design of thermofluid systems. Contemporary engineering ethics issues. Final report and presentation are required.

subject: ME
title: Design Project I
description: (2 1-hr Lec, 2 2-hr Lab) Heuristic application of engineering design process and project planning via a significant, open-ended design project that includes realistic constraints involving economics, environmental sustainability, manufacturability, ethics, health, safety, society, and politics. Extensive written communication required.
prerequisites: ME 322, ME 341, ME 372, and ME 375; or consent.

subject: ME
title: Design Project II
description: (1 Lec, 2 2-hr Lab) Continuation of design project initiated in ME 481. Extension of conceptual design to final design. Manufacturing of prototype: material and part selection, procurement, manufacturing specifications/ drawings. Testing of complex systems. Extensive oral communication required.

subject: ME
title: Research and Experiment Design
description: Introduction to research and experimentation via pursuit and reporting of an open-ended design project. Ethical issues in research and experimentation. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only.
major_restrictions: Engineering, Biological Engineering, and Ocean & Resources Engineering majors only.

subject: ME
title: System Engineering
description: Will cover system engineering design, analysis, and development concepts, requirements derivation/management, and program management processes, such as risk identification/ management and fiscal/schedule tools. Senior standing or higher.
prerequisites: ME 213 (with a minimum grade of C).

subject: ME
title: Pearl Harbor co-op
description: Provides real-world engineering experience through project assignment and working alongside a practicing engineer at the Pearl Harbor Naval Shipyard. Senior standing or higher.
prerequisites: (ME 322, ME 341, ME 372, and ME 375) with a minimum grade of C.

subject: ME
title: (Alpha) Mentored Projects
description: Investigation of advanced problems, making use of faculty expertise for specific domains of work. (B) Design Project I; (C) Design Project II; (D) Research and Experiment Design.
corequisites: ME 481 for (B); ME 482 for (C); ME 483 for (D).

subject: ME
title: Mentored Projects: Design Project I
description: Investigation of advanced problems, making use of faculty expertise for specific domains of work. (B) Design Project I; (C) Design Project II; (D) Research and Experiment Design.
corequisites: ME 481 for (B); ME 482 for (C); ME 483 for (D).

subject: ME
title: Mentored Projects: Design Project II
description: Analysis of principles of operation of renewable energy systems, and its interactions with sustainability. Fundamentals of renewable energy production, storage, and distribution. ME 490D - Mentored Projects: Research and Experiment Design - Credits: 1 Investigation of advanced problems, making use of faculty expertise for specific domains of work. (B) Design Project I; (C) Design Project II; (D) Research and Experiment Design.
corequisites: ME 481 for (B); ME 482 for (C); ME 483 for (D).

subject: ME
title: Special Topics in Mechanical Engineering
description: Specialized topics in thermosciences, mechanics, materials, systems, or design.

subject: ME
title: Special Topics in Mechanical Engineering
description: Specialized topics in thermosciences, mechanics, materials, systems, or design.

subject: ME
title: Project
description: Investigation of advanced problems in mechanical engineering design or development. Student must find faculty sponsor before registering.

subject: ME
title: Advanced Thermodynamics
description: Introduction to general principles of classical thermodynamics. Main topics include equilibrium conditions, thermodynamic relations, Legendre transformations, Maxwell relations, stability of thermodynamic systems, phase transitions, and critical phenomena.

subject: ME
title: Introduction to Statistical Thermodynamics
description: Fundamentals of statistical thermodynamics. Main topics include entropy, Boltzmann law, thermodynamic driving forces, statistical mechanics, chemical equilibria, solutions and mixtures, and applications of statistical thermodynamics in biology, chemistry, physics, and nanoscience.

subject: ME
title: Advanced Aerodynamics
description: Advanced topics in aerodynamics, two- and threedimensional wing theory, slender-body theory, lifting surface methods, vortex and wave drag, analytical and numerical methods, for computing unsteady aerodynamic behavior and introduction to flightdynamics.

subject: ME
title: Thermal Environmental Engineering
description: Physiological response to thermal environment, designs of passive and active cooling systems, student project.

subject: ME
title: Boiling and Two-Phase Flow
description: Two-phase flow pattern and flow pattern maps; twophase flow models (homogeneous, separate, drift flux, annular); laminar and turbulent film condensations; boiling incipience; pool boiling heat transfer; flow boiling heat transfer; critical heat flux (CHF).
prerequisites: ME 422, and either MATH 190 or ECE 160.

subject: ME
title: Viscous Flows
description: Formulation and properties of the Navier-Stokes equations; exact solutions; creeping flows; lubrication theory; laminar boundary layers; laminar stability, and transition to turbulence; turbulent boundary layers.

subject: ME
title: Biological Fluid Mechanics
description: Review of Newtonian fluid mechanics. Blood rheology and flow in elastic tubes. Murray's Law and pulsatile flow propagation. Microcirculation dynamics and biological transport. Aquatic movement and comparative biological examples. ME 627 - Environmental Heat, Mass, and Momentum Transfer - Credits: 3

subject: ME
title: Conduction Heat Transfer
description: Heat and mass transfer in Earth's interior with applications to geothermal and petroleum reservoir engineering.

subject: ME
title: Chemical Reactor Analysis and Design
description: Chemical kinetics with heat and mass transfer. Balance equations applied to selected ideal reactors. Departures from ideality.

subject: ME
title: Convection Heat Transfer
description: Heat transfer in laminar and turbulent boundary layers. Analogy between heat, momentum, mass transfer.

subject: ME
title: Renewable Energy Engineering I: Biomass
description: (1 Lec, 2 2-hr Lab) Experimental methods and theory of thermochemical biomass conversion: static and dynamic temperature and mass measurements, thermogravimetry, differential scanning calorimetry, GCMS and HPLC techniques.

subject: ME
title: Radiation Heat Transfer
description: Radiant interchange among surfaces. Gaseous radiation. Combined conduction, convection, and radiation heat transfer.

subject: ME
title: Microfluidics and Nanofluidics
description: Introduction to fundamental understanding of fluid mechanics and transport phenomena at micronanoscale; electrokinetics; chemical separation; colloids and emulsions; biophysics; micronanofabrication. ME 625 - Numerical Methods in Fluid Mechanics and Heat Transfer - Credits: 3

subject: ME
title: Advanced Materials Science
description: experimental techniques.

subject: ME
title: Advanced Transport Phenomena
description: Theory of momentum, heat, and mass transfer processes. Integral and differential conservation equations, constitutive laws, and interfacial boundary conditions. Dimensional analysis, perturbation theory, asymptotics, similarity, and Green's function methodology. Applications to problems in engineering.
class_standing: Graduate students only. ME 633 - Li-ion Battery Mechanistic Modeling: Principles and State of the Art - Credits: 3 Electrochemistry, Li-ion batteries, electric vehicles, electrochemical grid storage.

subject: ME
title: Corrosion Theory
description: Application of electrochemical theory and materials science to corrosion and oxidation reactions. Effect of environment, especially marine. Cathodic protection, coatings, inhibitors, treatment of water systems.

subject: ME
title: Fundamentals of Electrochemistry
description: Thermodynamics of cells, electrode kinetics, mass transfer by migration and diffusion, microelectrode techniques, forced convection, impedance, double-layer structure, and absorbed intermediates in electrode processes.
prerequisites: consent. ME 642 - Mechanical Behavior of Engineering Materials - Credits: 3

subject: ME
title: Clinically Driven Design and Development
description: Exploration of simple, cost-effective alternatives in medicine through different stages of concept generation, design analysis, and prototype validation and investigation of their commercialization potential.

subject: ME
title: Mechanics and Design Composites
description: Introduction to composites; anisotropic elasticity and laminate theory; hygrothermal effects; composite beams, columns, rods, plates, and shells; energy method; failure theories; joining of composites, computer-aided design in composites.

subject: ME
title: Nanoscience and Nanotechnology
description: Science and applications of nanotechnology. Synthesis of nanostructures; nanoscale structure characterization by electron microscopy and Raman spectroscopy; electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties of nanostructures; fabrication of nanodevices; energy, environmental, and biological applications of nanomaterials.

subject: ME
title: Nanosystems
description: Fabrication, design, and analysis of physical systems, sensors, and actuators at the nanoscale and microscale, including electrostatic and electromagnetic interactions, signal transduction, and measurements. Course work will focus on literature review and integration of current research. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: ME
title: Surface Phenomena
description: Fundamental and modern concepts of colloid and surface science. Main topics include surface thermodynamics, capillarity and wetting phenomena, surface forces, surfactants, and particles.

subject: ME
title: Automatic Control
description: Linear optimal feedback control, discrete time optimal control, fundamentals of adaptive control, application to motion and force control of robot arms and manipulators.

subject: ME
title: Guidance, Navigation and Control
description: Develop modern and advanced techniques in the design, development, and implementation of guidance, navigation, and control (GNC) systems for flight vehicles and other controllable dynamical systems.
prerequisites: grade of C or better in all of the following: ME 271 or CEE 271; ME 375; MATH 244 or MATH 253A; MATH 302 or MATH 307; or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: ME
title: Autonomous Marine Systems
description: Identifies the unique challenges faced by autonomous underwater and surface vehicles, and analyzes approaches to address those challenges. Topics include hydrodynamic vehicle modeling, propeller theory, subsystem integration, and motion control strategies.
prerequisites: MATH 307 or MATH 311 (or equivalent), and ECE 351 or ME 451 (or equivalent), or instructor consent.

subject: ME
title: Introduction to Fuel Cell Technology
description: Working principles of all major fuel cell types; fundamentals of proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cells; state-of-the-art theoretical models and diagnostic technologies for PEM fuel cells.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: ME
title: Continuum Mechanics
description: Cartesian tensors in mechanics, coordinate transformations, analysis of stress and strain, principal values, invariants, equilibrium and compatibility equations, constitutive relations, field equations. Problems in elasticity. Recommended: ME 371 or CEE 370, or consent.

subject: ME
title: Finite Element Analysis
description: Introduction to finite element analysis and design in mechanical engineering. Applications to machine design, vibrations, elasticity, heat transfer.

subject: ME
title: Advanced Dynamics
description: Lecture on rigid-body dynamics. Topics include: dynamical systems; motion representation and constraints; Newtonian mechanics; Lagrangian mechanics; Hamilton's principle; stability analysis; introduction to multibody dynamics.

subject: ME
title: High Growth Entrepreneurship
description: An interdisciplinary (JD-MBA) course examining legal, business, and technology issues related to building high growth companies. Student teams develop company feasibility reports and skills necessary to advise or build high growth businesses. Recommended: LAW 531. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year
major_restrictions: Law, MBA, and ME students only.

subject: ME
title: Air Pollution, Toxics, and Control
description: Chemical mechanisms of formation and destruction of major air pollutants, transport phenomena, and health and environmental impacts. Evolution of source and control technologies. Current and pending regulations and policies.
major_restrictions: CE, ECE, ME, and OE majors only.

subject: ME
title: Seminar
description: Current problems in all branches of mechanical engineering. All graduate students are required to attend; registrants are expected to present talks.

subject: ME
title: Advanced Topics in Mechanical Engineering
description: Highly specialized topics in thermosciences, mechanics, materials, system, or design. Medical Education
major_restrictions: candidacy for PhD in mechanical engineering.

subject: MDED
title: Evidence-Based Medicine
description: Critical appraisal track designed to improve the student's ability to seek and evaluate new medical knowledge.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: MDED
title: Unit 8 Block Selective
description: Consolidates knowledge of the foundational sciences emphasized in the first 2 years. Students will also attend lectures, workshops, simulations/labs, and clinical experiences that will help to prepare them for their clinical clerkships

subject: MDED
title: Clinical Skills Assessment
description: Required comprehensive inter-department multidisciplinary assessment program for fourth-year medical students.
prerequisites: FMCH 531, MED 531, OBGN 531, PED 531, PSTY 531, SURG 531 or FMCH 532, MED 532, OBGN 532, PED 532, PSTY 532, SURG 532.

subject: ME
title: Thesis Research
description: Thesis for degree of MS in mechanical engineering.
prerequisites: admission to candidacy and consent of thesis advisor.

subject: ME
title: Directed Instruction
description: Student assists in undergraduate classroom and/or project instruction under the direction and close supervision of faculty member.
prerequisites: admission to PhD candidacy or consent.

subject: ME
title: Dissertation Research
description: Research for doctoral dissertation.

subject: MDED
title: (Alpha) Senior Interdisciplinary Electives
description: Fourth-year elective in which students study selected interdisciplinary topics. (B) medical education elective; (C) complementary and alternative medicine; (D) leadership in underserved care; (E) Junior PBL course director. interdisciplinary topics. (B) medical education elective; (C) complementary and alternative medicine; (D) leadership in underserved care; (E) Junior PBL course director. 532, PED 532, PSTY 532, SURG 532.
prerequisites: FMCH 531, MED 531, OBGN 531, PED 531, PSTY 531, SURG 531 or FMCH 532, MED 532, OBGN
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only for (D) and (E)

subject: MDED
title: MD 1 Health and Illness
description: Introduction to concepts of health and disease through lectures, laboratories, and colloquia intended to broaden the learning from MD 1 health care problems in tutorials.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: MDED
title: MD 1 Health and Illness Tutorials
description: Introductory series of problem-based learning tutorials for first-year medical students focusing on concepts of health and disease through MD 1 health care problems.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: MDED
title: MD 2 CV/Pulmonary
description: Concepts focusing on cardiovascular and pulmonary systems through integrated basic science lectures, laboratories, and colloquia intended to broaden the learning from MD 2 health care problems in tutorials.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: MDED
title: MD 2 CV/Pulmonary Tutorials
description: Advanced series of problem-based learning tutorials for first-year medical students focusing on cardiovascular and pulmonary health care problems.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: MDED
title: MD 3 Renal/Hematology
description: MDED 556L - MD 6 Locomotor System, Nervous System, and Behavioral Problems - Credits: 6 Advanced series of problem-based learning tutorials for second-year medical students focusing on neurological/locomotor systems and behavioral health care problems.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: MDED
title: MD 3 Renal/Hematology Tutorials
description: Advanced series of problem-based learning tutorials for first-year medical students focusing on renal and he553lmatology health care problems.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: MDED
title: MD 7 The Life Cycle
description: Concepts focusing on the life cycle through integrated basic science lectures, laboratories, and colloquia intended to broaden the learning from MD 7 health care problems in tutorials.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: MDED
title: MD 4 GI/Endocrine
description: Concepts focusing on gastrointestinal and endocrine systems through integrated basic science lectures, laboratories, and colloquia intended to broaden the learning from MD 4 health care problems in tutorials.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: MDED
title: MD 4 GI/Endocrine Tutorials
description: Advanced series of problem-based learning tutorials for first-year medical students focusing on gastrointestinal and endocrine health care problems.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: MDED
title: MD 7 The Life Cycle Tutorials
description: Advanced series of problem-based learning tutorials for second-year medical students focusing on the life cycle health care problems.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: MDED
title: Third Year Colloquia
description: A series of lecture-discussions intended to broaden the perspectives of the Unit VI experiences and health-care problems.

subject: MDED
title: (Alpha) Senior Seminars
description: Review of topics and issues that will prepare senior students for the end of their undergraduate training and the start of their internship. (B) Week 1; (C) Week 2; (D) Week 3.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only for B

subject: MDED
title: Senior Seminars: Week 1
description: Review of topics and issues that will prepare senior students for the end of their undergraduate training and the start of their internship. (B) Week 1; (C) Week 2; (D) Week 3.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only for B

subject: MDED
title: Senior Seminars: Week 2
description: Review of topics and issues that will prepare senior students for the end of their undergraduate training and the start of their internship. (B) Week 1; (C) Week 2; (D) Week 3.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only for B

subject: MDED
title: Senior Seminars: Week 3
description: Review of topics and issues that will prepare senior students for the end of their undergraduate training and the start of their internship. (B) Week 1; (C) Week 2; (D) Week 3.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only for B

subject: MDED
title: Introduction to Clinical Skills
description: Introductory lectures and laboratories on history taking and physical exam skills.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: MDED
