subject: MICR
title: General Microbiology
description: Role of microorganisms; how they affect people, property, and the environment. A basic survey course covering broad aspects of biochemistry, genetics, molecular biology, and physiology; host-parasite relationships, public health, bacterial, mycotic and viral diseases; epidemiology; ecology of soils and water; environmental pollution; food microbiology; industrial applications at an introductory level. Not open to those with credit in MICR 351 or equivalent.

subject: MICR
title: Microbiology Laboratory
description: (2 3-hr Lab) Laboratory to accompany MICR 130 . Microbiological techniques and cultivation of microorganisms. Instruction in lab safety. Primarily for students in nursing and dental hygiene.

subject: MICR
title: Research Ethics
description: Introduction to the ethical issues faced by individuals and institutions involved in scientific research. Based on case studies, students will discuss and write about ethical issues in research. Issues include humans and animals in research, mentoring, authorship, ownership of data, genetic technologies and record keeping. This course is designed for students with majors in the natural sciences.
prerequisites: BIOL 171 (or concurrent), or MATH 307 (or concurrent), or MATH 311 (or concurrent), or PHYS 170 (or concurrent), or CHEM 272 (or concurrent); or consent.

subject: MICR
title: Biology of Microorganisms
description: and environmental interactions of microorganisms.
prerequisites: BIOL 171 or equivalent, CHEM 272/CHEM 272L; or consent.

subject: MICR
title: Biology of Microorganisms Lab
description: (2 3-hr Lab) Laboratory exercises to accompany MICR 351.

subject: MICR
title: Introductory Bioinformatics
description: (3 Lec, 1 3-hr Lab) Introductory bioinformatics will provide a basic foundation of biological information (DNA, protein, genome and proteome) by using information technology (IT).
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: MICR
title: Marine Microbiology
description: Evolution, ecology, biochemistry, genetics and physiology of marine bacteria by examining defined systems and organisms.
prerequisites: BIOL 275/BIOL 275L and BIOL 301 (or concurrent)/BIOL 301L (or concurrent), and OCN 201; or MICR 351/MICR 351L; or consent.

subject: MICR
title: Marine Microbiology Laboratory
description: (1 3-hr Lab) Laboratory to accompany MICR 401.
prerequisites: BIOL 275/BIOL 275L and BIOL 301 (or concurrent)/BIOL 301L (or concurrent) and OCN 201; or MICR 351/MICR 351L; and MICR 401 (or concurrent); or consent.

subject: MICR
title: Microbial Physiology
description: Fundamental physiological and metabolic processes of bacteria; emphasis on growth, functions of cell structures, varieties of energy metabolism, metabolic regulation, and differentiation at the prokaryote level.

subject: MICR
title: Microbial Physiology Lab
description: (2 3-hr Lab) Components and metabolism of the bacterial cell; emphasis on techniques of analysis of metabolism and molecular structure.

subject: MICR
title: Immunology
description: Structure and biological actions of antigens and antibodies; fundamentals of antibody synthesis; the relation of immunology to biology and medical sciences.
prerequisites: MICR 351 or BIOL 171, or consent. Recommended: BIOL 275/BIOL 275L.

subject: MICR
title: Immunology Lab
description: (2 3-hr Lab) Basic exercises and experiments in immunology, immunochemistry, immuno-biology to illustrate principles of MICR 461.

subject: MICR
title: Microbiology of Pathogens
description: Host-parasite relationships in microbial diseases of humans and animals with emphasis on bacterial pathogens.

subject: MICR
title: Microbiology of Pathogens Lab
description: (2 3-hr Lab) Characterization of bacterial pathogens. Isolation, identification, and diagnosis. This course develops written communication skills.

subject: MICR
title: Bacterial Molecular Pathogenesis
description: diseases or pathogenesis at the molecular level. Emphasis on bacterial virulence and host-pathogen interactions.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: MICR
title: Bacterial Genetics
description: Genetic analysis and molecular basis of transmission replication, mutation, and expression of heritable characteristics in prokaryotes.

subject: MICR
title: Bacterial Genetics Lab
description: (2 3-hr Lab) Techniques for study of transfer and expression of prokaryotic genes: transformation, conjugation, transposon mutagenesis, preparation and analysis of plasmid and chromosomal DNA. This course develops written communication skills.

subject: MICR
title: Microbes and Their Environment
description: Distribution, diversity, and roles of microorganisms in terrestrial, freshwater, and marine ecosystems. Importance of bacteria in pesticide degradation, bioremediation of oil spills, sewage treatment, biocontrol, food fermentation. Fundamental mechanisms of bacterial infectious

subject: MICR
title: Microbes and Their Environment Lab
description: (2 3-hr Lab) Techniques for study of interaction of microorganisms with and within their natural habitats; symbiosis between microorganisms and plants and animals; role of microorganisms in element cycling; food fermentation by bacteria.

subject: MICR
title: Research Ethics
description: Introduction to ethical issues faced by individuals and institutions involved in scientific research. Moral reasoning, humans and animals in research, mentoring, authorship, ownership of data and genetic technologies.

title: Advanced Immunology

prerequisites: graduate standing; undergraduates that have taken MICR 475 may register with consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: spring

subject: MICR
title: Advances in Microbial Ecology

prerequisites: PEPS 606 (with a minimum grade of B or better) or consent.

subject: MICR
title: Special Topics in Microbiology

subject: MICR
title: Dissertation Research
description: MICR 685 - Molecular and Cellular Bacterial Pathogenesis - Credits: 3 Detailed examination of the molecular and cellular mechanisms of bacterial pathogenesis. Overview of key literature, synthesis of scientific problems into research proposals.
prerequisites: MICR 431, MICR 463, or MICR 470; or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: spring

subject: MICR
title: Seminar
description: Required of graduate students. Military Science and Leadership A weekly two-hour leadership laboratory is required for courses 200 and above. This laboratory is optional only for the 100-level labs associated with MSL 101 and MSL 102, unless otherwise specified. The minimum required grade for prerequisites is a grade of C, except as noted.

subject: MSL
title: Introduction to Physical Fitness
description: Hands-on participatory course following the Army's physical fitness program. Classes conducted three days per week with Army ROTC cadets. Focus is on aerobic conditioning, muscular strength and endurance.

subject: MSL
title: Introduction to Military Science I
description: Introduces cadets to personal challenges and competencies critical for effective leadership; personal development of life skills such as goal setting, time management, physical fitness, and stress management related to leadership, officership, and the Army profession. Focus on developing basic knowledge and comprehension of Army Leadership Dimensions while understanding the ROTC program, its purpose in the Army, and its advantages for the student.

subject: MSL
title: Introduction to Military Science I Lab
description: are highlighted and applied to practical Army leadership tasks and situations. Cadets develop greater self awareness as they practice communication and team building skills, and tactics in real world scenarios. Provides a smooth transition to MSL 301.

subject: MSL
title: ROTC Basic Camp
description: Practical application in adventure training, Army field craft, rifle marksmanship, land navigation, drill and ceremonies, physical training. (2 Lec, 2-hr Lab) Challenges cadets to study, practice, and evaluate adaptive leadership skills with demands of the ROTC Advanced Camp. Challenging scenarios related to small unit tactical operations will develop selfawareness and critical thinking skills. Cadets will receive systematic, specific feedback on their leadership abilities, and analyze/evaluate their leadership values, attributes, skills and actions.

subject: MSL
title: Introduction to Military Science II
description: Overviews leadership fundamentals such as setting direction, problem-solving, listening, presenting briefs, providing feedback and using effective writing skills. Explores leadership values, attributes, skills, and actions in the context of practical, hands-on, and interactive exercises. Cadre role models and building stronger relationships among cadets through common experience and practical interaction are critical.

subject: MSL
title: Introduction to Military Science II Lab
description: Practical application in adventure training, Army field craft, rifle marksmanship, land navigation, drill and ceremonies, physical training.

subject: MSL
title: Intermediate Military Science I
description: Explores creative and innovative tactical leadership strategies and styles through historical case studies and engaging in interactive student exercises. Cadets practice aspects of personal motivation and team building by planning, executing, and assessing team exercises. Focus is on continued development of leadership values and attributes through understanding of rank, uniform, customs and courtesies.

subject: MSL
title: Intermediate Military Science II
description: Challenges of leading complex, contemporary operational environments. Dimensions of cross-cultural challenges of leadership in a constantly changing world

subject: MSL
title: Leading Small Organizations I
prerequisites: MSL 101, MSL 102, MSL 201, and MSL 202; or MSL 203; or consent.

subject: MSL
title: Leading Small Organizations II
description: (2 Lec, 2-hr Lab) Intense situational leadership challenges to build awareness and skills in leading small units. Decision-making, persuading, and motivating team members under fire are explored, evaluated, and developed. Military operations are reviewed to prepare for the ROTC Advance Camp. Cadets apply principles of Law of Land Warfare, Army training, and motivation to troop leading procedures; and are evaluated on what they know and do as leaders.

subject: MSL
title: ROTC Advanced Camp
description: Five-week summer field training exercise conducted at Fort Knox, Kentucky. Arduous and intensified leadership training is conducted throughout the five-week period. Required for U.S. Army commissioning.

subject: MSL
title: History of Military Warfare
description: Lecture/ discussion on the art and science of warfare concentrating on U.S. military history from the Colonial Period to present. Generally restricted to Army ROTC students, requiring twenty pages of graded writing assignments.

subject: MSL
title: Directed Reading and Research
description: Limited to military science students who have had at least one previous military science course for which a grade of B or higher was earned and a cumulative GPA of 2.0 or better.

subject: MSL
title: Advanced Military Research
description: Directs the student to conduct detailed research on a military topic and present to the department leadership plus assist MSL 400 series students on a battle analysis. Molecular and Cell Biology
major_restrictions: Must be in Military Science and Leadership Program or Military Service member in junior or greater standing.

subject: MSL
title: Fundamentals of Leadership
description: Introduces students to the fundamentals of leadership. Activities challenge students to connect theory to practice, develop positive relationships through application of effective leadership concepts with emphasis on teaching writing for personal development and team-building.

subject: MSL
title: Leadership Challenges and Goal Setting
description: (3 Lec, 2-hr Lab) Develops proficiency in planning, execution, and assessment of complex operations; students will learn to assess risk, make ethical decisions, function as a staff member, and provide leadership performance feedback to subordinates; emphasis on oral communication.

subject: MSL
title: Transition to Lieutenant
description: (3 Lec, 2-hr Lab) Explores dynamics of leading in complex situations of current military operations. Examines differences in customs and courtesies, military law, principles of war, and rules of engagement in the face of international terrorism. Interaction with non-government organizations, civilians on the battlefield, and host nation support are examined and evaluated. Case studies, scenarios, and What Now, Lieutenant? exercises prepare cadets to lead as commissioned officers in the U.S. Army.
prerequisites: C (not C-) or better in BIOL 171/BIOL 171L and CHEM 272.

subject: MCB
title: Research Ethics
description: Introduction to the ethical issues faced by individuals and institutions involved in scientific research. Based on case studies, students will discuss and write about ethical issues in research. Issues include humans and animals in research, mentoring, authorship, ownership of data, genetic technologies and record keeping. This course is designed for students with majors in the natural sciences.
prerequisites: BIOL 171 (or concurrent), or MATH 307 (or concurrent), or MATH 311 (or concurrent), or PHYS 170 (or concurrent), or CHEM 272 (or concurrent); or consent.

subject: MCB
title: Molecular Cell Biology I
description: Relationship between structure and function at macromolecular level.
prerequisites: C (not C-) or better in BIOL 275/BIOL 275L and CHEM 273, or consent.

subject: MCB
title: Bacterial Genetics
description: Genetic analysis and molecular basis of transmission replication, mutation, and expression of heritable characteristics in prokaryotes.

subject: MCB
title: Immunology
description: Structure and biological actions of antigens and antibodies; fundamentals of antibody synthesis; the relation of immunology to biology and medical sciences.
prerequisites: MICR 351 or BIOL 171; or consent. Recommended: BIOL 275/BIOL 275L.

subject: MCB
title: Immunology Lab
description: (2 3-hr Lab) Basic exercises and experiments in immunology, immunochemistry, immuno-biology to illustrate principles of MCB 461.

subject: MCB
title: The Biology of Cancer
description: Integrative study of the molecular basis of cancer, progression of disease, current treatments, and ethical implications of research. Analysis of published studies through written and small group discussions. Structured training in writing scientific review papers.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: MCB
title: Bacterial Genetics Lab
description: (2 3-hr Lab) Techniques for study of transfer and expression of prokaryotic genes: transformation, conjugation, transposon mutagenesis, preparation and analysis of plasmid and chromosomal DNA. This course develops written communication skills. Molecular Biosciences and Bioengineering The minimum required grade for prerequisites is a grade of C or better, unless otherwise specified.

subject: MBBE
title: Biotechnology: Science and Ethical Issues
description: Introduction to the concepts, goals, ethical issues and consequences of biotechnology using real-life case studies of GMOs, cloning, DNA fingerprinting, gene therapy and genetical engineering.
prerequisites: BIOL 171 (with a minimum grade of C) or consent.

subject: MBBE
title: Essential Biochemistry
description: Introduction to basic concepts of cellular biochemistry and metabolic pathways as applied to nutritional, medicinal and environmental biochemistry.
prerequisites: (CHEM 152 or CHEM 272) with a minimum grade of C, or consent.

subject: MBBE
title: Molecular Biotechnology
description: General principles, applications, and recent advances of the rapidly growing science of biotechnology. Topics include impact of biotechnology on medicine, animal sciences, environment, agriculture, forensics, and economic and socio-ethical issues.
prerequisites: MBBE 304, or MBBE 375, or BIOL 275L, or BIOL 375L; or consent.

subject: MBBE
title: Principles of Biochemistry
description: Molecular basis of living processes in bacteria, plants and animals; emphasis on metabolism of carbohydrates, lipids, proteins and nucleic acids.
prerequisites: C (not C-) or better in BIOL 275/BIOL 275L, CHEM 272 and CHEM 273; or consent.

subject: MBBE
title: Principles of Biochemistry Lab
description: (1 Lec, 1 3-hr Lab) Principles and techniques of biochemical laboratory. Teaches biochemistry laboratory methods as well as technical writing in the form of experimental reports.
gen_ed: DY MBBE 405 - Marine Functional Ecology and Biotechnology - Credits: 3 Marine functional genomics, biodiversity of marine natural habitats, marine microbial communities and their ecological functions, interactions of marine microbes and their host, climate change and marine biodiversity, marine biotechnology.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: MBBE
title: Molecular Cellular Biology II
description: Cell structure and function. Structure, chemistry, and functions of organelles and macromolecules.
prerequisites: C (not C-) or better in BIOL 407; or consent.

subject: MBBE
title: Food Engineering
description: Principles and applications of thermodynamics, electricity, fluid mechanics, heat transfer, psychrometry, and material and energy balances of food processing and preservation.
prerequisites: (BIOL 171, CHEM 162 or CHEM 171 or CHEM 181A, MATH 243 or MATH 253A, PHYS 151 or PHYS 170) with a minimum grade of C; or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: MBBE
title: Environmental Biochemistry
description: Biochemical and chemical principles of occurrence, distribution, biotic and abiotic conversion, fate, and impact of synthetic and natural molecules in the environment. Important pollutants will be used as case studies to illustrate the principles.
prerequisites: (ECE 160, ECE 211, and BE 350 or MATH 302 or MATH 307 or ECE 326) with a minimum grade of C; or consent.

subject: MBBE
title: Artificial Intelligence for Bioprocesses
description: hands-on coding, delves into how machine learning can be used to generate practical insights.
prerequisites: (MATH 215 or MATH 241 or MATH 251A and ECE 110 or ECE 160 or ICS 111) with a minimum grade of C; or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only.

subject: MBBE
title: Synthetic Biology
description: Introduction to principles, tools, and applications of synthetic biology; molecular techniques and design/analysis of synthetic gene circuits, syntheticbiology parts/devices, CRISPR-based systems, engineered microbial cell factories, for industrial, agricultural, medical applications.
prerequisites: (PHYS 152 (or PHYS 272), BIOL 275/BIOL 275L) with a minimum grade of C; or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: fall

subject: MBBE

prerequisites: ME 322 (with a minimum grade of C-) or BE 373 or CEE 320; or consent.

prerequisites: MBBE 402/BIOL 402 (with a minimum grade of B or higher), or with consent from instructor. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: fall

prerequisites: graduate student status with undergraduate courses in organic chemistry, microbiology, additional biological science, physics, and biochemistry. Basic knowledge of food science is expected; or consent.

subject: MUS
title: Integrating World Music in Education
description: Application of musical concepts through an integrated approach. This includes strategies, world music, literature, materials and resources for the classroom curriculum.
prerequisites: four semesters of MUS 232 and promotion by board examination.

subject: MUS
title: Electronic Music
description: Basic techniques of electronic sound synthesis.

subject: MUS
title: Audio Recording/Mixing/Sound Design
description: Preproduction, mixing, setup and recording suitable for film. Mixing techniques in software for small systems. Sound design using original synthesis techniques to enhance film and support stories.
prerequisites: MUS 240 or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: MUS
title: General Music Methods Lab
description: Observing, analyzing, participating, and teaching in general music classrooms. Supervised 40 hours of field experiences required.
prerequisites: MUS 250, MUS 286, and MUS 288; or consent.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): (Every 3rd semester)

subject: MUS
title: Instrumental Music Methods
description: Objectives, materials, and procedures of instrumental music in schools.

subject: MUS
title: Marching Band Design and Techniques
description: Design and techniques for the modern field marching band.
prerequisites: MUS 250, MUS 286, and MUS 326 (or concurrent); or consent.
repeatable: 1 time, up to 4 credits. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: MUS
title: Instrumental Music Methods Lab
description: Observing, analyzing, participating, and teaching in instrumental settings. Supervised 30 hours of field experiences required.
prerequisites: MUS 250, MUS 286, and MUS 326 (or concurrent); or consent.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): (Every 3rd semester)

subject: MUS
title: Choral Music Methods
description: Objectives, materials, and procedures of choral music in schools.
prerequisites: MUS 122B, MUS 250, MUS 286, and MUS 326 (or concurrent); or consent.

subject: MUS
title: Choral Music Methods Lab
description: Observing, analyzing, participating, and teaching in choral settings. Supervised 30 hours of field experiences required.
prerequisites: MUS 250, MUS 286, and MUS 326 (or concurrent); or consent.
grade_option: CR/ NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): (Every 3rd semester)

subject: MUS
title: Piano Pedagogy
description: Concepts, materials, and procedures for class and individual instruction in piano. Significant attention paid to developing writing skills.

subject: MUS
title: Superstars: A History of Musical Celebrity
description: Explores how the cultural phenomenon of celebrity has impacted musicians and composers from antiquity to the present.

subject: MUS
title: Music in Modern America
description: Varieties of music, including jazz and other popular forms; relevant antecedents.
class_standing: sophomore standing; freshmen with consent only.

subject: MUS
title: Counterpoint
description: Form, texture, and style in music literature from Renaissance to present. Formal analysis and writing. Contrapuntal textures and forms.

subject: MUS
title: Piano Pedagogy
description: Continuation of MUS 358. Significant attention paid to developing writing skills.

subject: MUS
title: Music in Film
description: Lecture investigating the role of music in narrative film, and developing critical skills through close study of films and their music. Understanding styles and techniques of film music within larger aesthetic trends and historical contexts. Form, texture, and style in music literature from Renaissance to present. Formal analysis and writing. Larger forms with various textures; recent contemporary approaches to continuity.
gen_ed: DH MUS 362 - Curtain Up! Broadway Musicals, Then and Now - Credits: 3 Traces the history of the Broadway musical in a survey of works from the mid-1800s through the "Hamilton" phenomenon, exploring their developmental process, structure, and sociocultural, religious, and political contexts. Course develops written communication

subject: MUS
title: Orchestration
description: Basic principles of scoring for orchestra and band; instrumental ranges, timbres, transpositions; transcribing or composing for band, orchestra, and chorus.

subject: MUS
title: Theory and Practice of Jazz Improvisation
description: Development of an improvising technique through analysis and performance practice. For instrumentalists only.

subject: MUS
title: Directed Study
description: Limited to majors with a minimum cumulative GPA of 2.7 or a minimum GPA of 3.0 in music.

subject: MUS
title: Topics in Music
description: Topics in history, literature, theory, applied music, music education, and ethnomusicology; for music majors. Consult department for topics and specific dates.
prerequisites: MUS 281 and appropriate lower division music courses; or consent.

subject: MUS
title: Music Cultures of the World
description: Folk, popular, and art music from major regions of the world, with emphasis upon Asia and the Pacific, representative styles and regional characteristics.

subject: MUS
title: (Alpha) Ensembles
description: Performance of literature for ensembles and performing groups of various sizes and kinds; (B) Hawaiian chorus; (C) University Chamber Singers; (D) piano-vocal collaboration; (E) composer-performer collaboration; (F) chamber music; (G) guitar; (K) jazz; (M) contemporary music; (N) theater music; (O) percussion; (P) digital and electronic musical arts; (Q) piano-instrumental collaboration; (R) saxophone choir.
prerequisites: 2 credits of MUS 289, or 4 credits of MUS 232, or consent for (E); audition or consent for all other alphas.
grade_option: A-F only for (E), (Q), and (R). Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only for (R)
repeatable: Unlimited times, repeatable five times for (R).

subject: MUS
title: Ensembles: Hawaiian chorus
description: music; (N) theater music; (O) percussion; (P) digital and electronic musical arts; (Q) piano-instrumental collaboration; (R) saxophone choir.

Baccalaureate Program.

subject: NHH
title: Humanities in Medicine II
description: Continuation of NHH 501.

subject: NHH
title: Medical Biology I
description: (2 Lec, 2 Tutorial, 1 3-hr Lab) Foundation in medical sciences, including anatomy, physiology, microbiology, pathology, and pharmacology (focus on respiratory, cardiovascular, and urinary systems); develop critical thinking and problem-solving skills. Includes lectures, problem-based tutorials, and histology and gross anatomy labs.
prerequisites: admission to Imi Hoʻola PostBaccalaureate Program.

subject: NHH
title: Medical Biology II
description: (2 Lec, 2 Tutorial, 1 3-hr Lab) Continuation of NHH 503.

subject: NHH
title: Applied Health Disparities Research
description: Instruction on social determinants of health, issues of health equity, and how these issues can be addressed through research. Discussions and activities promote a greater understanding of health disparities research.

subject: NHH
title: Directed Reading/Research
description: Individual reading and/or research.

subject: NHH
title: Humanities in Medicine I
description: Introduction to medical science stressing key historical figures and significant events to exemplify the development of scientific methods. Special emphasis on writing skills, health team concept, and culture and contributions of the Pacific. Continuation of NHH 505.

subject: NHH
title: Native Hawaiian Health Care
description: Clinical experience in the Native Hawaiian Health Care System and community health care centers, incorporating both western and traditional Hawaiian medicine. Open to fourth year medical students interested in Native Hawaiian Health Care. NHH 650 - Advanced Applied Health Disparities Research - Credits: V Instruction on social determinants of health, issues of health equity, and how these issues can be addressed through research. Discussions and activities promote a greater understanding of health disparities research.
major_restrictions: MD majors only. NHH 599 - Directed Reading/Research in Native Hawaiian Health - Credits: V Instruction of medical students in the social determinants of health, issues of health equity, specifically in Native Hawaiians, Pacific Islanders and/or disadvantaged people and teach them how these issues can be addressed through research.
class_standing: Graduate standing only. NHH 595 - Clinical Skills Preceptorship in the Lau Ola Clinic of the Department of Native Hawaiian Health Credits: V Provides a focused experience in clinical medicine with opportunities to practice the history and physical examination in Lau Ola, the clinical setting of the Department of Native Hawaiian Health.

subject: NHH
title: Directed Reading/Research
description: Individual reading and/or research. Natural Resources and Environmental Management

subject: NREM
title: Environmental Service Learning I
description: Experiential service learning course for students participating in the Hawaiʻi Youth Conservation Corps summer program as team members and leaders.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only
major_restrictions: Hawaiʻi Youth Conservation Corps summer program students only. NREM 192 - Introduction to Natural Resources and Environmental Management - Credits: 1 Introduction to NREM student learning outcomes and scholarly breadth within the department, exposure to oncampus resources, networking with current and former NREM students and external stakeholders, career skills development.

subject: NREM
title: Environmental Service Learning II
description: Experiential service learning course for students participating in the Hawaiʻi Youth Conservation Corps summer program as Hana Hou members. Hawaiʻi Youth Conservation Corps summer program students only.

subject: NREM
title: Introduction to Environmental Science
description: Analysis of our environment with emphasis on understanding relationships and interactions of physical, biological, technological, and political components using scientific methods of inquiry. Food supply and safety, water quality, pollution control, biodiversity, environmental policy. Open to nonmajors.

subject: NREM
title: Agricultural and Resource Economics
description: Introduction to basic economics concepts, including demand, supply, exchange, market price and market failure. Economic evaluation and policy for the uses of various natural resource endowments, especially in production agriculture, is included.

subject: NREM
title: Scientific Principles of Sustainability
description: Introduction to the scientific principles of sustainability, including the ecology of managed and natural ecosystems, global change biology, ecological principles of natural resource management, renewable energy technologies, and the environmental impacts of humans.

subject: NREM
title: Internship Preparation
description: Exploration of internship and career opportunities for NREM majors. Development of career-building skills, including resume, interview, and professional networking.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: NREM
title: Natural Resources Management
description: Biological and physical science aspects of natural resource management at local, national, and global scales. Topics covered include resource management of soil, water, forests, wetlands, coasts and wildlife.
prerequisites: (NREM 251/TPSS 251 or NREM 210; CHEM 151 or higher; and BIOL 172) with a minimum grade of C-; or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: NREM
title: Natural Resources Management Lab
description: (1 4-hr Lab) Laboratory and field methods covering biological and physical principles and concepts in natural resource management. Emphasis on basic field measurement techniques, computer skills commonly used in managing natural resources and writing scientific lab reports.
prerequisites: NREM 210/PEPS 210/SUST 210 or NREM 251/SOCS 251/TPSS 251 or (BIOL 101 or higher) or GEO 101 or (ERTH 101 or higher); and NREM 220/SUST 220 or one ECON course or two DS courses.

subject: NREM
title: Fundamentals of Soil Science
description: Origin, development, properties, management of tropical soils; classification of Hawaiian soils.
prerequisites: (CHEM 161 and CHEM 161L) with a minimum grade of C, or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Minimum prerequisite grade of C or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: NREM
title: Fundamentals of Soil Science Laboratory
description: Field and analytical methods for exploring the origin, development, properties, and management of soils, with an emphasis on tropical and Hawaiian soils.
prerequisites: (CHEM 161 and CHEM 161L) with a minimum grade of C.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: NREM
title: Environmental Ethics
description: Application of traditional moral theories to environmental issues. Development and evaluation of specific environmental ethical theories. Application of ethical theories to environmental decision-making.

subject: NREM
title: Managerial Accounting
description: Principles and methods of agricultural accounting. Preparing and interpreting financial statements. Sources and costs of credit, capital budgeting, tax management, estate planning.

subject: NREM
title: Enterprise Management
description: Introduction of practical concepts and methods used in business management. Introduce broad range of business strategies. Understand the critical role each strategy plays. Facilitate student's practice of analytical and critical thinking through case studies.

subject: NREM
title: Tropical Forestry/Agroforestry
description: Biophysical and socioeconomic description and analysis of major tropical forestry and agroforestry management systems, including Hawaiʻi and the Pacific Basin. The role of traditional land use, pressures from regional and global development, and efforts to create sustainable, diverse systems for rural communities will be discussed. higher. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: spring

description: Provides students with an understanding of different roles of the advanced professional nurse; an ability to proactively manage the environment of their specialty practice; includes 45 hours of field work experience.
major_restrictions: enrollment as a classified student in the graduate degree program in SONDH.

subject: NURS
title: Acute and Episodic Care Management
description: Diagnosis and management of health and illness with emphasis on clients with common acute/episodic conditions seen in context of family and community. of individuals across the lifespan. Diverse biopsychosocial perspectives will be considered.

subject: NURS
title: Child/Family Health Assessment
description: (2 Sem, 8-hr Lab) Opportunity to develop specialized clinical competence in assessment of children as a basis for the development of clinical specialization. Development of the individual's potential toward health maintenance functioning.

subject: NURS
title: Child Health: Special Health Needs
description: Provides a theoretical basis required for specialized clinical competence in adapting recent findings on child/ parent assessments, social and health risk indicators, the role of environment, and multidisciplinary settings in the care of pediatrics. Therapeutic modalities used in advanced practice psychiatric-mental health nursing with individuals, families, and groups representing culturally diverse populations.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: NURS
title: Child Health: Promotion and Maintenance
description: Presents normal developmental, physiological and social needs of children within the context of family; then focuses on the management of common acute and chronic illnesses of children.

subject: NURS
title: Women's Health: Special Problem
description: pregnancy, interconception, and the menopausal years. Emphasizes critical analysis of research in pregnancy, family planning, and common gynecological problems.

subject: NURS
title: Disciplinary Knowledge I
description: Exploration of a variety of philosophies of science and examination of factors influencing the development of nursing theory and the emergence of nursing as a discipline.

subject: NURS
title: Chronic Illness Management
description: Management of chronic illness using selected health care systems in culturally diverse communities. Management includes practice, consultation, education, research, and evaluation.
major_restrictions: NURS majors only or consent. NURS 641 - Advanced Nursing in the Global Health Environment - Credits: 3 Exploration and analysis of key global health topics of concern for APNs.

subject: NURS
title: Health Care Technology and Informatics
description: Introduction to the field of health care technology and informatics for clinicians through the application of health care information systems.

subject: NURS
title: Health Law
description: Introduction to medical jurisprudence, medical malpractice, informed consent, health care decisions, medical ethics, the health care industry, managed care, financing health care, and the role of government in health care.
cross_listed: LAW 532 NURS 646 - Advanced Psychiatric-Mental Health Practicum - Credits: V Supervised application of theories, research, and skills for advanced practice psychiatric-mental health nursing with individuals, families, and groups representing culturally diverse populations. Includes the program's capstone experience.

subject: NURS
title: Family Systems in Health and Illness
description: Family systems approach to include selected family theories, family research and family centered culturally sensitive clinical assessment and intervention in health and illness situations.

subject: NURS
title: Complementary and Traditional Care
description: A review of theoretical systems and approaches to complementary and traditional care in culturally diverse populations with a focus on wellness. Appropriate for all health-care professionals. NURS 655 - Tests and Measurement in Nursing Education - Credits: 3 Testing and evaluation applied to nursing education.
major_restrictions: Open to students in other healthcare fields by permission.

subject: NURS
title: (Alpha) Selected Topics in Nursing
description: Supervised practicum to build mid to senior level nursing leadership competencies and facilitate change in complex healthcare organizations. NURS 660D - Selected Topics in Nursing: developmentally disabled children and their families Credits: 3 Advanced study, exploration of special topics in clinical nursing. (D) developmentally disabled children and their families; (I) current issues in professional nursing.
grade_option: A-F only. NURS 662 - Leadership, Role, Identity and Organizational/System Change - Credits: 3 Seminar focused on theoretical and conceptual models of leadership and management as applied to relevant advanced nurses' roles, their respective populations, and changing healthcare environments.

subject: NURS
title: Analysis of Healthcare Delivery Models
description: Analysis of various clinical management concepts and health-care delivery systems influencing outcomes, process, and costs in the delivery of care.

subject: NURS
title: Clinical Economics and Finance
description: Economic and financial concepts and tools for use in developing and managing patient care processes. programmatic decisions.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: NURS
title: Advanced Practice Clinical
description: Application of concepts and principles of assessment, diagnosis, management and evaluation of clients in supervised clinical practicum for advanced practice nursing. NURS 681 - Diagnosis & Management of Complex Care Across the Lifespan - Credits: 2 NURS 678L - Primary Care III Lab (intermediate level): Special Populations (Peds, Gero, Women) - Credits: 3 Culminating seminar synthesizing and applying concepts from all APRN didactic course work, using case/problem-based learning via student and facultyguided discussions. Intermediate level clinical experience. Application of concepts and principles of assessment, diagnosis, evaluation, and management of clients in a primary care setting for advanced practice nursing students focusing on vulnerable populations (pediatric, geriatric, women).
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: NURS
title: Psychobiology of Mental Health Disorders
description: Focuses on the psychological and biological factors that influence mental health and illness among diverse groups of individuals across the lifespan. Relevant biopsychosocial theories and research findings are applied.
repeatable: Repeatable one time, up to six credits.

subject: NURS
title: Individual Psychotherapy for PsychiatricMental Health Nurse Practitioners
description: Major modalities of individual psychotherapy, such as psychodynamic, cognitive-behavioral, brief solutionfocused, and multicultural approaches will be examined. Critical dialogue about race, gender, culture, sexuality and socioeconomic status will be applied to case studies and reenactments. in the Doctor of Nursing Practice (DNP) Program to become psychiatric-mental health nurse practitioners (PMHNPs).
repeatable: Repeatable one time, or up to six credits.

subject: NURS
title: Introduction to Research Methods
description: Nursing research methods for qualitative and quantitative studies, institutional review board procedures, and how to develop a research proposal.
prerequisites: NURS 612 (or concurrent), NURS 612L (or concurrent), NURS 613 (or concurrent), and NURS 629 (or concurrent); or consent.

subject: NURS
title: Successful Aging: Physiologic
description: Advanced study of the functional, cultural, psychological, and socioeconomic phenomena that impact the physiology of normal and abnormal aging. Emphasis is on promoting successful aging among elder residents of the Pacific Basin and applications to primary health care. The final of three courses focused on preparing students

subject: NURS
title: Nursing Education Capstone
description: A culminating experience in nursing education program designed for students to integrate, critique, extend, and apply knowledge gained in the program.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: NURS
title: Directed Study or Research
description: Directed study of problems related to nursing theory and practice.

subject: NURS
title: Thesis Research
description: Research for master's thesis.

subject: NURS
title: Philosophical Thoughts
description: Introduces the major fields of philosophy for use in research. Frameworks for the evaluation and critique of philosophical approaches to research will be explored, debated, analyzed, and applied as relevant to the PhD student's dissertation.

subject: NURS
title: Seminar I: Nursing Science
description: Development and refinement of the student's research area of interest. Synthesis of theory from nursing and related disciplines. Articulation of the problem, significance, purpose of inquiry, related question(s), hypotheses (as applicable) and specific aims.

subject: NURS
title: Seminar II: Literature Review
description: In-depth review, critique, evaluation, and synthesis of a body of literature. Students will use standardized approaches to systematically search, locate, and evaluate evidence in a focused area to generate an integrative review.

subject: NURS
title: Seminar III: Refining the Research Plan
description: Continued research and scholarly development in an area of nursing scientific concentration. Student refines the research plan (in terms of quantitative, qualitative, and/or mixed methods approaches). dissemination strategies relevant and affirming to culturally diverse/marginalized populations and multidisciplinary professional audiences.

subject: NURS
title: Designing and Conducting Clinical Trials
description: Small group sections will guide students as they produce their own clinical trial protocol. Topics like ethics and statistical power will be addressed briefly.
repeatable: 1 time NURS 721 - Instrument Development and Evaluation in Research - Credits: 3 Theory of testing and evaluation applied to nursing research in culturally diverse populations.

subject: NURS
title: Advanced Nursing Science
description: Provides opportunities to know and practice the process of theory development in nursing and to test concepts, relationships, and models about health behavior in culturally diverse populations.

subject: NURS
title: Health Policy and Economics
description: Evaluate the implications of policies on health care funding and regulations, provider payments, and other variables shaping the ability of health care systems (Western and other) on advanced practice.

subject: NURS
title: Quantitative Methods and Measures
description: Critical analysis of quantitative research methods used to analyze problems of interest to nursing in culturally diverse populations.
prerequisites: NURS 620 or equivalent course and an advanced statistics course; or consent.

subject: NURS
title: Qualitative Methods
description: Qualitative methods and approaches to research used in nursing and health sciences will be examined. Multiple paradigms and traditions will be reviewed, including culturally safe approaches and indigenous methodologies.

subject: NURS
title: Seminar on Women and Health
description: Women's health and the role of women health professionals. Current literature and research regarding attitudes, roles, rights, and health care. NURS 735 - Effective Program Evaluation for Quality Improvement - Credits: 3 Integration of program evaluation and translational science for assessment and communication of programmatic health impacts to improve well-being of patients, families, and stakeholders/communities at local, regional, national/ international levels.

subject: NURS
title: Creative Learning Strategies for Adults
description: strategies that assist maturing, self-directed persons to develop their potentialities.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: NURS
title: Concept Development and Analysis
description: Principles of and frameworks for program evaluation. Students develop logic models and evaluation plans for a community program and collect and analyze evaluation data.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: NURS
title: Advanced Statistics I
description: Current theories, issues, and trends of curriculum development and assessment strategies in nursing education.
prerequisites: NURS 747 with a minimum grade of B-.
major_restrictions: NURS majors only. NURS 750 - Foundation of Health Systems and Translation Science - Credits: 3 Integrate scientific findings from various fields of study to the leadership and management of healthcare systems to ensure the delivery of quality and safe care.

subject: NURS
title: Advanced Statistics II
description: Advanced application of multivariate statistics to analyze complex problems of interest to nursing in culturally diverse populations.
prerequisites: NURS 741 and NURS 752 or equivalent courses, or consent. Once a year.

subject: NURS
title: DNP Scholarly Project A: Proposal
description: Prepares students to implement a Doctor of Nursing Practice Scholarly Project Evidenced Based Practice problem by identifying a practice problem, developing a PICO(T) statement, conducting a literature review and synthesis, and developing an implementation plan.

subject: NURS
title: DNP Scholarly Project B: Implementation
description: Experiential learning course where students implement their Evidence Based Practice (EBP) change in collaboration with content experts, team members, and stakeholders within SONDH and the organization where the change is occurring.

subject: NURS
title: Dissertation Proposal Writing Seminar
description: Prepares Doctor of Nursing Practice (DNP) students to evaluate the effectiveness of their evidence based practice change, synthesize findings of the project, and disseminate results and conclusions.

subject: NURS
title: Trends in Healthcare
description: Evaluates the historical foundations of healthcare systems and their relationship to current issues and trends in healthcare services. Analyzes the variations in healthcare delivery systems across nations and the impact on health outcomes.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: NURS
title: Translation Science
description: Synthesis and analysis of interventions and variables that influence the rate of adoption of innovations. Application of concepts to patient populations and systems of healthcare.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: NURS
title: Culturally Competent Research Methods
description: Analysis of concepts, issues, and methods in conducting culturally competent research in the health field.

subject: NURS
title: Advanced Clinical Economics and Finance
description: Theory and practice of financial management to achieve high quality and cost effectiveness in healthcare. Hybrid course which combines face-to-face with online learning.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: NURS
title: Capstone Field Study
description: Supervised experience in executive nursing leadership to address important health issues for high-risk or underserved populations utilizing an interdisciplinary systemsbased approach.
prerequisites: NURS 664 and NURS 774 (or concurrent), or consent.

subject: NURS
title: Doctor of Nursing Practice Project
description: Under the guidance of their DNP Project Team members, students will synthesize, integrate, and translate newly acquired knowledge and skills in the implementation and evaluation of their DNP Project.

subject: NURS
title: Nursing Research Practicum
description: Faculty guided research opportunity to gain mastery of research skills and techniques through participation in mentored research.
prerequisites: NURS 741 (with a minimum grade of B-), NURS 742 (with a minimum grade of B-), NURS 751 (with a minimum grade of B-) and a quantitative methods course.

subject: NURS
title: Grant Writing and Grant Management
description: Designed to introduce students to grants, grant writing, and grant management. Combined lecture discussion, seminar, and exercises on grants, grant writing, and grant management for health sciences. Open to NURS students only, others with approval.

subject: OBGN
title: Electives in OB/GYN: labor and delivery
description: Opportunities for advanced study of selected topics. (B) labor and delivery; (D) sub-internship in family planning; (E) high-risk OB; (F) endocrinology; (G) oncology; (H) topics with individual preceptor; (I) extramural electives.

subject: NURS
title: Dissertation Research
description: Research for doctoral dissertation. Opportunities for advanced study of selected topics. (B) labor and delivery; (D) sub-internship in family planning; (E) high-risk OB; (F) endocrinology; (G) oncology; (H) topics with individual preceptor; (I) extramural electives.

subject: OBGN
title: Electives in OB/GYN: high-risk OB
description: Opportunities for advanced study of selected topics. (B) labor and delivery; (D) sub-internship in family planning; (E) high-risk OB; (F) endocrinology; (G) oncology; (H) topics with individual preceptor; (I) extramural electives.

subject: OBGN
title: Electives in OB/GYN: endocrinology
description: Opportunities for advanced study of selected topics. (B) labor and delivery; (D) sub-internship in family planning; (E) high-risk OB; (F) endocrinology; (G) oncology; (H) topics with individual preceptor; (I) extramural electives.
corequisites: FMCH, MED, PED, PSTY, and SURG 632.

subject: OBGN
title: Electives in OB/GYN: oncology
description: topics with individual preceptor; (I) extramural electives.

subject: OBGN
title: Directed Reading/Research
description: Ocean and Earth Science and Technology The following courses are not part of a specified department's curriculum. These courses are highly interdisciplinary in nature, drawing on faculty expertise from throughout SOEST and the Mānoa campus. They are not based in any of the departments but are administered and coordinated by the associate dean's office. OEST 735 is a key element of the graduate ocean policy certificate program.

subject: OEST
title: International Exchange Study/Research
description: Study overseas in an approved international exchange or similar program.
major_restrictions: GES, ERTH, and MET majors only.

subject: OEST
title: The College Experience
description: Required for entering SOEST majors to develop skills and applications for a successful college experience. Students will examine academic goals and implement strategies to achieve goals including time management and career exploration.

subject: OEST
title: Natural Hazards
description: Science of natural hazards: impact on human civilization of events in the lithosphere, atmosphere, biosphere, and hydrosphere (e.g., earthquakes, hurricanes, red tides, and floods), and impact of humans on their exposure to and mitigation of the hazards.

subject: OEST
title: Contemporary Issues in Energy Systems
description: Introductory experience in energy systems including current status of fossil fuel, renewable energy technologies, energy utilization and conservation; environmental and policy aspects.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: OEST
title: Ethics in Scientific Research
description: Introduction to issues of ethics in scientific research and scientific misconduct. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only
major_restrictions: ERTH, GES, or ATMO majors only.

subject: OEST
title: Principles of Sustainability Analysis
description: Introduction to the principles of sustainability analysis through execution of Life Cycle Analysis applied to products, processes, or systems. LCA and the evaluation of environmental impact will be presented. Personal computer or laptop (Word 97 or higher) OS, and minimum of 4GB RAM.
prerequisites: (CHEM 161 and PHYS 170) with a minimum grade of C-; Junior standing or higher, or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: OEST
title: Undergraduate Directed Research
description: Student carries out a scientific project of small to moderate scope with one or more chosen advisors. Student must complete a document in the style of a scientific journal article.
class_standing: Sophomore standing or higher. OEST 510 - Earth Sciences Professional Development Education - Credits: V Specialized Earth Science topics workshops designed for in-service teachers who wish to learn new approaches and content areas in geology and geophysics, meteorology and oceanography. Credits earned cannot be applied for graduate degrees.

subject: OEST
title: Communicating Ocean Sciences
description: Application of current learning theory and pedagogical practices in formal and informal learning environments to improve communication and instructional skills. Graduate students from multiple science disciplines welcome. Requires knowledge of biology, earth sciences, and/or chemistry.
prerequisites: at least one course in introductory biology, geology, earth science, chemistry or marine science, an interest in ocean science, and enthusiasm for teaching science; or consent.

subject: OEST
title: Directed Reading/Research
description: Selected interdisciplinary ocean/earth-related topics involving science, technology, engineering, and/or policy.

subject: OEST
title: Ocean Policy and Management
description: Interdisciplinary approach to problems relating to humans and their interactions with the world's oceans and coasts. Focus includes institutions for governing the world's oceans and coasts at all scales and on the role of scientific knowledge in managing marine and coastal resources. Ocean and Resources Engineering

subject: ORE
title: Ocean Technology—Man in the Sea
description: Survey of human activities in the ocean, from the most traditional to the most innovative technical and engineering accomplishments.

subject: ORE
title: Surf Science and Culture
description: Introduction to wave science covering generation, propagation, transformation, and coastal processes related to surfing and the human interaction with the ocean using multiple perspectives.

subject: ORE
title: Surf Science and Culture Lab
description: Practical laboratory dedicated to wave science covering generation, propagation, transformation, and coastal processes related to surfing and the ocean environment through technology.

subject: ORE
title: Mineral and Energy Resources
description: Hard mineral and petroleum origins, exploration and exploitation. Renewable and non-renewable resources distribution. Political and scientific constraints.

subject: ORE
title: Hydrodynamics of Fluid-Body Interaction
description: Hydrodynamics of ships, coastal and offshore structures. Wave forces by potential theory and by Morison's equation. Method of source distribution for potential flow problems. Flows with prescribed body motion, fixed and freely floating bodies.

subject: ORE
title: Buoyancy and Stability
description: Ship nomenclature and geometry, hydrostatic principles of surface ships and underwater vehicles in free-floating, partially waterborne, and damaged conditions. Subdivision of ships. Launching.
gen_ed: DP ORE 601 - Ocean and Resources Engineering Laboratory - Credits: 3 Design, construction, and evaluation of an engineering system. Laboratory and field experience and data analysis supplemented with appropriate theory.

subject: ORE
title: Oceanography for Ocean Engineers
description: Physical, chemical, biological, and geological ocean environments for ocean engineers. Introduction to ocean dynamical processes and general circulation. Ocean measurement techniques, theory of underwater acoustics. Sonar, swath bathymetry, and tomography applications.

subject: ORE
title: Water Wave Mechanics
description: Governing equations in free surface flow, deterministic and probabilistic wave theories, wave transformation, wave-induced coastal currents, tides, ocean engineering operational sea state, and design wave criteria.

subject: ORE
title: Dynamics of Ocean Structures
description: Response of floating platforms and vessels to wave action, spectral analysis in sea keeping. Frequency and time domain analyses of rigid body motions in six degrees of freedom.

subject: ORE
title: Coastal Flood Mitigation
description: Design and solutions to coastal flood mitigation problems. Topics include climate adaptation; engineering solutions and best practices to mitigate coastal risk under different ocean hazard scenarios; and ecological approaches to mitigate coastal risk.
prerequisites: consent; knowledge of AutoCAD and ORE 661 desirable.

subject: ORE
title: Coastal Modeling
description: Coastal modeling using the SMS Surface-Water Modeling software. Applications to solving coastal problems for different ocean hazard scenarios by applying models for tides, waves, coastal circulation, wave-current interaction, sediment transport, and/or morphology change.

subject: ORE
title: Structural Analysis in Ocean Engineering
description: Structural and finite element analyses and design of ocean structures to withstand hydrostatic and hydrodynamic loading of the sea. Considerations include material type, safety factor, stress concentration, and fatigue.

subject: ORE
title: Environmental Fluid Dynamics
description: Fluid dynamics for coastal and estuarine environments. Turbulent mixing processes in homogeneous and stratified fluids. Buoyancy driven flows, internal hydraulics, topographic effects and estuarine circulation. Spill and pollutant dispersal.

subject: ORE
title: Ocean Instrumentation and Technology
description: Overview of past, present, and emerging technologies and instrumentation that support oceanographic and marine operations. Will cover the design, operation, and maintenance of the mechanical, electrical, and computing components of these systems.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: ORE
title: Applications in Ocean Acoustics
description: ORE 664 - Nearshore Processes and Sediment Transport - Credits: 3 Sediment transport by waves and currents in coastal areas and its effect on morphological processes. Effect of man-made structures on littoral drift and shoreline.

subject: ORE
title: Marine Renewable Energy
description: Ocean thermal energy conversion (OTEC) systems: applicability, thermodynamics, design challenges; wave energy converters: floating devices, oscillating water column, optimal hydrodynamic performance; current, tidal, and offshore wind power.
prerequisites: ORE 607; basic knowledge of thermodynamics desirable.

subject: ORE
title: Marine Mineral Resources Engineering
description: Activities in marine minerals development are examined in a multidisciplinary systems approach involving engineering, Earth and environmental sciences and economics. ORE 680 - Ocean Engineering and Resilience in a Changing Climate - Credits: 3
prerequisites: consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: ORE
title: Autonomous Marine Systems
description: Identifies the unique challenges faced by autonomous underwater and surface vehicles, and analyzes approaches to address those challenges. Topics include hydrodynamic vehicle modeling, propeller theory, subsystem integration, and motion control strategies.
prerequisites: MATH 307 or MATH 311 (or equivalent), and ECE 351 or ME 451 (or equivalent), or instructor consent.

subject: ORE
title: Coastal and Harbor Engineering
description: Planning and design of seawalls, groins, jetties, breakwaters, and layout of ports. Design requirements for harbor entrances and channels. Littoral drift and sedimentation problems. Navigation and mooring requirements.

subject: ORE
title: Directed Reading or Research
description: Independent study for students working on a Plan B master's project. A grade of Satisfactory (S) is assigned when the project is satisfactorily completed.

subject: ORE
title: Thesis Research
major_restrictions: candidacy for MS in ocean and resources engineering.

subject: ORE
title: Nonlinear Water Wave Theories
description: Higher-order theories. Forced oscillations. Stoke's theory. Nonlinear shallow-water wave equations and hydraulic jumps; effects of rotation. Internal waves. Analytical techniques necessary will be developed as course progresses.

subject: ORE
title: (Alpha) Capstone Design Project
description: Major design experience based on knowledge and skills acquired in earlier course work and incorporating realistic constraints that include economic, environmental, ethical, social, and liability considerations. Emphasis is placed on teamwork and consultant-client relationship. (B) coastal engineering; (C) offshore engineering; (D) ocean resources engineering; (E) oceanic engineering. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only for (E) permanent faculty. concurrent) SUST 112 (or concurrent). ORE 792 - Seminar in Ocean and Resources Engineering - Credits: 1
major_restrictions: ORE majors only. ORE 791 - Special Topics in Ocean and Resources Engineering - Credits: V Content will reflect special interests of visiting and

subject: OCN
title: Sustainability in a Changing World
description: Attendance at 15 approved seminars is required along with submission of notes. Environmentally sustainable and non-sustainable practices, and the impacts of climate change, on the development and spread of human societies from prehistory to the 1500s in Asia, Africa, Europe, the Americas, and Hawaiʻi/Oceania. Active learning environment.

subject: ORE
title: Dissertation Research
description: Oceanography The minimum required grade for undergraduate prerequisites is a C (2.0) or better, and graduate prerequisites is a B (3.0) or better, unless otherwise specified.
major_restrictions: candidacy for PhD in ocean and resources engineering.

subject: OCN
title: Global Environmental Challenges
description: Scientific approach to evaluating human-caused environmental challenges and their potential solutions.

subject: OCN

OCN 150 - Introduction to Quantitative Earth and Environmental Science - Credits: 3 Introduction to pre-calculus math and physics applied to Earth and environmental science. Students work on realworld problems and engage in participatory learning. Preparatory for classes in calculus and physics.
prerequisites: MATH 134, MATH 161, or MATH assessment exam (with score required for MATH 140X). Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only
gen_ed: DB OCN 102L - Introduction to the Environment and Sustainability Lab - Credits: 1 Introduction to a variety of quantitative and qualitative approaches and methodologies to describe and assess key components to the environment.

subject: OCN

description: Inquiry-driven and experimental marine biology summer program based on the expertise of HIMB researchers whose specialties demonstrate how human impacts and global change affect coral reef ecosystems. Emphases on student team research projects.
repeatable: 1 time Frequency of Course (when offered): Summer only
class_standing: High school or UG freshman only.

subject: OCN
title: Introduction to Directed Research
description: Introduction to philosophy of science for those with some background in the natural sciences. Special emphasis on issues arising from the construction and use of models.

subject: OCN
title: Science of the Sea
description: Origin and features of ocean basins and seawater; global cycles of carbon and nutrients; currents; waves; tides; characteristics of marine organisms; marine ecological principles; marine pollution; climate change causes and consequences. Online asynchronous; synchronous format.

subject: OCN
title: Science of the Sea Laboratory
description: Experiments, computer exercises, and field trips demonstrating the geological, physical, chemical, and biological principles of earth and ocean sciences.

subject: OCN
title: Global Environmental Change
description: Delve into global environmental change issues such as geometric population growth, food production, plastic waste, and global warming that are of growing interest and importance to our society.
prerequisites: OCN 201/OCN 201L or ERTH 101/ERTH 101L, CHEM 161/CHEM 161L, and MATH 241; or consent.

cross_listed: PHIL 315 OCN 318 - Introduction to Environmental Monitoring Systems and Measurements - Credits: 3 Introduction to environmental monitoring systems for earth science students. Students will learn how to construct, program, and deploy simple environmental monitoring systems to collect in-situ environmental data.

subject: OCN

Advanced mathematical methods with emphasis on application to the earth and ocean sciences and engineering. Topics include linear algebra, vector calculus, ordinary differential equations, and numerical methods.
prerequisites: GES 311, MATH 242, PHYS 170/PHYS 170L, and CHEM 161/CHEM 161L; or consent.

Analysis of enacted policies from case studies to understanding the effectiveness, challenges, contradictions, and limitations of each.
prerequisites: any 100 or 200 level OCN course, or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

title: Mineral and Energy Resources of the Sea
description: Hard mineral and petroleum origins, exploration, and exploitation. Renewable and non-renewable resources distribution. Political and scientific constraints.
prerequisites: (BIOL 171 and BIOL 172) with a minimum grade of C; or consent.

Factors affecting disease transmission and virulence. Effects of human activities and environmental change on disease transmission. Emphasis on issues pertinent to Hawaiʻi.

description: land, ocean, and climate applications. Students will explore the fundamentals of electromagnetic radiation, and learn to critically interpret patterns in environmental changes over time and space.

title: Undergraduate Internship
description: Experiential approach to earth science; students serve as interns to field professionals; responsibilities include supervised field work.

subject: OCN
title: Directed Reading
