subject: PHRM
title: General Pharmacology
description: Similar to PHRM 201 but wider in scope of drugs discussed. Intended for undergraduates in the health sciences and related fields.

subject: PHRM
title: Directed Reading and Research
description: Fourth-year elective in pediatric sub-specialty areas in which medical students may receive clinical experiences and an in-depth study of selected sub-specialty areas within the field of pediatrics. (B) adolescent medicine; (C) ambulatory pediatric care; (D) pediatric child abuse; (E) clinical genetics; (F) Sub-Internship in neonatology; (G) pediatric cardiology; (H) Sub-Internship in pediatric emergency medicine; (I) Sub-Internship in pediatric hematology/oncology; (J) Sub-Internship in infectious diseases; (K) Sub-Internship in pediatrics—general ward in pediatrics; (M) extramural electives in pediatrics; (N) pediatric rheumatology; (P) developmental behavioral pediatrics; (Q) pediatric international preceptorship; (R) Sub-Internship in pediatric critical care; (S) pediatric radiology; (T) pediatric ultrasound; (U) pediatric nephrology; (V) pediatric sports medicine; (W) pediatric neurology; (X) pediatric pulmonology; (Y) pediatric palliative care.
repeatable: 2 times per alpha, up to 36 credits; 1 time by exception only for (D), (W), (X), (Y); not repeatable for (Q).

subject: PHRM
title: Selected Topics in Pharmacology
description: Elective for medical students in Pharmacology.

subject: PHRM
title: Principles of Pharmacology
description: Pharmacology elective course for medical students.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: PHRM
title: Research in Pharmacology
description: Pharmacology research elective for medical students.

subject: PHRM
title: General Pharmacology
description: Pharmacology Pharmacodynamics, receptor theory, modeling, clinical trials and the FDA will be covered. Concepts in ADME/T and clinical research are also considered.

subject: PHRM
title: Introduction to General Pharmacology
description: Drugs discussed with emphasis on sites and mechanism of action, toxicity, fate, and uses of major therapeutic agents.

subject: PHRM
title: Systemic Pharmacology
description: covering major organ and functional systems of the human body. Concepts in pharmacological research at the animal, organ system and whole human level will also be considered.

subject: PHRM
title: Neuropharmacology
description: Physiology and pharmacology of central and peripheral nervous systems, focusing on synaptic chemistry and signaling.
prerequisites: CMB 606, or consent from the course director.

subject: PHRM
title: Dissertation Research
description: Philosophy

subject: PHIL
title: Asian Traditions
description: Universal themes and problems from Asian perspective.

subject: PHIL
title: Asian Traditions
description: Universal themes and problems from Asian perspective. PHIL 100 - Introduction to Philosophy: Survey of Problems - Credits: 3

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy Through Computer Science
description: Introduction to classic philosophical issues (e.g., external world skepticism and free will) through computational concepts grounded in an introduction to computer science using Python.

subject: PHIL
title: Introduction to Deductive Logic
description: Principles of modern deductive logic.

subject: PHIL
title: Introduction to Deductive Logic
description: Principles of modern deductive logic. Honors Program restriction: Yes Philosophical attempts to evaluate conduct, character, and social practices.

subject: PHIL
title: Introduction to Inductive Logic
description: Introduction to the theory of arguments based on probabilities and to the theory of decision-making in the context of uncertainty.

subject: PHIL
title: Introduction to Critical Thinking
description: (LED) Introduction to critical thinking and its application in everyday life. The materials progress from logic and the structure of argument to fallacies and the nature of science.

subject: PHIL
title: How To Be Happy
description: Everyone wants to be happy. What is happiness? How do we become happy? Students examine and practice proposals from ancient philosophy and tragedy and recent psychology about the ingredients, methods, and difficulties of living well.

subject: PHIL
title: Introduction to World Philosophy I
description: Introduction to philosophy as it has manifested itself differently across cultures throughout the world. Focus on the development of philosophical thought from its beginnings up until 1500 CE.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: PHIL
title: Introduction to World Philosophy II
description: Philosophy attempts to understand the human being and the societies they form. Introduces students to the notion of world philosophy, focusing upon thinkers who have helped to shape our present. philosophical responses to these and other fundamental questions, and develop skills of writing, reading, and oral communication.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: PHIL
title: Medieval to Enlightenment Philosophy
description: Introduction to the history of philosophy based on translations of texts originally written by men and women in post-classical Latin and Arabic. Significant attention is paid to developing writing skills.

subject: PHIL
title: Modern Philosophy
description: Introduction to the history of philosophy based on texts or translations of "modern" works, that is works originally written in a modern European language.

subject: PHIL
title: Women Philosophers
description: Introduces students to the ideas of women philosophers.
prerequisites: any course 100 or above in PHIL or WGSS, or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: PHIL
title: Existentialism: Freedom, Being, Death
description: Introduction to major thinkers and fundamental concepts and debates of Existentialism, taking Existentialism as a global movement expressed in philosophical texts, literature, and film. Significant attention is paid to developing writing skills.

subject: PHIL
title: Ancient Philosophy
description: What is happiness? reality? the best political community? Students explore ancient Greek and Roman

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy of Religion
description: Problems and methods. Nature of religious experience, alternatives to theism, existence of god, relation between faith and reason, nature of religious language.
prerequisites: one PHIL 100-level course or consent of instructor.

subject: PHIL
title: Business Ethics
description: Case studies and critical analyses of ethical issues in business. Readings from business, philosophy, law, etc.
prerequisites: any course 100 or above in PHIL or BUS or BLAW, or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Ethical Theory
description: Problems and methods in theory of moral conduct and decision.
prerequisites: any course PHIL 101 or above in PHIL or above 100 in POLS or SOC; or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Political Philosophy
description: Problems and methods in philosophical theories of political legitimacy.
prerequisites: any course PHIL 101 or above in PHIL or above 100 in POLS or SOC, or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Social Philosophy
description: Problems and methods in examination of contemporary life, values, and institutions in light of traditional philosophical problems of freedom, justice, authority, equality.

subject: PHIL
title: Theory of Knowledge
description: Problems and methods in epistemology. Nature of knowledge, its varieties, possibilities, and limitations.
prerequisites: any course PHIL 100 or above in PHIL, or 200 or above with either DB or DP or DS designation; or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy of Science
description: Philosophical questions about the methods of scientific inquiry. Significant attention given to developing writing skills.
prerequisites: any course PHIL 100 or above in PHIL, or 200 or above with either DB or DP designation; or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy of Education
description: Uses tools of philosophical inquiry to explore historical and contemporary perspectives on the nature of education, including concepts of knowledge, teaching, learning, and human flourishing.
prerequisites: any course 100 or above in PHIL, or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Ethics in Health Care
description: Ethical issues in application and organization of biomedical resources; professional responsibility, confidentiality, euthanasia, experimentation on human subjects, etc.
prerequisites: any course 100 or above in PHIL or MED or NURS or with a DB designation; or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy and Aesthetics of Film
description: Aesthetics and ontology of film and video, based on readings in the philosophy of film and the viewing of a number of films per semester.

subject: PHIL
title: Ethics in Practice
description: Team-taught exploration of five contemporary ethical issues using a variety of philosophical approaches and methods.
prerequisites: any PHIL 101 course or above in PHIL or above 100 in POLS or SOC; or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy and Evolution
description: Explores the ethical and epistemological implications of the theory of evolution. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: PHIL
title: Critical Thinking: Pre-Medicine
description: Scientific and social perspectives on the nature of disease and their impact on medical practice. Exploration of these topics through reading, writing and critical inquiry.
prerequisites: any course in PHIL, PHIL 100 or above; or any two BIOL, CHEM or PHYS courses; or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Critical Thinking: Pre-Law
description: Introduction to concepts and techniques for evaluating arguments with special emphasis on their application both to questions of law and to issues in jurisprudence.
prerequisites: any course 100 or above in PHIL or POLS or SOC, or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy of Law
description: Historical and contemporary issues in law and legal theory. Law and morality; legal responsibility, justice, rights, punishment, judicial reasoning.
prerequisites: any course PHIL 101 or above in PHIL or above 100 in BLAW or POLS or SOC, or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Ethical Issues in the Law
description: Exploration of ethical issues that have come before (mainly U.S.) courts, including but not confined to, medical and criminal justice ethics.
prerequisites: PHIL 101 or a course numbered 200 or above in PHIL or HIST or ENG or with a DS designation; or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: American Philosophy
description: Introduction to philosophy of science for those with some background in the natural sciences. Special emphasis on issues arising from the construction and use of models.
prerequisites: any course 200 or above in PHIL or any course 200 or above with either DB or DP designation, or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: spring

subject: PHIL
title: Science, Technology, and Society
description: Investigation of some of the complex interconnections between science, technology, and society.
prerequisites: any course PHIL 100 or above in PHIL or in a course with either DB or DP or DS designation, or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Bodies, Minds, and Selves
description: Explores the nature of the mind. Questions addressed include: How does the mind relate to the body? What is the nature of consciousness? Are you the same person today as you were yesterday?
prerequisites: any 100-level PHIL course or above, or consent.

subject: PHIL
title: Critical Race Theory and Philosophy
description: (LED) Introduction to the work of philosophers whose writings aim to contribute to movements for racial justice.

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy of Disability
description: Uses tools of philosophical inquiry to explore philosophical concepts emerging from the study of disability. Ethical issues of personhood and justice are discussed, with significant attention given to helping students express their thinking through writing.

subject: PHIL
title: Buddhist Philosophy
description: Survey of central thinkers and schools.

subject: PHIL
title: Chinese Philosophy
description: Survey of important schools and thinkers in classical Chinese traditions: Confucianism, Daoism, Mohism, Legalism. A significant emphasis will be placed on developing the ability to write clearly, critically, and coherently.

subject: PHIL
title: Japanese Philosophy
description: Survey of central thinkers and schools from ancient to modern.

subject: PHIL
title: The Meaning of War

subject: PSTY
title: Philosophy & Human Suffering
description: To see how various philosophies and religions have tackled the question, "why is there suffering in the world?" Read short excerpts from "classic" texts and discuss in a welcoming atmosphere and draw own conclusions. Medical students only.
grade_option: CR/NC only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

title: Directed Reading/Research

Unless otherwise noted, 700-level seminars are explorations of current issues in their respective areas. subject: PSY
title: Survey of Psychology
description: An overview of the field: psychophysiology, perception, learning, cognition, stress, personality, social psychology.

subject: PSY
title: Introduction to Psychobiology
description: Survey of study of behavior from a natural sciences viewpoint. Evolution, ethological analysis of behavior genetics, neural mechanisms, drugs and behavior, biological development.

subject: PSY
title: Developmental Psychology
description: Emotional, mental, physical, social development from infancy to adulthood; interests and abilities at different age levels.

subject: PSY
title: Psychology of Gender
description: Survey of topics in psychology relevant to gender and its impact on the lives of women and men: socialization of gender, mental health, racial identity, majority-minority status, sexual orientation, life-span issues and violence.

subject: PSY
title: Social Psychology
description: Cognitive, behavioral, and emotional effects of people: interpersonal relations, attribution, attitudes, group behavior, stereotypes, social roles, aggression, helping, self-concept; applications.

subject: PSY
title: Psychology of Personality
description: Scientific study of personality, its meaning, assessment, development, relation to cultural-social determinants.

subject: PSY
title: Introduction to Behavioral Psychology
description: Outline of basic learning principles. A general, unified approach to study of human personality and behavior. Based upon a learning conception; various areas of psychology and the other social sciences are treated.

subject: PSY
title: Introduction to Clinical Psychology
description: History, theories, nature of psychological problems, methods of assessment, forms of intervention, current developments.

subject: PSY
title: Introduction to Community Psychology
description: Examination of human functioning in social and ecological context. Topics include stress, health, intergroup relations, culture, ethnicity, social competence, and community empowerment.

subject: PSY
title: Introduction to Educational Psychology
description: Psychology applied to education, including cognitive, sociocultural, and multicultural approaches to teaching, learning, development, and research. Develops knowledge and written communication skills through an introduction to classroom assessment, instruction, motivation, classroom management, and standardized testing.

subject: PSY
title: Learning and Motivation
description: Theoretical interpretations; survey of major theorists and contemporary controversial issues; major influences in classical and instrumental conditioning.

subject: PSY
title: Psychology of Emotion
description: Survey of traditional views and leading theories, and research in related topics.

subject: PSY
title: Cognitive Psychology
description: Survey of cognitive processes involved in perception, attention, memory, language, problem-solving, reasoning, judgment, intelligence and consciousness, among others.

subject: PSY
title: Behavioral Neuroscience
description: Coverage of the relationship between the brain and body on learning and memory, stress, motivated and regulatory behavioral functions, and mental disorders.

subject: PSY
title: Psychopharmacology
description: Coverage of the principles of pharmacology in relation to the effects of drug actions on the brain and behavior.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: PSY
title: Sensation and Perception
description: In-depth coverage of the basic principles involved in sensing and perceiving our environment.

subject: PSY
title: Social Development of Children
description: Survey of the social and emotional development of children and adolescents.

subject: PSY
title: Adult Development and Aging
description: Overview from a multidisciplinary, life-span perspective. Includes research techniques, personality development, family relationships, occupational attainment, death.

subject: PSY
title: Cross-Cultural Psychology
description: Psychological theories and cultural systems; understanding of own and other cultures; psychological and cultural perception of social motivation; cultural similarities and differences in interpersonal relations.

subject: PSY
title: Psychology of Human Sexuality
description: Psychosocial aspects of human sexual relationships. Social psychology of emotional and physiological arousal, interpersonal attraction, and societal regulation of intimate relationships.

subject: PSY
title: Abnormal Psychology
description: Nature and causes of psychological disorders.

subject: PSY
title: Consumer Behavior
description: Analysis of consumer behavior and motivation; principles of learning, personality, perception, and group influence, with emphasis upon mass communication effects.

subject: PSY
title: History of Psychology
description: Origin and development of contemporary points of view.
prerequisites: PSY 100. Recommended: 9 credit hours in psychology.

subject: PSY
title: Seminar on the Psychology of Knowledge
description: Selected topics in the psychology of knowledge and mind from Western and/or non-Western perspectives.
repeatable: In different topics up to 9 credit hours.

subject: PSY
title: Practicum in Psychology
description: Supervised psychological experience in school, clinic, hospital, industry, social welfare, government, etc.

subject: PSY
title: Teaching General Psychology
description: Supervised experience.
prerequisites: PSY 100, at least 12 additional credit hours in psychology, and written consent.

subject: PSY
title: Psychometrics: Advanced Topics
description: In-depth coverage of some area of theory, research, or methodology relevant to individual differences, measurement, or aspects of psychometrics.

subject: PSY
title: Experimental Psychology: Advanced Topics
description: In-depth coverage of some area of theory and research, in cognitive psychology.

subject: PSY
title: Psychobiology: Advanced Topics
description: In-depth coverage of some area of theory and research in psychobiology, physiological psychology, or sensory processes.

subject: PSY
title: Clinical Child and Adolescent Psychology
description: Social and emotional maladjustment in children and adolescents, developmental disabilities, pediatric psychology, psychological interventions with children and adolescents.

subject: PSY
title: Development Psychology: Advanced Topics
description: In-depth coverage of some area of theory and research in developmental psychology.

subject: PSY
title: Special Topics in Psychology
description: Covers topics of current or special interest not covered in regular course offerings or advanced topics seminars.

subject: PSY
title: Directed Reading or Research
description: Psychological principles for understanding and dealing with wellness and illness. Theories and research on stress-related disorders; prevention of stress through lifestyle and healthy behaviors.
prerequisites: PSY 100 or consent. Recommended: PSY 220 or PSY 322.

subject: PSY
title: Methodologic Foundations of Psychology
description: Methods used in psychological research; observational, correlational, and experimental types of design.

subject: PSY
title: Introduction to Regression
description: Introduction to quantitative methods in behavioral sciences and the general linear model with a focus on regression. Topics include correlation, bivariate and multiple regression, mediation, and moderation. Requires basic statistics. (Meets PhD common inquiry methods requirement or elective.)

subject: PSY
title: Teaching Personal Development
description: Supervised experience in leading a seminar in personal development.

subject: PSY
title: Applied Psychology: Advanced Topics
description: Coverage in-depth of some areas of theory and research. PSY 611 - Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) and Related Methods - Credits: 3 Introduction to ANOVA and its extensions from both traditional and general linear model approaches. Topics include single and multi-factor ANOVA, multiple comparisons, analysis of covariance (ANCOVA), and repeated-measures ANOVA. PSY 613 - Factor Analysis and Structural Equation Models (SEM) - Credits: 3 Theories and applications to latent variables models. Topics include path analysis, exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis, structural equation models (SEM), multi-sample SEM, mean structure, latent growth curve models, and multilevel SEM. Requires basic knowledge of regression.

subject: PSY
title: Multivariate Analysis
description: Analysis of multiple dependent variables. Topics include multivariate normal distribution, Hotelling's 72, multivariate analysis of variance (MANOVA), discriminant analysis, cluster analysis, canonical correlation, and principal components analysis (PCA).
prerequisites: PSY 610, EDEP 604, or consent. PSY 616 - Measurement in Education and Social Sciences - Credits: 3 Test theories and applications in education and social sciences. Topics include the true score model; reliability; generalizability theory; validity; item response theory; and applications in research. Class requires knowledge in ANOVA and regression.

subject: PSY
title: Advanced Psychometrics
description: Theories and applications of modern psychometrics. Topics include unidimensional and multidimensional models of item response theory, detecting biased items, measurement invariance, scaling methods, and current issues in psychometrics.

subject: PSY
title: Thinking
description: Provides an introduction to higher cognition (thinking and reasoning) and its foundations, particularly as they relate to the larger field of cognitive science.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: PSY
title: Comparative Cognition
description: Survey of the historical and contemporary study of cognition across species, including learning, memory, attention, navigation, reasoning, social interaction, and communication.

subject: PSY
title: Selected Topics in Comparative Psychology
description: Intensive review of comparative, communicative, sensory, or learning mechanisms in animals.

subject: PSY
title: Categorical Data Analysis
description: Theories and methods for data analysis with categorical and discrete variables. Topics include contingency tables; logistic regression; log-linear models; and introduction to generalized linear models.

subject: PSY
title: Psychopharmacology
description: Basic principles of pharmacology as they apply to the brain and specific psychological disorders such as anxiety, depression, post-traumatic stress disorders, schizophrenia, psychosis, memory, and drug use. Theories and applications of analysis of nested (clustered) data. Topics include fixed and random effects, intra-class correlation, cross-sectional multilevel models, and multilevel models, and multilevel models with repeated measures and longitudinal data. Requires basic knowledge of regression.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year
cross_listed: EDEP 618 PSY 619 - Analysis of Multilevel Models and Longitudinal Data - Credits: 3

subject: PSY
title: Principles of Learning
description: Historical, theoretical, and methodological foundations of developmental psychology. Survey of the principles of learning, including important discoveries in the development of the study of learning, major theories, and both basic and applied research in contemporary literature.

subject: PSY
title: Cognitive Psychology
description: In-depth survey of the computational and representational structures and processes of cognition. Special attention devoted to consideration of the relationship between brain, mind, and computation. Relation of central and peripheral nervous systems to behavior.

subject: PSY
title: Cognitive Development
description: Familiarizes students with current research and theory in cognitive development through readings of original journal articles and monographs.

subject: PSY
title: Social Psychology
description: Theories and research in social cognition and behavior.

subject: PSY
title: Advanced Assessment
description: Application of psychological theories to cross-cultural phenomena; assessment of cross-cultural processes and social motivations; culture and personality; research evaluation and design. Conceptual and methodological foundations of clinical applications of assessment.

subject: PSY
title: Psychology and Social Issues
description: Conflict, dissent, community issues, problems; social change and its relation to mental disorder.

subject: PSY
title: Applied Social Psychology
description: Problems in use of social psychology principles in human affairs; multidisciplinary considerations.

subject: PSY
title: Social Psychology of Love and Sex
description: Seminar in psychosocial aspects of human sexual relationships. Social psychology of cognitive, emotional and physiological arousal, interpersonal attraction, mate selection, and antecedents and consequences of intimate relationships.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: PSY
title: Introduction to Clinical Psychology
description: Preparation for becoming a clinical psychologist with emphasis on scientist-practitioner model, professional ethics, diversity and professional development.
major_restrictions: graduate student in psychology or consent of instructor.

subject: PSY
title: Introduction to Assessment I
description: Psychometric theory; ethics; diversity issues; principles and methods of cognitive-intellectual, neuropsychological, and personality assessment.

subject: PSY
title: Introduction to Assessment II
description: Administration and interpretation of cognitiveintellectual and personality assessment devices.
prerequisites: PSY 671 and PSY 672 and enrollment in Clinical Studies Program, or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: PSY
title: Treatment Research
description: Idiographic and nomothetic approaches to clinical treatment research methods and findings.
prerequisites: PSY 670 (or concurrent) and PSY 671, or consent.

subject: PSY
title: Psychopathology
description: Comprehensive study of the mental disorders across the lifespan.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: PSY
title: Child Practicum
description: Supervised clinical assessment and treatment of children and adolescents.

subject: PSY
title: Adult Practicum
description: Supervised clinical assessment and treatment of adults.

subject: PSY
title: Practicum in Clinical Psychology
description: This supervision-style class will cover cases and issues related to students' clinic work, supervision, or educational goals while training in external practicum sites.

subject: PSY
title: Cultural Community Psychology

subject: PSY
title: Thesis Research
description: Research for master's thesis. Minimum of six credit hours is required.

prerequisites: PSY 610 (with a minimum grade of B) or instructor consent.

title: Research in Psychometrics
description: Supervised reading, discussion, research projects in areas of special interest.

title: Research in Experimental Psychology
description: Supervised reading, discussion, research projects in areas of special interest.

description: Supervised reading, discussion, research projects in areas of special interest.

description: Psychological interventions for youth, as well as parent training. PSY 772 - Adult Treatment: Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy - Credits: 3 Training in cognitive-behavioral strategies for treating adults.
major_restrictions: Enrolled in Clinical Studies Program only. PSY major only.

subject: PSY
title: Seminar in Psychological Therapies
repeatable: Unlimited times PSY 776 - Health Psychology: Behavioral and Biological Bases - Credits: 3 Psychological and biological bases of health psychology and behavioral medicine. Overview of cognitive, behavioral, and psychophysiological mechanisms; theories and methods of prevention in physical disease.

subject: PSY
title: Research in Clinical Psychology
description: Supervised reading, discussion, research projects in areas of special interest.

subject: PSY
title: Community Psychology Research
description: Supervised reading, discussion, research projects in areas of special interest.

subject: PSY

description: Research for doctoral dissertation. Public Administration

subject: PUBA
title: Survey of Public Policy and Analysis
description: Students will learn about the policy making process, the results of policy decisions and how public policy is assessed, analyzed, and responded to. Also discusses important policy issues that currently fill the political landscape.

subject: PUBA
title: Research Methods for Policy Evaluation
description: Explores methodological approaches to the evaluation of public policies and strengths and weaknesses of various social science research methods. Students will learn how to employ them to determine the effectiveness of various public policies.

subject: PUBA
title: Foundations of Nonprofit Management
description: Broad overview of nonprofit organizations, including what it means to be a nonprofit, strategies of nonprofit organizations, and the management of nonprofits. Topics include advocacy, leadership, and evaluating success. Sophomore standing or higher.

subject: PUBA
title: Multicultural Leadership in Public Service
description: Develop students' culturally agile leadership to allow them effectively lead in work on international, regional, and local projects and problems of compelling public interest that cross cultures. Junior standing or higher.

subject: PUBA
title: Practicum and Internship
description: Resolution provides an opportunity for students to apply the skills and concepts learned in earlier courses.
prerequisites: any two other PACE courses or consent.
cross_listed: PACE 495 PUBA 499 - Directed Reading and Research in Public Administration - Credits: V

subject: PUBA
title: Leadership and Ethics
description: Independent research and reading on topics in public administration, public service, and community development. PUBA 605 - Effective Communication in Public Administration - Credits: 3 PUBA 601 - Program Evaluation for Public and Nonprofit Organizations - Credits: 3 Knowledge and skills to effectively communicate in the public sector. Focus on communication foundations and skills, levels and contexts of public sector communication, and handling challenges such as diverse and multi-cultural settings. Methods for evaluating the effectiveness of programs in the public and nonprofit sectors; performance measurement to inform managerial decision making. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only
major_restrictions: PUBA and PUBA Cert. majors only.

subject: PUBA
title: Introduction to Public Administration
description: Develop a comprehension of the history and foundation of public administration. Topics include economic, political, and social dynamics; decision-making and leadership theories, management challenges, human resources, budgeting, program evaluation, policy, and technology.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only
major_restrictions: PUBA and PUBA Cert. majors only.

subject: PUBA
title: Organizations: Theory and Change
description: Explores characteristics and structural, human resources, political, and cultural frames of organizational theory. Focus on organizational change strategies and theories. Discusses how to use these frames and theories in everyday management of public service organizations.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only
major_restrictions: PUBA and PUBA Cert. majors only.

subject: PUBA
title: Public Administration Research Methods
description: Introduction to research methods for public administrators to understand the principles and methods used to conduct and analyze valid research. Examples are oriented to the field; theory and hands-on practice utilized.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only
major_restrictions: PUBA and PUBA Cert. majors only.

subject: PUBA
title: Public Budgeting
description: Institutions and issues related to public-sector budgeting at federal, state, and local levels. Process of developing public budgets and constraints on public policy reflected in budgets. PUBA 622 - Strategies of Change: Leaders and Leadership - Credits: 3 Explores the key elements of leadership in public settings by examining what leaders actually do, looking at popular media portrayals of leadership, and talking together with guests about the challenges of leadership, effective followership, and positive change.

subject: PUBA
title: Policy Analysis and Implementation
description: Explore U.S. law as applied to public institutions using economic lens. Rationale of property, contract, and tort law; evolution of administrative law, economic efficiency of common law system, effects of legal rules on economic behavior.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only
major_restrictions: PUBA majors only. PUBA 614 - Program Evaluation for Public and Nonprofit Organizations - Credits: 3 Methods for evaluating the effectiveness of programs in the public and nonprofit sectors; performance measurement to inform managerial decision making.

subject: PUBA
title: Reforming Public Organizations
description: Looks at the challenges and opportunities for changing public organizations so that they may be more successful in meeting their public responsibilities and better places for people to work. Focus is on the creation of positive images of organization and effective change strategies.
prerequisites: PUBA 606 (with a minimum grade of B) or consent.
class_standing: Graduate students only or consent. PUBA 627 - Managing Workplace Diversity and Inclusion - Credits: 3 Examines rationales, impacts, and various dimensions of diversity and inclusion beyond race and gender. Students learn and apply public management tools used to foster workplace diversity and inclusion.

subject: PUBA
title: ICT Policy and Planning
description: Processes and methods of planning appropriate to the information and communication sectors, including future economic, social, political, technical, and environmental perspectives.

subject: PUBA
title: Special Topics
description: Topics of current interest in the field of public service and public administration, taught by regular and visiting faculty.
major_restrictions: PUBA majors only or approval of instructor.

subject: PUBA
title: Nonprofit Management
description: Fundamental aspects of managing a nonprofit organization: overview of the nonprofit sector; mission and scope of nonprofit organizations; organizational structures and functions; resource and volunteer development; major management issues.

subject: PUBA
title: Indigenous Governance
description: Overview of indigenous governing systems, particularly in the Americas and the Pacific. Students will learn the legal frameworks and principles of these systems, and how services are provided to citizens.
repeatable: Up to 6 credits for different topics

subject: PUBA
title: MPA Practicum
description: Placement in public, private, and nonprofit organizations to observe and analyze organizational functions and processes while undertaking projects of use to the host agency.
prerequisites: with a minimum grade of B: PUBA 602, PUBA 603, and PUBA 604.

subject: PUBA
title: Certificate Practicum
description: Students in the nonprofit management track of the certificate will learn by doing and observing in a nonprofit organization selected in consultation with the student's advisor.

subject: PUBA
title: Capstone Seminar
description: Culminates public administration core courses by incorporating theoretical, analytical, and practicum observations into examination of public issues of importance to Hawaiʻi and the region.
prerequisites: (PUBA 601 , PUBA 602 , PUBA 607) with a minimum grade of B.

subject: PUBA
title: Thesis Research
description: Public Health Sciences The courses listed below are offered subject to student interest and faculty availability. The minimum required grade for prerequisites for undergraduate-level courses is C- or better, unless otherwise specified. All courses 600-level and above require graduate standing; individuals who are not public health students require instructor consent to enroll in graduate-level courses. The minimum required grade for prerequisites for graduate-level courses is B- or better, unless otherwise specified.

subject: PH
title: Introduction to Public Health
description: Introduces public health concepts with an emphasis on principles and tools for population health, disease prevention, health professions and healthcare systems, and public health professions and systems.

subject: PH
title: Public Health Issues in Hawaiʻi
description: Application of general public health concepts and tools with broader public health issues as they relate to the State of Hawaiʻi.

subject: PH
title: Introduction to Global Health
description: Introduction to the basic principles of global PH. Topics include the application of these principles to global PH issues, exploration of links between health, economic, and social status, health disparities and global interventions.

subject: PH
title: Quantitative Reasoning for Public Health
description: Inductive and deductive reasoning; tabular, symbolic, verbal, and graphical forms of functions and relations; graphs and pictorial representations of data; interpretations of probabilistic data; surveys and statistical studies as related to public health.
gen_ed: FQ PH 211 - Public Health Student Success in the College Experience - Credits: 2 Required for entering PH majors to gain strategies for a successful college experience while studying public health. Students will examine academic and career goals and implement strategies to achieve them.

subject: PH
title: Seminar in Public Health Issues
description: Seminar will explore current issues and case studies in epidemiology, issues and causes of chronic and infectious diseases, how the environment interacts with health, and how social and behavioral factors affect personal health.

subject: PH
title: Native Hawaiian Health Determinants
description: Seminar to work with faculty in applying evidence-based knowledge on social determinants of health in the formation of research, policy, and program development for improving population health and reducing health disparities for Native Hawaiians. Junior standing or higher.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: PH
title: Introduction to Epidemiology
description: Lecture/discussion on the fundamental principles of epidemiology, exploring patterns of disease, threats to health and EPI methods for prevention, control, and treatment.
major_restrictions: PH majors only. PH 325 - Youth Risk and Protection—Public Health Research, Practice, and Policy - Credits: 3 Application of public health, related to youth health risks and protective factors using an eco-developmental framework.

subject: PH
title: The United States Health Care System
description: Overview of the U.S. health care system. Topics will include health economics, health service expenditures, comparative health systems, health policy, and issues of cost containment, access, and quality of care.

subject: PH
title: Public Health and the Environment
description: Examines a variety of issues associated with environmental effects on disease incidence, morbidity, and mortality in relation to public health prevention strategies. Sophomore standing and above.

subject: PH
title: Public Health Biology and Pathophysiology
description: Explores the biological basis of human disease and the role public health measures play in reducing both the extent and impact of chronic and acute diseases on individuals and society.
prerequisites: PH 201, and one of the following: BIOL 101 or BIOL 171 or BIOL 172 or PHYL 103 or PHYL 141 or FSHN 185.

subject: PH
title: Introduction to Biostatistics
description: Basic biostatistics methods in public health and biomedical research. Topics covered include data collection, data analyses, and interpretation of statistical results. Sophomore standing or higher.

subject: PH
title: Advanced Epidemiology
description: Students will gain a deeper understanding of the core concepts used in epidemiologic research and practice. Upon completion, students will have the knowledge and skills necessary to conduct an epidemiologic study. Junior standing or higher.

subject: PH
title: Nutrition and Disease Prevention
description: Lecture/discussion. Examines a variety of issues associated with the effects of diet on disease incidence, morbidity, and mortality in relation to public health prevention strategies. Junior standing or higher.
class_standing: Sophomore standing or higher. PH 422 - Social Behavioral Health II: Health Promotion in Communities - Credits: 3 Introduction to health education and health promotion programming in public health, and to social/behavioral theories used to develop health interventions that affect communities, institutions, and policies. Introduction to common program planning models.

subject: PH
title: Tobacco & Community Disparities
description: Assessing the facilitators and barriers of smoking initiation, cessation, and exposure to second-hand smoke within communities. Use of photovoice and its application to policy and addressing disparities.

subject: PH
title: Health Policy and Management
description: Examines the role that health policy and management plays in population-based public health practice, including the delivery, quality, and costs of health care and the structure, process, and outcomes of health services delivery. Sophomore standing or higher.

subject: PH
title: Back to the Future: Aging in Today's Society
description: By 2050, more than a quarter of the world's population will be 60 years of age or older. Explores what we know about aging today to encourage a lifetime of aging well.
prerequisites: PH 201 or SW 360 or WGSS 305 or PSY 100 or HDFS 230 or NURS 200; or consent.

subject: PH
title: Introduction to One Health
description: human health connections. Junior standing or higher.
prerequisites: (PH 201, or ANSC 200, or BIOC 141, or BIOL 101, or BIOL 171, or BOT 101, or ERTH 101, or GES 102, or OEST 101, or OCN 102, or ATMO 150, or SOC 180) with a minimum grade of C or better; or consent.

subject: PH
title: Introduction to Environmental Microbiology
description: Lecture/discussion. Will define the nature and biological activities of microorganisms in different environments and evaluate the effects of these microbes on human activities and health. Junior standing or higher.

subject: PH
title: Public Health Applied Learning Experience
description: practice acquired during the public health undergraduate degree. Students also reflect on, finalize, and present written applied learning experience projects at a public form. Senior standing and higher. PH 489 - Public Health Undergraduate Capstone Seminar - Credits: 3 PH 492H - Current Issues and Topics in Public Health: health policy and management - Credits: V Synthesis of public health knowledge, skills, and Current and emerging issues and varying topics related to public health. (B) biostatistics; (E) epidemiology; (H) health policy and management; (S) social and behavioral health sciences; (T) public health.

subject: PH
title: Public Health Foundations
description: Focus will provide a broad introduction to the field of public health and orientation to overarching issues in the field.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: PH
title: U.S. Health Care Services and Policy
description: Overview of the historical, conceptual, ethical and political context for health care delivery in the U.S. Explores current trends, practices and issues in the delivery of health care services in both private and public sector.

subject: PH
title: Public Health Biology
description: Writing-intensive asynchronous computer-based course examines biological processes and challenges relevant to the public health professional. Topics include anatomical, pathophysiological, and molecular bases of public health; genetics, immunology, ethics; disease prevention, control, and management. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: PH
title: Fundamentals to One Health
description: Familiarizes students with the fundamentals of One Health—an interdisciplinary field of study linking ecosystems to human and animal health. It uses contemporary examples and emphasizes oceans and human health connections.
class_standing: Graduate students only. PH 623 - Introduction to Health Promotion Theory and Methods - Credits: 3 Individual and community health; implications for public health practice, individual and social change processes.

subject: PH
title: Health Economics
description: Integrated concepts in health economics and its application towards health policy issues; market failures in health care; factors affecting U.S. health care spending potential impact on equity/efficiency stemming from changes in health care delivery.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: PH
title: Cultural Competency in Health Care
description: Presents both analytical and practical approaches to cultural competency domains, concepts, models, frameworks, patterns and communication that occur in cross-cultural healthcare situations.

subject: PH
title: Needs Assessment
description: Knowledge and skills acquisition in conducting needs assessment in public health practice.

subject: PH
title: Indigenous Health Seminar
description: Examines public health through an Indigenous lens, integrates competencies across all public health disciplines, and will apply them in context of working for and with Indigenous communities to improve health and wellness.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: PH
title: Advanced Topics in Health Policy
description: Examines major federal and local policies that impact health and health care delivery in the U.S. and other nations; considers effectiveness of these policy-making institutions for improving population health; covers methods in policy analysis.

subject: PH
title: Grant Writing in Public Health
description: Lecture/ discussion on grant writing with public health focus. Includes basic components of grant proposals, assessing appropriate funding opportunities, data sources/resources for justifying grants, and the funder's perspective. Student will prepare a brief foundation grant proposal.

subject: PH
title: Ecological Epidemiology
description: Applications of population biology, pathogen/host life history, and population genetics to infectious disease epidemiology, including micro- and macroparasites, and implications to disease control and prevention of strategies.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: spring

subject: PH
title: Interdisciplinary Seminar
description: Topics such as contemporary issues in global health and population studies, international health programs, demographic methods, global economy and health, human right and humanitarian assistance, social justice, global environmental changes and health.

subject: PH
title: Global Health and Human Security
description: Provides the knowledge, skills, and attitudes required to successfully manage health security crises and protect human vulnerability in the global context with a special focus on problems with high likelihood and risk in the Pacific.

subject: PH
title: Biostatistics I
description: Introduction to statistical methods for public health sciences. Probability, experimental design, t tests and analysis of variance, 2X2 contingency tables, linear regression, introduction to life tables.

subject: PH
title: Biostatistics II
description: Poisson distribution, Fisher's exact test, contrasts in ANOVA, two way ANOVA, multiple linear regression and analysis of covariance, path analysis, logistic regression, method of maximum likelihood, likelihood ratio tests.
prerequisites: PH 655, completion of one semester of calculus; or consent.

subject: PH
title: Computer Applications in Public Health
description: Applications of computers to problems common to public health. Emphasis on data analysis and processing using existing computer programs.

subject: PH
title: Methods of Demographic Analysis
description: Statistical evaluation and analysis of population data; data sources; population growth; composition; standardization of rates; mortality and the life table; nuptiality and fertility; distribution, migration, and urbanization; projections and stable population theory.

subject: PH
title: Principles of Epidemiology I
description: Introduction to epidemiologic principles and methods. Topics covered include: outbreak investigation, measures of morbidity and mortality, measurements of risk, biological variability, screening, measurements of error, sampling, statistical significance, study design, and association and causation.

subject: PH
title: Principles of Epidemiology II
description: Lecture/ discussion on: design and interpretation of experimental and observational studies; causation and casual inference; biases in study design; random error and statistics role in epidemiology; and epidemiological data analysis.

subject: PH
title: Seminar in Infectious Disease Control
description: Strategies for controlling important infectious diseases in the Pacific area. Emphasis on epidemiology, ecology, and public health principles.
prerequisites: PH 663 (or concurrent) and one semester in microbiology, or consent.

subject: PH
title: Infectious Disease Micro II
description: importance to human diseases. The genome, structure, replication, as well as host immune responses, epidemiology, clinical features, and animal models will be presented.

subject: PH
title: Epidemiological Study Design Critique
description: Critique of study design using published public health literature. Emphasis on exchange of ideas, alternative approaches; stresses epidemiology as science of public health.

subject: PH
title: Community and Public Health Practice
description: Community organization and development applicable to the delivery of health services. Understanding community dynamics, mobilizing community groups for effective health care practice and delivery.

subject: PH
title: Leading and Managing Health Programs
description: Assess how to organize community partnerships to create and communicate a shared vision for a changing future; discuss solutions to organizational and community challenges; maximize motivation to reach public health goals.

subject: PH
title: Native Health Ethics, Law, and Policy
description: Review theories and laws concerning health care ethics, policy, and practice using Indigenous case studies. Topics include Indigenous health systems, advocacy, and the intersecting issues of self-determination, ethics, agenda setting, and the policy cycle. Will cover different families of animal viruses of PH 674 - Advanced Native Hawaiian Health Determinants - Credits: 3 Applications of evidence-based knowledge about the social determinants of health in the formation of research, policy, and program development for improving population health and reducing health disparities for Native Hawaiians.

subject: PH
title: Community Engaged Research and Practice
description: Explores collaborative and engaged approaches with communities in public health research and practice. With a focus on Indigenous Peoples' health, we delve into Indigenous knowledge and empowerment in evaluation, needs assessment, intervention, and health promotion.

subject: PH
title: Managing Global Health Service Delivery
description: Provides knowledge, skills, attitudes and resources that health managers require to manage and maintain the quality of partnerships, facilities, programs, community services, people, drugs, and information in limited resources settings.

subject: PH
title: Health Emergencies in Large Populations

subject: PH
title: Environmental Determinants of Health
description: Environmental factors in personal and community health; implications for public health practice. Consideration of major issues from local, U.S., and international perspectives.

subject: PH
title: Global Nutrition

grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt. years: fall

subject: PH
title: Advanced Child and Adolescent Nutrition
description: Addresses nutrition, growth, and development in children and adolescents and nutrition-related issues, such as childhood obesity and chronic disease risk factors, with a focus on current research in the Pacific region.
prerequisites: FSHN 370 or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: PH
title: Directed Reading/Research
description: Dietary, biochemical, anthropometric and clinical methods used for evaluating nutrition and diet in the etiology and epidemiology of disease.

subject: PH
title: Global Health Challenges
description: Addresses critical, contemporary, and transnational issues best addressed by cooperative international action. Health issues are examined in the context of intersecting effects of limited resources, socioeconomics, politics, and environmental change.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year PH 691 - Fundamentals of Environmental Epidemiology - Credits: 2 Examines the complex relationship between environmental contaminants and human health. Emphasis on environmental epidemiology study design, environmental exposure monitoring and risk assessment, disease and environmental exposure mapping, and spatial data analysis and modeling with GIS.

subject: PH
title: Health Communication

subject: PH

grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: PH

Physical activity content includes benefits, factors that influence, levels, valid instruments to assess, and programs to promote physical activity. major_restrictions: PH majors only. Consent or PhD in PH or EPID only.

subject: PH

subject: PH

subject: PH
title: Health Behavior Change

It will also focus on the major theories of behavior and behavior change. Emphasis will be placed on understanding concepts, principles, explanations, and how these are translated into practical interventions for adoption and maintaining behavior change. PH 742 - Qualitative Research for Public Health Sciences - Credits: 3 Provides a basic understanding of qualitative research approaches, methodologies, and techniques and for public health research and practice (needs assessment, program development, and evaluation strategies).

subject: PH

Methodologic issues, contemporary findings and recommendations for future research will be discussed. Examine the epidemiologic study of the social distribution and social determinants of states of health, including the identification of social-environmental exposures and their relation to physical and mental health outcomes.

subject: PH

subject: PH

subject: PH
title: Neuroepidemiology
description: Lecture/discussion providing an overview of the epidemiology of neurological and neurodegenerative diseases and their risk factors, and methodological considerations for the study of these diseases. subject: PH
title: Special Topics in Tropical Medicine

Examination of complex sociocultural factors that privilege some and oppress/marginalize others. Challenges commonly held tropes and complicates the discourse regarding individual, family, group, organizational and community development within the social environment. Recommended: SW 200. description: Study of current social services for children in the U.S. with focus on familiarization of child welfare programs and services in Hawaiʻi.
