prerequisites: PH 201 or SW 360 or WGSS 305 or PSY 100 or HDFS 230 or NURS 200; or consent.
class_standing: senior standing or consent. SW 477 - Social Welfare Concepts and Issues in Gerontology - Credits: 3

subject: SW
title: Research Development in Social Welfare
description: Introduction to and application of language of research, theoretical concepts underlying advancement of knowledge, practical steps in research.

subject: SW
title: Topics in Social Welfare
description: An examination of current trends and issues in social work.
prerequisites: completion of required junior-level SW courses (i.e., SW 302, SW 303, SW 325, SW 326, SW 360, SW 361, and SW 391) with C or better.
gen_ed: DS SW 470 - Native Hawaiian Perspectives: A Cultural Context for Well-Being - Credits: 3 To preserve the cultural knowledge of kupuna as it relates to health and wellbeing. Disseminate cultural knowledge of health and well-being to social work students and students in allied health professions.

subject: SW
title: Undergraduate Field Education II
description: Intermediate instruction to field education; application of social work knowledge, skills, and values to the field experience.
prerequisites: SW 391 complete with C or better, and majors only.

subject: SW
title: Undergraduate Field Education III
description: Advanced instruction to field education; application of social work knowledge, skills, and values to the field experience.
prerequisites: SW 490 complete with C or better, and majors only.

subject: SW
title: Directed Reading and Research
description: Planned individualized study or research in special area related to social work practice interest. Up to 3 credit hours.
major_restrictions: majors only, senior standing, and consent of program chair and faculty advisor.

subject: SW
title: Social Work Practice with Individuals
description: Beginning practice course introduces students to the basic processes of social work and the roles and skills needed for generalist practice. Relevant theories of social work practice with individuals are explored for the efficacy with various problems and for their applicability to practice with various ethnocultures, social classes, and oppressed populations. Interviewing and interpersonal skill development are incorporated.

subject: SW
title: Social Welfare Policy and Services
description: Examines in a historical and comparative framework the economic, social, political, organizational, and administrative factors influencing the development, formulation, and implementation of social welfare policies in the U.S. Provides opportunity for the application of various models of social policy analysis in major areas of social welfare programming and service delivery.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: SW
title: Policies, Programs and Services on Aging
description: Explores policies, programs, and services for older adults. Students learn about the aging network, assess older adults' needs, link older adults to appropriate services in the community, and track legislative bills that address older adults' quality of life.

subject: SW
title: Death and Dying
description: Physical, social, cultural, psychological, and spiritual dimensions of dying, death, and bereavement.

subject: SW
title: Social and Cultural Aspects of Aging
description: gerontology.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only
class_standing: graduate standing. SW 650 - Research Designs and Data Analyses for the Evaluation of Practice Effectiveness - Credits: 3 Extending the study of scientific methods introduced in SW 640. Covers the range of empirical research methods and data analytic procedures suitable for knowledge building and practice evaluation at all levels of intervention from case to program.

subject: SW
title: Quantitative Methods I
description: Introduction to quantitative methods in behavioral sciences. Introduction to general linear model as principle of data analysis. Course requires basic statistics. (Meets PhD common inquiry methods requirement or elective.)

subject: SW
title: Human Behavior in the Social Environment I
description: Examination of complex factors that privilege some and oppress/marginalize others; challenges commonly held tropes and complicates the discourse regarding individual, family, group, organizational and community development within the social environment.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: SW
title: Human Behavior in the Social Environment II
description: Focus on human development across the lifespan with attention given to unique challenges and opportunities faced in each phase. Emphasis is placed on "populations at risk" and the strengths and assets unique to diverse populations.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: SW
title: Treatment of Chemical Dependency
description: Introduction to treatment of alcoholism and other chemical dependencies. Application of social work strategies in work with individuals and families in the disease and recovery process.

subject: SW
title: Applied Regression and Analysis of Variance
description: Augments existing courses, enhances practicum, and satisfies students' expressed need for firmer understanding and working knowledge of the DSM, the prevailing diagnostic system for psychological disorders in the United States.

subject: SW
title: Multivariate Methods
description: Multivariate forms of multiple linear regression, analysis of variance, and analysis of co-variance. Multiple discriminant analysis, canonical correlation, and principal components analysis are discussed.

subject: SW
title: Child Welfare as a Field of Social Work
description: historical, theoretical, empirical, and legal findings for skill development in intervening in dysfunctional parent/ child interaction.

subject: SW
title: Community and Public Health Practice
description: Community organization and development applicable to the delivery of health services. Understanding community dynamics, mobilizing community groups for effective health care practice and delivery.

subject: SW
title: Topics in Social Welfare
description: Current trends in field of social welfare. Recent courses have focused on forensic social work, immigrants and refugees, and leadership in human services. Meets seminar requirement.

subject: SW
title: Marriage and Family Therapy
description: Introduction to couple and family therapy offers an overview of family systems perspective, theory and technique relevant to informing effective social work case planning, case management, advocacy, and interaction with clients.

subject: SW
title: Generalist Field Education I
description: Field education is the signature pedagogy of social work education. Students will engage in supervised social work practice and have the opportunity to apply generalist knowledge, skills, values, and ethics to practice.
prerequisites: SW 606 (or concurrent)(with a minimum grade of C).

subject: SW
title: Generalist Field Education II
description: generalist knowledge, skills, values, and ethics to practice.
prerequisites: SW 690 and SW 607 (or concurrent) and SW 631 (or concurrent) (all with a minimum grade of C).

subject: SW
title: Health and Aging
description: Biological and physiological changes associated with aging. Social and psychological factors associated with health maintenance. Major threats to health, changing patterns of morbidity and mortality of the aged.

subject: SW
title: Directed Reading and Research
description: Students, on the basis of special interest, select a faculty member to work with on a problem for which planned individualized study or research is deemed advisable.

subject: SW
title: Thesis Research
description: Independent research under supervision of a thesis committee. Includes formal proposal and defense of finished research.

subject: SW
title: Methods of Group Psychotherapy
description: Designed specifically to train students in the theory and practice of leading psychotherapy groups; it includes historical developments, research, theories, and application of group psychotherapy, group techniques and exercises.

subject: SW
title: Therapeutic Strategies with the Older Adult
description: Focuses on interdisciplinary strategies with older adults: individual, family, and group therapy; eclectic mental health approaches; case management; and environmental intervention. Emphasis placed on the use of these strategies as preventive, as well as supportive, measures for the well, transition, and frail elderly. Meets seminar requirement. SW 717 - Social Work Practice with Children and Families - Credits: 3 SW 724 - Seminar in Social Work Practice in Mental Health - Credits: 3 Advanced practice course for students specializing in social work with children and families. Designed to provide students with an in-depth understanding of both theoretical formulations and therapeutic techniques for practice in the field of family and child welfare. Emphasis placed on the development of specialized knowledge and skills for assessment, intervention, and evaluation of a variety of common child and family practice situations. Prepares students for social work practice in mental health settings. Focused on engagement and assessment while emphasizing cultural, person in environment, and strengths-based approaches.

subject: SW
title: Social Work Practice in Mental Health
description: Prepares students for social work practice in mental health settings, with a focus on history and policy in the U.S. and Hawaiʻi, as well as culturally-grounded and evidence-based practice.

subject: SW
title: Social Work Practice with Older Adults
description: Designed to provide students with an in-depth understanding of theoretical formulations and intervention strategies for working with older adults and their families. Emphasis on diversity/justice.

subject: SW
title: Social Work Practice in Health Care
description: Didactic and experiential learning activity focuses on the major role functions of the social worker in the health field including assessment, contracting, counseling, advocacy, case management, discharge planning, family group work, community and team building. Covers health care policy, research directions in practice and social work management issues.

subject: SW
title: Seminar in Social Work with Older Adults
description: SW 723 - Seminar in Social Work Practice in Health Care - Credits: 3

subject: SW
title: Social Policy Analysis
description: Students pursue in-depth a specific topic in the areas of social planning, social policy analysis, evaluation of social programs, administration, supervision, and consultation. Selectively a comparative perspective is introduced and case studies used to illustrate concepts, principles, and techniques, with implications for practice. Meets seminar requirement.

subject: SW
title: Social Work and the Law
description: Knowledge of judicial systems and law relevant to social work practice in corrections, child-family welfare, health, and mental health. Skills for effective participation in the legal process are acquired in moot court and in practice for testifying.

subject: SW
title: Review of Research in Social Work
description: In-depth study of research in a substantive area. Each seminar will be devoted to a particular topic: e.g., foster care of children, effectiveness of social work intervention, etc.

subject: SW
title: Individual or Group Research Project--Plan B
description: Same as SW 743.

subject: SW
title: Individual or Group Research Project--Plan B
description: epistemology, and the analysis and development of knowledge for social work practice.
major_restrictions: PhD candidate in social welfare or consent.

subject: SW
title: Quantitative Methods II
description: Empirical research methodology with emphasis on design principles and measurement theory; design and measurement issues and problems in cross-cultural research.
prerequisites: SW 640 or SW 651 or equivalent; departmental approval.
major_restrictions: PhD candidate in social welfare or consent. SW 752 - Qualitative Research: Philosophical, Methodological and Analytic Approaches - Credits: 3 Theories and methods of qualitative research; problem formulation, informant selection, study design, data collection and analysis utilizing qualitative approaches.

subject: SW
title: Dissertation Seminar
description: Culminating experience in social welfare doctoral specialization; integration of PhD core and specialization course work.
major_restrictions: classified student in PhD in social welfare program or consent.

subject: SW
title: Seminar in International Social Work
description: Approaches to social problems and trends in the profession in international, cross-cultural perspectives. Emphasis on developmental aspects of social work. Political, economic, social, and cultural forces shaping social welfare in national development. Meets seminar requirement. SW 750 - Analysis and Development of Knowledge for Social Work - Credits: 3

subject: SW
title: Cultural Factors in Work with Hawaiians
description: Hawaiians and part-Hawaiians. Special emphasis on supports in the Hawaiian system that may promote maximal functioning for those Hawaiians experiencing problems in today's society. Meets seminar requirement.

subject: SW
title: Seminar on Women and Health
description: Women's health and the role of women health professionals. Current literature and research regarding attitudes, roles, rights, and health care.

subject: SW
title: Specialist Field Education I
description: Instruction in the field is continued. The field experience in the second year provides an opportunity for the student to test out concepts, principles, theories, and alternate approaches in actual practice settings.
prerequisites: SW 717 (or concurrent) or SW 722 (or concurrent) or SW 724 (or concurrent) or SW 726 (or concurrent) (all with a minimum grade of B -).

subject: SW
title: Specialist Field Education II
description: Continuation of SW 790.
prerequisites: SW 606 and SW 607; SW 630 and SW 631; SW 640 and SW 650; SW 659 and SW 660; SW 690 and SW 691.

subject: SW
title: Dissertation Research
description: Sociology In addition to the prerequisites specified below, all 300level courses have as a prerequisite SOC 100 or a 200level sociology course, or consent. In addition to the prerequisites specified below, all 400-level courses require SOC 300 or consent. All prerequisite courses require a minimum grade of C (not C-), unless otherwise specified. All graduate courses in the department require classified graduate standing in sociology or consent. Additional prerequisites are specified below.

subject: SOC
title: Introduction to Sociology
description: Basic social relationships, social structures, and processes.

subject: SOC
title: Introduction to Sociology
description: Basic social relationships, social structures, and processes.

subject: SOC
title: Introduction to Data Analysis
description: Basic analytic skills widely used in quantitative analysis of social science data, including descriptive statistics, rates and probability, comparison of groups, introduction to causal relationships, and application of these techniques to real life examples.

subject: SOC
title: Introduction to Race and Ethnic Relations
description: Japan, as well as the Chinese language sphere. Analyzes social forces shaping pop culture industries, artists, and productions in the region from a global perspective.

subject: SOC
title: Introduction to Social Problems
description: Theoretical and substantive survey of the nature and causes of social problems; selected types: poverty, inequality, deviance, etc.

subject: SOC
title: Introduction to Juvenile Delinquency
description: Forms of juvenile deviance; conditions and processes that result in alienation and deviance of youth. Juvenile corrections as institutionalized societal responses.

subject: SOC
title: Principles of Sociological Inquiry
description: (3 Lec, 2 50-min Lab) Basic methods of sociology for production and analysis of data. Foundations for understanding research and for advanced courses in methods and statistics. Restricted to students in the honors program and required for students taking the honors track in sociology.

subject: SOC
title: Survey of Urban Sociology
description: Urban processes and social problems, such as poverty, crime, racial segregation, homelessness, housing policy, urbanization, and neighborhood ethnic diversity. How places shape identity and opportunity. Research methods applied to communities, places, and neighborhoods of Hawaiʻi.

subject: SOC
title: Introduction to Sociology of the Family
description: Family patterns, mate selection, parent-child interaction, socialization of roles, legal sanctions, trends in organization, functions.
prerequisites: SOC 100 or any 200-level SOC course, or WGSS 151 or WGSS 202, or POLS 110; or consent.

subject: SOC
title: East Asian Popular Cultures
description: definition and measurement of socioeconomic status; racial, ethnic and gender inequality; differences in lifestyles and life chances; social mobility.

subject: SOC
title: Survey of Sociology of Work
description: Work from viewpoint of individuals; meaningfulness versus productivity; how work, economics, and the industrial system affect individual goals.

subject: SOC
title: Survey of Social Change
description: Causes, processes, and effects of social change, using single- and multi-cause models in simple and complex industrialized societies.

subject: SOC
title: Women and Social Policy
description: Social and economic policies affecting women in families, education, social services, government, health care, the economy; public policy implementation and development; policy impact on women.
prerequisites: SOC 100 or any 200-level SOC course, or WGSS 151 or any 200- or 300-level WGSS course; or consent.

subject: SOC
title: Survey of Sociological Theory
description: Major theorists and their influences, from Comte to today.

subject: SOC
title: Sociology of Law
description: Law as a political enforcement of the social order; how it is organized and operates; determinants of effectiveness; ways it adapts to and facilitates changing social conditions.

subject: SOC
title: Criminology
description: Concepts used in crime, law enforcement, criminal justice, and corrections. Types of criminal behavior; costs and effects of control.

subject: SOC
title: Drugs and Society
description: Use of mood- and mind-altering drugs in America among adults, youth, and cross-culturally. Illicit drug culture, psychedelics, and perception; social norms and deviant behavior.

subject: SOC
title: Deviance and Social Control
description: Interrelations of deviance, criminology, juvenile delinquency, corrections, social control, sociology of law. Key concepts, theories.

subject: SOC
title: Criminal Justice Organizations
description: Examines major criminal justice organizations-police, courts, and prisons. Using organizational theory, identifies the role of organizational goals, structure, resources, legitimacy, culture, and front-line workers in shaping criminal justice policy and practice.
prerequisites: SOC 100 or a 200-level SOC course, or consent.

subject: SOC
title: Survey of Social Psychology
description: Major principles; social attitudes, theories of conformity and change, person perception and attribution theory, social role, role conflict and role behavior, group structure, and behavior.

subject: SOC
title: Sociology of Happiness in Unsettled Times
description: Combines theoretical, scientific and 'hands-on' learning to explore how happiness and well-being can be cultivated, even in this challenging era of extreme global climate events.

subject: SOC
title: Survey of Sociology of Education
description: Formal education as one aspect of socialization. Emphasis on the American system; including business, military, and religious institutions' role in educating people. Significant time will be spent practicing and developing writing skills.

subject: SOC
title: Survey of Sociology of Aging
description: Aging as a social phenomenon, including social impacts of growing elderly population and emerging social patterns among the elderly. Important theoretical perspectives and cross-national research.

subject: SOC
title: Survey of Medical Sociology
description: Social factors in disease and treatment; illness behavior, roles of patients and healers; nature of healing professions; use of medical services; alternative systems of medical organization.

subject: SOC
title: Chinese Society and Culture
description: Social institutions, family, community, education, stratification, government, economy; impact of modernization and revolution on their contemporary transformation.

subject: SOC
title: Japanese Society and Culture
description: Persistence and change in economy, policy, religion, education, family, and other institutions of modern Japan.

subject: SOC
title: Sociology of Korea
description: Social institutions, family, education, religion, cultural values, social classes, economic development, social movements, gender relations, North-South relations, and unification issues.
prerequisites: SOC 100 or any 200-level SOC course, or consent.

subject: SOC
title: Sociology of Gender
description: Effect of sex and gender roles (both traditional and nontraditional) on attitudes and behavior within the family and educational, economic, and governmental systems. Recommended: at least one WGSS course.
prerequisites: SOC 100 or any 200-level SOC course, or WGSS 151 or any 200- or 300-level WGSS course, or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: SOC
title: Law, Politics and Society
description: Relationships between law, politics, and society will be explored. Emphasis is placed on several dimensions of legality: legal "indeterminacy" and some of the many things that law does for us and to us; law's response to violence; the connections between law and social change; access to the law and its sociological dimensions; how/why law fails and what happens when it does.
prerequisites: SOC 300, or one 300-level WGSS or ES course, or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only.
class_standing: Sophomore standing or higher, or consent.
cross_listed: SUST 394 SOC 400 - Food, Body, and Women: Analysis of Biopolitics - Credits: 3 Explores how food, body, and other "matter of life" are imbedded in biopolitics from the feminist perspectives.

subject: SOC
title: Analysis in Urban Sociology
description: households; unemployment; ethics of revaluing feminized work; labor rights: scope: local and global.
prerequisites: SOC 300, or one 300-level WGSS or ES course; or consent.

subject: SOC
title: Organizations and Society
description: Schools, hospitals, industries, prisons, and government agencies analyzed in terms of self-actualization, alienation, human relations, communication, leadership, organizational conflicts.

subject: SOC
title: Analysis in Social Stratification
description: Approaches to research in social inequality: community studies; historical and cross-cultural analyses of poverty, working class, middle class, power structure, social mobility, etc.

subject: SOC
title: Analysis in Population and Society
description: Advanced topics related to crime, juvenile delinquency, and criminal justice explored sociologically.

subject: SOC
title: Punishment & Society
description: Institutions, organizations, and individuals charged with carrying out punishment in the criminal justice system and beyond.

subject: SOC
title: Economy and Society
description: Study of the dominant trend of economic change and its impact on society; globalization of economic activities and transformation of industrial society to postindustrial one; corporate restructuring and downsizing and their impact on employment and income distribution; gender relations in workplaces; the impact of globalization on the newly industrializing countries.

subject: SOC
title: Technology and Society
description: Nature of technology, social forces that affect its adoption; impact on society; innovation.

subject: SOC
title: Analysis in Law and Social Change
description: Interrelationships between legal orders and other social institutions; use of "law" to change major status relationships, e.g., boss-worker, woman-man, childadult.

subject: SOC
title: Women and Crime
description: Women's relations with the criminal justice system; types of women's offenses; responses to women's crime; women as victims; women as workers in the criminal justice system. Recommended: at least one WGSS course. Gender and race of paid and unpaid work in the formal and informal sectors of the economy; carework in
prerequisites: SOC 300, or WGSS 151 or any 200- or 300level WGSS course; or consent.

subject: SOC
title: Prisons
description: Prisons, jails, and other detention facilities across historical, social, and/or international contexts.

subject: SOC
title: Social Structure and the Individual
description: Effects of social institutions on individuals. Role of socioeconomic status, cultural background, family structure, peer group, schools, and occupational roles in socialization.

subject: SOC
title: Analysis in Gender Violence
description: Historical and structural theories of gender-based violence, including domestic and sexual abuse, prostitution, trafficking, cross-cultural perspectives, social policy and practices. Junior standing or graduate standing only.
prerequisites: SOC 300 or consent. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: SOC

description: Examines the problem of violence, particularly sexual violence, over the life cycle. Offers gendered perspective in activities aimed at prevention and treatment of violence, and cross cultural perspectives.
prerequisites: SOC 300, WGSS 151 or any 200- or 300level WGSS course; or consent.

subject: SOC
title: Analysis in Marriage and the Family
description: Theory and methods of studying social interaction in marriage and the family; examination of marriage, mating, love, and choice. Empirical research emphasizing Hawaiʻi.

subject: SOC
title: Analysis in Medical Sociology
description: Application of sociological theories and concepts to medical social situations and behavior; problems of obtaining data for research.

subject: SOC
title: Sociology of Religion
description: Seminar in research on sociological aspects of religious sectarianism; attention to Hawaiʻi.

subject: SOC
title: Racism and Ethnicity in Hawaiʻi
description: Historical and contemporary social processes involved in race, racism, and inter-ethnic relations in Hawaiʻi. Focus on intersection of Asian and/or Pacific Island cultures with Native Hawaiian culture.
prerequisites: SOC 300 or one ES 300 level course, or consent.

subject: SOC
title: Sociology of the Arts
description: Relation of art to society; role of artist, audience, critic, patron, museum; Western and other societies; attitudes toward new styles.

subject: SOC
title: Analysis in Sports and Society
description: Critical perspectives on sports and society. Topics include power and inequality; mobility, status, and economics; youth development; globalization; gender; and violence in sports and the wider society.
prerequisites: SOC 300 and SOC 321. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: SOC
title: Popular Culture
description: Popular culture as manifested in film, sports, TV, comics, magazines, etc.; relation to sociological theories and studies.

subject: SOC
title: Analysis in Survey Research
description: Survey research design and analysis, including theory selection instrument construction, sampling techniques, data collection, computerized data analysis, and writing up research reports of the findings.

subject: SOC
title: Social Statistics
description: Common statistical procedures emphasizing univariate and bivariate description; some attention to multivariate techniques and statistical inference, within context of research procedures.

subject: SOC
title: Politics of Multiculturalism
description: The development of ethnic relations and political approaches to multiculturalism in two multiethnic nations: Canada and the U.S.
prerequisites: SOC 300 or one 300 level ES course, or consent.

subject: SOC
title: Social Sciences Internship
description: Internship in public, private, or non-profit organizations providing opportunity for practical experience and application of social sciences concepts and theories. Three to six credits per semester;
repeatable: 2 times, up to 12 credits. Consent of instructor.

subject: SOC
title: Topics in Sociology
description: Topics course that explore current issues and try new ideas.

subject: SOC
title: Social Statistics Laboratory
description: Required lab for computer applications for analysis of sociological data.

subject: SOC
title: Topics in Sociology: Student Projects
description: Students create their own study group and solicit an advisor from faculty. Consult department for assistance.

subject: SOC
title: Analysis in Field Research Methods
description: Techniques for collecting and analyzing qualitative data. Participant observation; small groups in natural settings; community studies. Grounded theory; theories of everyday life; reality construction.
gen_ed: DS SOC 491 - Discussion Group Leader-Freshman Seminar - Credits: 6 A unique opportunity for students to gain leadership, teaching, and public speaking skills as a SOC 100 leader for a section of 10 freshmen students while being mentored weekly by a faculty course coordinator.

subject: SOC
title: Statistics for Regression Analysis
description: Dealing with the multiple linear regression and logistic regression models, focusing on modeling, i.e., specification of the explanatory variables to answer different research questions. Emphasis on applications using statistical package programs. SOC 605L is required.

subject: SOC
title: Contemporary Sociological Theory
description: Seminar offers a critical overview of major perspectives and representative works in sociology theory from the 1960s to the present, including intellectual contexts and historical development.

subject: SOC
title: Research Methods and Design
description: Emphasis on theory selection, theory construction, and choice of research strategies.

subject: SOC
title: Seminar in Methods of Content Analysis
description: Content analysis combines quantitative and qualitative methods to analyze text systematically. Covers sampling and case selection; manual and computer-assisted methods of coding and analyzing textual data; writing reports using content analysis data.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: SOC
title: Organizational Analysis
description: Theoretical approaches to organizations; organizational structure and process; organizational pathologies and effectiveness; the organization and its environment.

subject: SOC
title: Survey Research Design and Analysis
description: Survey study designs, survey sampling, questionnaire construction, interviewing, pre-tests, pilot studies, logic of measurement and association, table construction, and elaboration models.

subject: SOC
title: Seminar in Stress and Health
description: Analysis of current theory and empirical research on relationship of stress and health; sociological, psychological, and community psychiatry models and current issues.

subject: SOC
title: Sociology of Mental Health and Illness
description: Examines sociological research and theories about mental health and illness. A key question in medical sociology will be addressed: What is the relationship between society and mental health?

subject: SOC
title: Seminar in Social Stratification
description: Classical theories of social class, contemporary developments; crucial research issues, appropriate methodologies.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: SOC
title: Feminist Criminology
description: Key themes in feminist criminology are explored including focus on masculinities and crime, race and intersectionality, global criminology, and the ways in which the criminal justice system controls women and girls.

subject: SOC
title: Sociology of Sustainability
description: Analyses of sustainability, environmental, and technoscience issues from sociological perspectives. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only Major current theories, history of their development, elaborations of typologies, implications for treatment modalities.

subject: SOC
title: Thesis Research
description: Research for master's thesis.

subject: SOC
title: American Punishment
description: Examines the history of American criminal punishment, from the birth of the penitentiary to the rise of the prisonindustrial complex.

subject: SOC
title: Introduction to Human Population
description: Comparative analysis of quantitative and qualitative aspects of population; factors affecting size, distribution, and composition; impact of population size and composition on society.

subject: SOC
title: Methods of Demographic Analysis
description: Statistical evaluation and analysis of population data; data sources; population growth; composition; standardization of rates; mortality and the life table; nuptiality and fertility; distribution, migration, urbanization; projections and stable population theory.

subject: SOC
title: Teaching Seminar
description: Examines research on teaching, learning, and ethics, as well as practical skills for teaching at the university level. Syllabi and teaching philosophies are developed, which

subject: SOC

description: Research design, data collection, field problems and analysis in the evaluation of social programs. Examples from criminal justice, corrections, drug treatment, mental health, and public health.

subject: SOC
title: Advanced Statistics
description: Dealing with advanced statistical methods beyond multiple linear regression, such as logit, event history analysis, and multi-level analysis. Emphasis is on applications of the techniques to social science research.

subject: SOC
title: Cultural Analysis
description: Contemporary issues in cultural sociology, covering key theoretical perspectives, analytic methods and substantive areas for empirical research.

subject: SOC
title: Seminar in Sociology of Knowledge
description: Sociological theory applied to bases of knowledge in everyday life, professional communities, and the sciences. Research and theory-building activities of sociologists; ethnomethodology; construction of social structure, culture, and consciousness.

subject: SOC
title: Seminar in Current Issues in Sociology
description: Substantive areas that are of current interest and the focus of research, but not addressed in other courses.

subject: SOC
title: Advanced Medical Sociology
description: Application of theoretical paradigms and methodologies to the examination of selected research topics in the field of medical sociology.

subject: SOC
title: Seminar in Aging, Culture, and Health
description: Overview of the major theories, perspectives, and empirical findings relating to aging in various cultural contexts.
prerequisites: SOC 606 (with a minimum grade of B) or consent.
major_restrictions: SOC, PSY, NURS, SW, PH majors only.

subject: SOC
title: Comparative Family and Gender
description: Discusses the major perspectives on family and gender relations and examines related empirical research. Emphasis is on the cross-cultural comparisons across the U.S. and Asia in the context of globalizing economies and cultures.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years

subject: SOC
title: Comparative Study of East Asia
description: Comparative analysis of social organization, social processes, and change of both capitalist and communist countries of East Asia, with each other and other areas of the world.

subject: SOC
title: Social Change—Pacific Islands
description: Analysis of social change; transformation from subsistence societies to commodified, wage-labor societies with participation in world economy.

subject: SOC
title: Modern Japanese Society
description: Social and behavioral studies of Japanese values, social organization, and personality development. Problems of value conflict, political protest, world role, tradition, and social change.

subject: SOC
title: Seminar in Modern Chinese Society
description: Developmental policies, social change, and impact on modern Chinese social institutions. Includes China, Hong Kong, and Taiwan. May include social and demographic change, population, social stratification, gender, and family problems.

subject: SOC

subject: SPED
title: Strategies Across Content Area
description: Strategies for teaching math, science, and social sciences to students of all ages with mild/moderate disabilities; selection of appropriate materials, teaching techniques, curriculum development.

subject: SPED
title: Teaching Skills for Social Competence
description: Issues in social development, self-determination, and social skills competence training for children and youth with disabilities; experience in group social skills training and development of individualized programs.
prerequisites: either SPED 485 or SPED 630, and either SPED 611 or SPED 614; or consent.

subject: SPED
title: Field Experiences in Special Education
description: Students spend a minimum of nine hours per week in settings appropriate to concurrently enrolled courses; supervision provided by participating teacher and college supervisor.

subject: SPED
title: Advanced Practicum
description: Supervised education/community experiences; minimum of 20 hours weekly with special-needs population (MR, LD, SED, SMH); pre-school through postsecondary settings (public/private). Related seminar or equivalent and completion of SPED core required. SPED 633 - Motor Development/Intervention for Students with Severe Disabilities - Credits: 3

subject: SPED
title: Internship
description: Supervised education/community experiences with special needs populations, preschool through postsecondary settings.

subject: SPED
title: Clinical Practice Special Projects
description: Development and implementation of a field-based research professional development project under the direction of the student's advisor; limited to students enrolled in the interdisciplinary program.

subject: SPED
title: Early Intervention for Special Populations
description: Issues important to early childhood special education. Early screening and assessment, working with families, curriculum options/models, program evaluation.

subject: SPED
title: Language/Communication InterventionCommunication Disorders/Autism
description: Current research in normal language learning, assessment, planning, implementation, evaluation of language delayed/disordered children, including children with autism. Procedures for attaining language/functional communication objectives using ongoing routines/activities.

subject: SPED
title: Inclusive Physical Activity
description: Advanced knowledge on issues of socio-cultural, learning styles, diversity, and exceptionalities, and how these influence instruction, engagement, and behavior in physical activity settings across the lifespan.
cross_listed: KRS 634 SPED 635 - Procedures for Early Childhood Special Education - Credits: 3 Assessment and intervention strategies to promote behavior change with young children with disabilities. Promoting child skill gains and integrated programming options.

subject: SPED
title: Topics and Issues in Reading Difficulties
description: Introduction of foundational knowledge of language and literacy development. Current topics and issues related to literacy development in students with Dyslexia and related reading difficulties.

subject: SPED
title: Fundamentals of Language and Literacy
description: Introduction of basic linguistic structures of written English related to reading, spelling, and writing for beginning or struggling readers. Covers evidence-based practices for teaching literacy to students with Dyslexia and related reading difficulties. SPED 639 - Advanced Fundamentals of Language and Literacy - Credits: 3 Advanced linguistic structures of written English related to reading, spelling, and writing for older students. Covers diagnostic assessment, planning, instruction, and progress monitoring for students with Dyslexia or related reading difficulties.

subject: SPED
title: Seminar on Mild/Moderate Disabilities
description: Study of issues, research, program development in the area of mild/moderate disabilities. SPED 641E - Seminar in Issues in Special Education: developmental disabilities - Credits: 3 SPED 641 - (Alpha) Seminar in Issues in Special Education - Credits: 3 Seminar on issues, trends, research, and program development in the field of special education. (B) current issues and trends; (C) technology; (D) foundations; (E) developmental disabilities; (F) cultural and linguistic diversity; (G) evidence-based practices; (H) single case research. Seminar on issues, trends, research, and program development in the field of special education. (B) current issues and trends; (C) technology; (D) foundations; (E) developmental disabilities; (F) cultural and linguistic diversity; (G) evidence-based practices; (H) single case research.

subject: SPED
title: Seminar on Universal Design for Learning
description: Seminar on issues, trends, research, and program development in the field of special education. (B) current issues and trends; (C) technology; (D) foundations; (E) developmental disabilities; (F) cultural and linguistic diversity; (G) evidence-based practices; (H) single case research.
grade_option: A-F only. SPED 642 - Seminar on Applied Research/Special Education - Credits: 3 Study and development of applied research topics in special education.

subject: SPED
title: Seminar on Severe/Multiple Disabilities
description: Research, program development trends/issues in the area of severe/multiple disabilities (birth through adulthood).
prerequisites: (SPED 631 and SPED 635) with a minimum grade of B- or better, or consent.
major_restrictions: College of Education majors only. SPED 645 - Seminar in Early Childhood Special Education - Credits: 3 Study of issues/trends. Research and program development in early childhood special education.

subject: SPED
title: Leadership in Special Education
description: Seminar on topics related to leadership in the field of special education, including issues for teacher leaders, administrators, and teacher preparation personnel.

subject: SPED
title: Transition/Supported Employment
description: Transition planning for youth with disabilities in preparation for employment as adults in private and public sector businesses. change procedures will also be introduced.
class_standing: Graduate students only. SPED 671 - Advanced Principles of Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) - Credits: 3 Focuses on the definitions and characteristics of applied behavior analysis and the concepts, principles, and processes. Concepts related to ethics and behavior

subject: SPED
title: Thesis Research
description: Research for master's thesis.

subject: SPED
title: Ethics and Professional Conduct
description: Focuses on content on the Behavior Analysis Certification Board's code for ethical conduct as well as advanced concepts, principles, and processes of applied behavior analysis.

subject: SPED
title: Applied Behavior Analysis Fieldwork
description: Field experience in applied behavior analysis supervised by a Board Certified Behavior Analyst (BCBA). The format is a combination of fieldwork and seminar meetings.

subject: SPED
title: Research Practicum in Special Education
description: Directed research experience to demonstrate mastery of research skills and techniques through developing and writing research proposals.
prerequisites: SPED 642 or consent, or departmental approval.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: SPED
title: Plan B Master's Project
description: Independent study for students working on a Plan B master's project.

subject: SPED
title: Directed Reading/Research
description: Individual reading/research.
prerequisites: consent of instructor and department chair.

subject: SPED
title: Seminar in Exceptionalities
description: Current and historical topics, issues, and trends in the field of exceptionalities.

subject: SPED
title: Doctoral Internship
description: Supervised internship (minimum 18 hours per week) in program development and administration or research in the student's area of emphasis.

subject: SPED
title: Professional Seminar in Exceptionalities
description: Professional norms, duties, and expectations of leaders in exceptionalities; transitioning from practitioner to researcher and leadership roles; familiarity with current research and trends in the field of exceptionalities.

subject: SPED
title: Single-Case Experimental Design
description: Advanced single-case experimental design; examines the logic of internal and external validity of small "N" design and its functionalist foundations.

subject: SPED
title: Special Topics in Exceptionalities
description: Critical examination and discussion of historical or current topic in early intervention, special education, and/or developmental disabilities.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: SPED
title: Grant Development/Procurement
description: Overview of grant development and procurement processes related to special education personnel preparation and research and demonstration programs. Students design a grant proposal related to a particular problem or need within their employment settings. Surgery

subject: SURG
title: Surgery Longitudinal Clerkship
description: A clinically based, year-long, introductory in general surgery and selected subspecialties. Outpatientconducted one day per week for six months in a community-based, ambulatory care facility. Inpatientconducted for four weeks in an acute-care hospital facility.

subject: SURG
title: Emergency Medical Care
description: Clinical experiences in management of medical, surgical, and psychiatric problems requiring urgent care.

subject: SURG
title: (Alpha) Electives in Surgery
description: Advanced clinical experience in: (B) urology; (C) ophthalmology; (D) otolaryngology; (E) plastic surgery; (F) neurosurgery; (G) orthopaedics; (H) anesthesia; (I) surgical intensive care; (J) Sub-I-general surgery; (K) SubI-pediatric surgery; (M) diagnostic radiology; (N) radiation oncology; (O) EM Sub-I; (P) extramural elec in surg; (Q) Sub-I- cardiovascular; (R) surgical research; (S) surgical anatomy; (T) preceptorship in Asia; (U) biomedical technology design; (X) transplant surgery; (Y) bedside ultrasounds; (Z) laparoscopic skills.

prerequisites: SURG 532 for all except (M), (U), and (Y); and consent for (R) and (S); admission into JABSOM for (U) and (Y).

subject: SUST
title: Dynamic Earth
description: The natural physical environment; the landscape; rocks and minerals, rivers and oceans; volcanism, earthquakes, and other processes inside the Earth; effects of human use of the Earth and its resources. Field trip.

subject: SUST
title: The Natural Environment
description: Introduction to physical geography including weather, climate, vegetation, soils, geology, and landforms. Environmental issues and natural hazards.

subject: SUST
title: The Natural Environment Lab
description: A survey of field and laboratory methods commonly used by physical geographers.

subject: SUST
title: Biology & Society Laboratory
description: (1 3-hr Lab) Explores connections between biological principles and everyday life with a focus on the environment. Topics include environmental health and sustainability with examples from Hawaiʻi. Not a BIOL major elective.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Spring only

subject: SUST
title: Sustainable Cities
description: How do we plan cities to meet long-term social, economic, and environmental needs? Students will learn about sustainability as it relates to issues like energy, transportation, housing, land, water, and food. Emphasis on writing instruction.

subject: SUST
title: Sustainability in a Changing World
description: Environmentally sustainable and non-sustainable practices, and the impacts of climate change, on the development and spread of human societies from prehistory to the 1500s in Asia, Africa, Europe, the Americas, and Hawaiʻi/Oceania. Active learning environment.

subject: SUST
title: Humans and the Environment
description: Introduction to a variety of quantitative and qualitative approaches and methodologies to describe and assess key components to the environment.
prerequisites: SUST 112 (or concurrent) or GES 102 (or concurrent) or OCN 102 (or concurrent).

subject: SUST
title: Chemistry in a Sustainable World
description: Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only

subject: SUST
title: Sustainable Food and Energy Field Course
description: Examines the nexus of food, energy, and water in Hawaiʻi for sustainable development.

subject: SUST
title: Global Environmental History
description: Explores the influence of nature-climate, topography, plants, animals, and microorganisms-on human history and the way people, in turn, have influenced the natural world around them.

subject: SUST
title: Religion and the Environment
description: Examines the roles contemporary religious groups play in ground movements for sustainability. Introduces students to key scholars, religious leaders, and activists who are implementing sustainable solutions to pressing environmental problems.

subject: SUST
title: Climate Change
description: Introduction to the science of climate change focusing on Earth's past, present and future, specifically, the causes, consequences, and record of past changes; future projections, mitigation, and adaptation. Appropriate for both science and non-science majors.
prerequisites: ERTH 101 or ERTH 103 or ERTH 104 or ERTH 106 or ERTH 111 or ERTH 130 or ATMO 101 or GES 102, or consent.

subject: SUST
title: Historical Ecology of Hawaiʻi
description: The Hawaiian socio-ecosystem is the product of centuries of human land use and resource exploitation. Explores the events and processes that have shaped the islands' ecology and future sustainability.

subject: SUST
title: Introduction to Environmental Science
description: Analysis of our environment with emphasis on understanding relationships and interactions of physical, biological, technological, and political components using scientific methods of inquiry. Food supply and safety, water quality, pollution control, biodiversity, environmental policy. Open to nonmajors.

subject: SUST
title: Agriculture, Environment, and Society
description: Relationship of plants, soils, and the environment, and how they relate to cultural practices and society in agroecosystems with an emphasis on Hawaiʻi as a model system.

subject: SUST
title: Hawaiian Perspectives in Ahupuaʻa
description: Examination of the ahupuaʻa system as it was conceptualized by the ancient Hawaiians, and exploration of its relevance in modern society; an introductory class to the mālama ʻāina track designed to build critical writing skills.

subject: SUST
title: Agricultural and Resource Economics
description: Introduction to basic economics concepts, including demand, supply, exchange, market price and market failure. Economic evaluation and policy for the uses of various natural resource endowments, especially in production agriculture, is included.

subject: SUST
title: Topics in Sustainability
description: Emphasizes understanding of interconnectivity of human and natural systems, and critical thinking towards proposed solutions.

subject: SUST
title: Organic Food Crop Production
description: Combined lecture/lab on the theory and practice of certified organic food production. Field visits to organic farms/markets included. Open to nonmajors. Frequency of Course (when offered): Fall only
repeatable: Repeatable on different topics up to six credit hours.

subject: SUST
title: Hawaiians
description: The social system, land stewardship, and governance of Native Hawaiians in a Pacific context. Transformation of the Hawaiian system by a capitalist economy. Native Hawaiian regeneration and quest for sovereign governance as an Oceanic society.

subject: SUST
title: Scientific Principles of Sustainability
description: Introduction to the scientific principles of sustainability, including the ecology of managed and natural ecosystems, global change biology, ecological principles of natural resource management, renewable energy technologies, and the environmental impacts of humans.

subject: SUST
title: Introduction to Environmental Humanities
description: Introduction to the environmental humanities, which rests at the intersection of the arts, humanities, and environmental studies. SUST 304 - Global and Local Perspectives on Severe Weather - Credits: 3 Dynamics and structure of the main types of severe weather (tropical cyclones, supercell storms, tornadoes, flash floods, hailstorms, fog, etc.); future changes due to climate change; risk assessment; severe weather in Hawaiʻi.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Alt years: Spring
major_restrictions: ATMO, GES, GG, GEO, GES, NREM, or OCN students only.
class_standing: Junior standing or higher, or consent.

subject: SUST
title: Environmental Issues
description: Global environmental problems in historical perspective; physical, biological, sociocultural views.
prerequisites: one of BIOL 101 , BIOL 123 , or GEO 101 ; or consent.

subject: SUST
title: Conservation Ethics
description: Introduction to and discussion of ethical issues associated with biodiversity, ecology, and conservation biology.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: SUST
title: Natural Resources Management
description: Biological and physical science aspects of natural resource management at local, national, and global scales. Topics covered include resource management of soil, water, forests, wetlands, coasts and wildlife.

subject: SUST
title: Global Environmental Issues
prerequisites: NREM 210/PEPS 210/SUST 210 or NREM 251/SOCS 251/TPSS 251 or (BIOL 101 or higher) or GEO 101 or (ERTH 101 or higher); and NREM 220/SUST 220 or one ECON course or two DS courses.

subject: SUST
title: Plant Conservation Biology
description: Introduction to the concepts and principles of plant conservation biology and to plant conservation-inpractice in Hawaiʻi and elsewhere.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: SUST
title: Plant Conservation Biology Lab
description: Use and abuse of natural resources and humanity's progress toward developing a sustainable relationship with its supporting environment.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: SUST
title: Water and Society
description: Interaction of people with water at household, community, regional, national, and international scales, from cultural, political, economic, and biophysical perspectives.
class_standing: sophomore standing or higher, or consent.

subject: SUST
title: Environmental Ethics
description: Application of traditional moral theories to environmental issues. Development and evaluation of specific environmental ethical theories. Application of ethical theories to environmental decision-making. Introduction to approaches, methods, and analyses used in the study and practice of plant conservation, with an emphasis on experimental design and problemsolving. Includes both laboratory and field components. SUST 318 - Race, Indigeneity, and Environmental Justice - Credits: 3 Introduction to environmental justice, explores the premise that all people have a right to a life-affirming environment. Will examine environmental racism, and the geographical dimensions of race and indigeneity. endangered species to pollution to climate change. Interaction of population, development, and environment in global governance.
prerequisites: one DS or DH course, or consent.
grade_option: A-F only. Frequency of Course (when offered): Once a year

subject: SUST
title: Environment and Agriculture
description: Overview of environmental issues related to agriculture, specifically pest management issues, and options for environmentally responsible management and amelioration of these impacts. Ethical issues on impact of agriculture on the environment will be discussed.

subject: SUST
title: Hawaiʻi and the Pacific
description: Hawaiʻi as part of the Pacific community: selected historical and contemporary problems of Pacific areas; cultural and economic imperialism, land alienation, and the impact of development on Pacific peoples.

subject: SUST
title: Globalization and Environment

subject: SUST

subject: SUST
title: Culture and Environment

subject: SUST
title: Economic Change and Hawaiʻi's People

repeatable: Each alpha is repeatable five times.

repeatable: Each alpha is repeatable five times.

Students entering public accounting should prepare to become a Certified Public Accountant (CPA). Many students who graduate with an accounting degree are employed by national or international CPA firms, or by local CPA accounting firms. Notes: Student ID:__________________________ Student Name:_______________________ Advisor Name:_______________________ Catalog: 2025-2026 UH Mānoa Catalog Program: Counseling, School Counseling concentration, MEd Minimum Credits Required:__________________ Counseling, School Counseling concentration, MEd The University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa’s School Counseling program provides graduate-level training designed to prepare students for a fulfilling career as a professional school counselor. This is a three-year academic program leading to a Master of Education (MEd) in Counseling. The program emphasizes a holistic approach to school counseling, focusing on the academic, career, and social-emotional development of students. Through rigorous coursework, supervised field experiences, and a culminating project, students develop the knowledge, skills, and dispositions necessary to become effective, ethical, and culturally responsive school counselors. Admission Requirements In addition to the application form required by Graduate Division, prospective students must submit: 1. Personal Statement: A well-written personal statement is required as part of the application. This statement should detail the applicant’s interest in school counseling, describe any relevant experiences, and outline their professional goals in the field. 2. Letters of Recommendation: Three professional letters of recommendation are required. These should be from individuals who can speak to the applicant’s academic abilities, professional potential, and personal qualities relevant to success in school counseling. 3. Curriculum Vitae or Resume Applicants must submit a current resume or curriculum vitae that highlights their educational background, work experience, and any relevant volunteer or extracurricular activities. 4. Transcript(s) of all prior undergraduate and graduate course work to Graduate Division. 5. For non-native speakers of English, a minimum TOEFL score of 600/100 or a minimum IELTS score of 6.0 (overall band test results) unless waived in accordance with Graduate Division guidelines. Requirements Core Courses Course Name EDEP 634 - Human Development Across the Lifespan OR

subject: EDEP
title: Qualitative Research Methods in Education
description: 3 Credits: 3 Credits: 3 Credits: 3 Credits: 3 Term Taken Grade Gen Ed Credits: 3 Credits: 3 Credits: 3 Plan A (Thesis) Students whose objective is doctoral study are recommended to define a Plan A program of study at the master’s level. Plan A candidates must take at least 6 credits of thesis research (EDEP 700). At the discretion of the thesis chair, up to five credits of EDEP 699, previously completed, may be substituted for five of the six EDEP 700 credits. Graduate Division requires that a minimum of 12 credits must be earned in courses numbered 600-798, in addition to six credits of directed reading (EDEP 699) and thesis research (EDEP 700). The development of a thesis proposal is concurrent with the selection of a thesis chair and committee. The proposal includes a literature review that contextualizes the research question(s) within existing research and theory. The proposal also includes a description of the proposed research design and methods, including how the data will be analyzed. Students work with their thesis chair to develop their proposal. After the thesis proposal is defended and approved, and Human Studies Program approval is received, Master’s Form II is submitted to Graduate Division, and the student may enroll in thesis research (EDEP 700) at the beginning of the next academic semester. Students must register for at least one EDEP 700 credit during the semester in which they graduate and apply for graduation by the appropriate deadline. It is the responsibility of the student to keep all members of the thesis committee informed of the scope, plan, and progress of the thesis research. Copies of the completed thesis that are approved by the thesis chair must be submitted to committee members at least two weeks prior to the date of the final oral examination by the student committee. Upon successful defense of the thesis and subsequent completion of revisions, Master’s Form III is submitted to Graduate Division. When the final edited document is submitted to Graduate Division, Form IV should be submitted at the same time. Plan B (Nonthesis) The culminating requirement is a Plan B project/paper, an original educational inquiry resulting in a product that informs educational practice. The development of a Plan B project is concurrent with the selection of a Plan B advisor. Students develop a 8-10 page proposal outlining their projects that are then approved by both their Plan B advisor and a second reader that the student and advisor selects. Not more than 6 credits in directed reading/research (EDEP 699) may be applied to meet degree requirements. A presentation of the Plan B project/ paper is required during the final semester. If candidates are not enrolled in other courses, they must be enrolled in at least one credit of EDEP 699 - Directed Reading and/or Research. Students must apply for graduation when registering for their final semester of study. subject: ECON
title: Strategic Behavior and Experimental Economics
description: 3 Credits: 3 Credits: 3 Credits: 3 Term Taken Grade Gen Ed Notes: Students choosing Methods/Theory as a field take 6 additional credits as electives. Students who do not choose Methods/Theory as a field take 9 additional credits as electives. Some field courses are offered annually, others less frequently. Students may petition to substitute a field in another discipline or another field in economics for one of the five fields listed above. The third-year research paper is a transition experience between PhD course work and independent dissertation research. A successful paper will demonstrate a depth of knowledge within an area of specialization and the ability to implement the research methods needed for dissertation-level research. The deadline for submitting your paper is May 1 of your third year of graduate studies and the deadline for resubmission is August 1. Students who do not meet these deadlines will be placed on academic probation in the fall semester of their fourth year of studies. The comprehensive examination—of which the written qualifying examinations are a part—will include a broad probing of the candidate’s general economic knowledge. The oral part of the PhD comprehensive examination will be administered jointly with the defense of the dissertation proposal, before a dissertation committee chosen by the student and approved by the graduate chair and Graduate Division. A student who fails the comprehensive examination may repeat it once. A student who fails a second time is dismissed from the program. Students who pass the oral exam are advanced to candidacy for the PhD. The final examination, which is oral, covers the candidate’s defense of the final dissertation draft. It is administered orally and is open to the public. Candidates failing the final examination may be allowed to repeat it once upon petition approved by the graduate faculty concerned and the dean of Graduate Division. Those failing it twice are dismissed from the program. Finally, a dissertation accepted by the dissertation committee is submitted to Graduate Division. The final dissertation must also conform to UH Mānoa standards in content and format. Notes: Student ID:__________________________ Student Name:_______________________ Advisor Name:_______________________ Catalog: 2025-2026 UH Mānoa Catalog Program: Education - Educational Administration Concentration, PhD Minimum Credits Required:__________________ Education - Educational Administration Concentration, PhD Educational Administration provides advanced graduate study for highly qualified, experienced educational leaders in elementary, secondary, and higher education settings. The number of required credit hours depends on qualifications. Students complete courses required by the college and the program, electives specific to a chosen area of emphasis, a field project/internship or an apprenticeship in college teaching, and a dissertation. Areas of emphasis within the program include management and leadership, organizational theory, policy and governance, organizational socialization, and research methods. The program also includes courses in K-12 or higher education and courses taken outside the department. Notes: Student ID:__________________________ Student Name:_______________________ Advisor Name:_______________________ Catalog: 2025-2026 UH Mānoa Catalog Program: Education - Educational Foundations Concentration, PhD Minimum Credits Required:__________________ Education - Educational Foundations Concentration, PhD Educational Foundations prepares educational professionals with an understanding of the historical, philosophical, cultural, social, and political contexts of education so that they can make informed and wise decisions about educational problems and policy issues. Graduates are expected to exert leadership in the field of education and deal with those aspects and problems in society that need to be taken into account in advancing educational thought, policy development, and practice, especially where these concern the social role of the school and other educational agencies. The number of required credit hours depends on qualifications. Students complete courses required by the college and the program, electives specific to a chosen area of emphasis, a field project/internship or an apprenticeship in college teaching, and a dissertation. Courses in this concentration address an area of emphasis in history, philosophy, comparative or social foundations of education, and/or courses outside the department. Notes: Student ID:__________________________ Student Name:_______________________ Advisor Name:_______________________ Catalog: 2025-2026 UH Mānoa Catalog Program: Education - Educational Policy Studies Concentration, PhD Minimum Credits Required:__________________ Education - Educational Policy Studies Concentration, PhD Educational Policy Studies consists of a multidisciplinary program of study and research concerned with identifying and ameliorating significant educational problems. It draws upon concepts and research methods from a variety of fields (including the social sciences, history, law, and philosophy) in defining problems and formulating solutions. This concentration prepares professionals from diverse backgrounds for effective informed engagement in this process. At the same time, it prepares such persons to pursue research and service agendas geared toward lifting policy analysis, discourse, and action to new levels. The number of required credit hours depends on qualifications. Students complete courses required by the college and the program, electives specific to a chosen area of emphasis, a field project/internship or an apprenticeship in college teaching, and a dissertation. Notes: Student ID:__________________________ Student Name:_______________________ Advisor Name:_______________________ Catalog: 2025-2026 UH Mānoa Catalog Program: Education - Exceptionalities Concentration, PhD Minimum Credits Required:__________________ Education - Exceptionalities Concentration, PhD The program in Exceptionalities prepares professionals to work as leaders in the education and support of individuals who have unique needs, often due to disabilities. The field is broad, addressing life-span concerns and involving such services as special education, advocacy, family support, community services, and vocational training and support. Graduates of the program are expected to assume leadership roles addressing local, regional, national, and international issues related to research and higher education and/or program development and evaluation. The number of required credit hours depends on qualifications. Students complete courses required by the college and the program, electives specific to a chosen area of emphasis, a field project/internship or an apprenticeship in college teaching, and a dissertation. Notes: Student ID:__________________________ Student Name:_______________________ Advisor Name:_______________________ Catalog: 2025-2026 UH Mānoa Catalog Program: Education - Global and International Education Concentration, PhD Minimum Credits Required:__________________ Education - Global and International Education Concentration, PhD Global and International Education aims to prepare in-service educators, educational researchers, and analysts for leadership positions in educational and research settings. Strongly concerned with theory generation, application, and analysis, this concentration draws significantly upon concepts, theories, and research methods from a variety of fields—including the social sciences, history, law, and philosophy—in understanding, defining, analyzing, and researching educational issues and problems around the world and in global perspective. Given the spread of globalization around the world, the growth of internationalization, and evident student mobility from country to country, the study of education and educational systems at all levels in global and international dimensions is increasingly important. The number of required credit hours depends on qualifications. Students complete courses required by the college and the program, electives specific to a chosen area of emphasis, a field project/internship or an apprenticeship in college teaching, and a dissertation.
